OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 46-year-old male patient, with a known history of
 
 human immunodeficiency virus
 
 and
 
 hepatitis C virus
 
 coinfection, underwent a computed tomography (CT) to assess liver lesions detected by US, which were later identified as haemangiomas. On physical examination, hypoplastic fingernails were found.
 Imaging Findings: Incidentally, the CT revealed the presence of bilateral osseous processes projecting from the posterior aspect of the iliac bones (Figures 1a and 1b). Anteroposterior and lateral knee radiographs showed bilateral patellar hypoplasia associated with luxation (Figure 2a), while elbow radiographs demonstrated bilateral radial head hypoplasia with radio-humeral dislocation and abnormal radial curvature (Figure 2b).
PostDescription:

 Imaging Findings: Incidentally, the CT revealed the presence of bilateral osseous processes projecting from the posterior aspect of the iliac bones. Anteroposterior and lateral knee radiographs showed bilateral patellar hypoplasia associated with luxation, while elbow radiographs demonstrated bilateral radial head hypoplasia with radio-humeral dislocation and abnormal radial curvature.

model_answer_raw:

A) Key findings:  
- Adult male patient with known HIV and hepatitis C coinfection.  
- Hypoplastic fingernails on physical examination.  
- Bilateral osseous processes projecting from the posterior iliac bones.  
- Bilateral patellar hypoplasia with luxation (knees).  
- Bilateral radial head hypoplasia with radio-humeral dislocation and abnormal radial curvature (elbows).  

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  

1) Nail–patella syndrome (NPS):  
   Pros: Classic triad includes nail hypoplasia/dystrophy, patellar hypoplasia or aplasia, and elbow abnormalities such as radial head subluxation/dislocation and iliac spurs (posterior iliac processes). Bilateral findings align well.  
   Cons: None significant; fits perfectly with clinical and imaging findings.  

2) Coffin–Siris syndrome:  
   Pros: Nail hypoplasia/absence can be a feature.  
   Cons: Primarily presents with developmental delay, absent fifth fingernail or distal phalanx, and often includes coarsened facial features and growth retardation; limb skeletal anomalies are not typically patellar hypoplasia or iliac spurs. Less likely given specifics.  

3) Meier–Gorlin syndrome:  
   Pros: Includes radial ray abnormalities and patellar anomalies.  
   Cons: Usually presents with growth retardation, micrognathia, and microtia; radiologic features often include absent or hypoplastic patella and absent radial bones, but iliac spurs and elbow dislocations are not characteristic. Less consistent with findings.  

4) Coxopodopatellar syndrome:  
   Pros: Involves patellar aplasia/hypoplasia and knee anomalies.  
   Cons: Rare; typically includes craniofacial anomalies, spinal defects, and foot abnormalities (coccygeal and podalic anomalies). Elbow abnormalities may be present but nail changes and iliac spurs are not classic features. Less specific.  

5) Genitopatellar syndrome:  
   Pros: Patellar hypoplasia and renal/genitourinary anomalies.  
   Cons: Nail hypoplasia is not a hallmark; limb anomalies differ. Also includes absent or hypoplastic patella, but elbow and iliac bone findings are not typical.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Nail–patella syndrome (best fit for the triad of nail hypoplasia, patellar hypoplasia/luxation, and iliac spurs).  
2) Coxopodopatellar syndrome (close but lacks nail and iliac findings).  
3) Genitopatellar syndrome (patellar changes, but lacks other characteristic features).  
4) Meier–Gorlin syndrome (some overlap, but less specific for this triad).  
5) Coffin–Siris syndrome (least compatible with the described osteoarticular findings).  

<final>Nail–patella syndrome</final><|return|>
options: Nail–patella syndrome | Coffin–Siris syndrome | Meier–Gorlin syndrome | Coxopodopatellar syndrome | Genitopatellar syndrome

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 81-year-old male with a history of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation presents to the emergency department with a one-week onset of orthopnoea and exertional dyspnoea. Laboratory tests demonstrate elevated troponins, the electrocardiogram reveals a third-degree atrioventricular block, and the echocardiogram shows right ventricular thickening and pericardial effusion.
 Imaging Findings: A PET-CT scan (Figure 1) was carried out revealing a large hypermetabolic myocardial tissue. It extended through the right chambers and around the large vessels, sparing the left ventricle. Additionally, a hypermetabolic nodular image was identified at the left lung base.
 
 Therefore, a contrast-enhanced chest CT scan was performed (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c and 2d). It confirmed the presence of an infiltrative hypodense mass in the myocardium. It involved the right atrium, right ventricle, interatrial septum, and atrioventricular groove. The mass caused mural thickening of the right cavities. A cuff encircled the right coronary artery along its course. A moderate pericardial effusion was also observed. Finally, the CT scan confirmed the solid nodule in the posterior segment of the left lower lobe.
 
 A CT-guided percutaneous biopsy of the lung nodule was made for histological examination.

- 81-year-old male with paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, orthopnoea, exertional dyspnoea.  
- Elevated troponins, third-degree AV block, right ventricular thickening, and pericardial effusion on echocardiogram.  
- PET-CT: large hypermetabolic myocardial tissue involving right chambers and surrounding large vessels, sparing the LV, plus hypermetabolic lung nodule.  
- CT: infiltrative hypodense mass infiltrating myocardium of right atrium, right ventricle, interatrial septum, atrioventricular groove; mural thickening of right chambers, cuff around right coronary artery, moderate pericardial effusion.  
- Lung nodule confirmed, biopsy scheduled.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  
1) Angiosarcoma  
   - Pros: Primary cardiac angiosarcoma typically involves right atrium, may invade coronary arteries, cause pericardial effusion, presents with right atrial mass and infiltration of right heart.  
   - Cons: Usually highly aggressive and can present with similar symptoms; but sparing of LV is typical for right-sided involvement. However, angiosarcomas are less common than lymphoma in this presentation.  

2) Ig-G4 related disease  
   - Pros: Can cause pericardial and cardiac involvement and lung nodules.  
   - Cons: Typically involves diffuse fibrotic changes, not focal infiltrative mass, usually less metabolically active on PET-CT. Not classic for myocardial infiltrative mass sparing LV.  

3) Primary cardiac lymphoma  
   - Pros: Can present with right-sided cardiac involvement, large infiltrative mass, right coronary artery cuffing, pericardial effusion, elevated troponins and AV block. PET-CT hypermetabolic mass and lung nodule could suggest systemic involvement or metastatic spread of lymphoma.  
   - Cons: Rare, but in older immunocompetent patients can occur. Pathognomonic involvement of right heart structures matches typical distribution.  

4) Erdheim–Chester disease  
   - Pros: Multifocal infiltrative disease, can involve heart, pericardium, large structures.  
   - Cons: Typically involves left heart more commonly, especially pericardial infiltration and right atrial involvement is less classic; PET-CT typically shows symmetrical bone involvement; lung nodules can occur but lung biopsy needed.  

5) Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma  
   - Pros: Sarcomas can infiltrate myocardium and present right-sided mass.  
   - Cons: Rare cardiac sarcomas generally present as discrete masses with necrotic/heterogeneous appearance, less likely with widespread infiltration and pericardial effusion sparingly. No mention of necrosis or hemorrhage; lymphoma more likely.  

C) Ranking and tie-breaking:  
- Most specific and matching findings: Primary cardiac lymphoma (right-sided infiltrative, metabolic activity, pericardial effusion, AV block, right coronary artery cuff).  
- Second: Angiosarcoma, similar right atrial localization but less metabolic activity described generally, and the involvement of multiple right-sided structures plus coronary artery cuff is more classic for lymphoma.  
- The other diagnoses have less supporting evidence or mismatched typical patterns.  

</analysis><final> Primary cardiac lymphoma </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 37-year-old man presented to our department of surgery with complains of high-grade fever, chills, vomiting and abdominal pain in epigastric region for the last 7 days. Laboratory investigations revealed a raised total leukocyte count (TLC) (of 18,500 cells/μL) with increased absolute neutrophil count (80%), and mildly deranged alkaline phosphate levels (200 IU/L).
 Imaging Findings: Initial abdominal ultrasonography (USG) revealed hepatomegaly with a large left lobe liver abscess (350 cc) and signs of impending rupture. Mild prominence of intrahepatic bile ducts (Figures 1a and 1b) and a few intrahepatic veno-venous collaterals (measuring ~2 mm in diameter) were also noted between the branch of the middle and right hepatic vein (Figures 2a, 2b and 6).
 
 Doppler study showed echogenic content with absent colour flow at the porto-splenic confluence, suggesting thrombosis, along with collateral vessel formation at the porta hepatis, likely indicative of cavernous transformation (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, 4a and 4b).
 
 Contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) scan of the abdomen confirmed the abscess and thrombosis, additionally revealing pinpoint narrowing of the inferior vena cava (IVC) with a small segment thrombus (Figures 5a, 5b, 5c, 5d and 5e). Notably, pancreatic parenchymal varices were observed (involving the head, and body of the pancreas) draining directly into the splenic vein (Figures 3e and 4c).
 
 Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) was also performed (Figures 7 and 8), revealing prominent intrahepatic bile ducts in the left lobe of the liver and non-visualisation of the biliary channels distal to the common hepatic duct (CHD) consistent with biliary obstruction.

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
1. Clinical presentation: High-grade fever, chills, vomiting, epigastric abdominal pain (7 days), raised leukocyte count with neutrophilia, and mildly elevated alkaline phosphatase.  
2. Imaging: Large left lobe liver abscess (350 cc) with signs of impending rupture.  
3. Imaging: Thrombosis at the porto-splenic confluence with cavernous transformation of the portal vein.  
4. Imaging: Pinpoint narrowing of the IVC with a small segment of thrombus.  
5. Imaging: Intrahepatic ductal dilatation limited to the left lobe; non-visualization distal to the CHD, suggesting biliary obstruction.  
6. Imaging: Presence of pancreatic parenchymal varices involving head and body, draining into the splenic vein.  
7. Imaging: Intrahepatic veno-venous collaterals near the middle and right hepatic veins.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1. Liver cirrhosis with portal hypertension and varices formation  
   - Pros: Portal vein thrombosis/cavernous transformation, varices formation.  
   - Cons: Patient is only 37 years old with no mention of chronic liver disease signs; presence of focal large abscess rather than diffuse disease; hepatic dysfunction not highlighted; no morphological evidence of cirrhotic changes reported.  
2. Arteriovenous malformation (AVM)  
   - Pros: Vascular lesions can cause shunting and varices.  
   - Cons: No imaging evidence of abnormal arteriovenous shunts; location and pattern of thrombosis not typical of AVM; presence of abscess and thrombosis more suggestive of infection-related process.  
3. Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess  
   - Pros: Liver abscess with portal vein thrombosis (pylephlebitis) matches findings; cavernous transformation and varices are sequelae of chronic portal thrombosis; pancreatic parenchymal varices due to collateral flow through splenic vein; biliary obstruction due to involvement at CHD level.  
   - Cons: Pancreatic parenchymal varices are rare but can be secondary to portal hypertension from thrombosis; no contradictory findings.  
4. Cystic lesions of pancreas  
   - Pros: Potential to cause varices via compression.  
   - Cons: No cystic lesion described; presentation and imaging centered on liver abscess and vascular thrombosis.  
5. Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasms (IPMN)  
   - Pros: Can cause biliary obstruction.  
   - Cons: No description of pancreatic cystic dilatation or communication with ducts; presence of liver abscess and thrombotic complications less typical.  

C) Ranking and tie-breaking:  
1. Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess (best match with clinical and imaging features)  
2. Liver cirrhosis with portal hypertension and varices formation (less likely given age and lack of cirrhosis signs)  
3. Cystic lesions of pancreas (unlikely)  
4. Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasms (unlikely)  
5. Arteriovenous malformation (least likely)  

Final choice: Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess.  
</analysis><final> Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess </final>
<|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 31-year-old male presented at the urologist with scrotal swelling for 4 weeks. He had no relevant history. Clinically there was a voluminous solid swelling of the right hemiscrotum and retraction of the penis. Blood results showed extremely high alpha-fetoprotein levels >53.000 µg/L.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound showed a large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum with solid and cystic/necrotic components and vascularisation (Figures 1, 2 and 3). There was no visualisation of normal testicular parenchyma or epididymis on the right. There was a normal left testis and epididymis (not shown).
 
 CT showed, besides the large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum (Figure 4), no metastasis or lymphadenopathies.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 31-year-old male presented at the urologist with scrotal swelling for 4 weeks. He had no relevant history. Clinically there was a voluminous solid swelling of the right hemiscrotum and retraction of the penis. Blood results showed extremely high alpha-fetoprotein levels >53.000 µg/L.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound showed a large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum with solid and cystic/necrotic components and vascularisation. There was no visualisation of normal testicular parenchyma or epididymis on the right. There was a normal left testis and epididymis (not shown). CT showed, besides the large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum, no metastasis or lymphadenopathies.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Mixed germinal cell tumour, Pure yolk sac tumour, Seminoma, Choriocarcinoma, Teratoma, Lymphoma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 31-year-old male with a large solid heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum, with solid and cystic/necrotic components and vascularisation.  
- No normal testicular or epididymal tissue visible on the right side, suggesting replacement or infiltration.  
- Extremely high serum alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) >53,000 µg/L.  
- No metastases or lymphadenopathy on CT.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1. Mixed germinal cell tumour  
   - Pros: Mixed germ cell tumors are common and can contain multiple histologic components, which might explain large heterogeneous mass.  
   - Cons: Usually, AFP is elevated if yolk sac or embryonal carcinoma components are present, but the extremely high AFP level often points to pure yolk sac tumor rather than mixed. No mention of specific heterogeneity suggestive of mixed components.  

2. Pure yolk sac tumour  
   - Pros: Classic for extremely high AFP levels (> 53,000 µg/L). Typically presents as a solid heterogeneous testicular mass with solid and cystic/necrotic areas. High vascularisation typical.  
   - Cons: None evident from the clinical/imaging data.  

3. Seminoma  
   - Pros: Usually solid, homogeneous, but typically does not elevate AFP.  
   - Cons: Patient's extremely high AFP argues against seminoma; seminomas rarely secrete AFP.  

4. Choriocarcinoma  
   - Pros: Rare testicular tumour that can have necrosis and solid components.  
   - Cons: Typically associated with elevated hCG, not AFP; no mention of hCG elevation; very rare.  

5. Teratoma  
   - Pros: Heterogeneous mass with solid and cystic components is characteristic.  
   - Cons: Teratomas typically do not elevate AFP unless there are embryonal carcinoma components; the clinical presentation does not suggest it.  

6. Lymphoma  
   - Pros: Can present as a testicular mass in older males, but less likely at age 31.  
   - Cons: Typically does not produce AFP; normal left testis argues against systemic disease.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1. Pure yolk sac tumour (consistent with markedly elevated AFP, classic imaging)  
2. Mixed germinal cell tumour (possible, but extremely high AFP favors pure yolk sac)  
3. Teratoma (less likely without AFP elevation)  
4. Choriocarcinoma (unlikely without hCG elevation)  
5. Seminoma (AFP rules out)  
6. Lymphoma (age and labs argue against it)  
</analysis>

<final>Pure yolk sac tumour</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old male presented with sudden-onset left-sided weakness and mild slurring of speech lasting two days, accompanied by forgetfulness, particularly difficulty in retaining new information. Neurological examination showed left
 
 hemiparesis in the left upper and lower extremities, with intact cranial nerve functions.
 Imaging Findings: MRI
 
 revealed acute watershed infarcts in the right centrum semiovale (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c and 1d). Sulcal hyperintensity in the right temporal region (ivy sign) was noted (Figure 1d).
 
 CT demonstrated a hypoplastic carotid canal on the left (Figure 2).
 
 CT angiography (CTA) showed left internal carotid artery hypoplasia and focal stenosis of bilateral supraclinoid
 
 internal carotid arteries (
 
 ICAs) (Figure 3). The left
 
 middle
 
 cerebral artery (
 
 MCA) was supplied via the posterior circulation, while the left
 
 anterior cerebral artery (
 
 ACA) was perfused through the
 
 anterior communicating (
 
 ACom) artery. The foetal origin of the left
 
 posterior cerebral artery (
 
 PCA) was identified.
 
 Digital subtraction angiography (
 
 DSA) confirmed these findings, showing no “puff of smoke” appearance (Figures 4a and 4b) and indicating an absence of collateral formation.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old male presented with sudden-onset left-sided weakness and mild slurring of speech lasting two days, accompanied by forgetfulness, particularly difficulty in retaining new information. Neurological examination showed left hemiparesis in the left upper and lower extremities, with intact cranial nerve functions.
 Imaging Findings: MRI revealed acute watershed infarcts in the right centrum semiovale. Sulcal hyperintensity in the right temporal region (ivy sign) was noted. CT demonstrated a hypoplastic carotid canal on the left. CT angiography (CTA) showed left internal carotid artery hypoplasia and focal stenosis of bilateral supraclinoid internal carotid arteries (ICAs). The left middle cerebral artery (MCA) was supplied via the posterior circulation, while the left anterior cerebral artery (ACA) was perfused through the anterior communicating (ACom) artery. The foetal origin of the left posterior cerebral artery (PCA) was identified. Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) confirmed these findings, showing no “puff of smoke” appearance and indicating an absence of collateral formation.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Atherosclerotic ICA stenosis with secondary ischemic events, Primary Moyamoya syndrome with bilateral involvement, Moyamoya disease with ICA hypoplasia, ICA dissection causing ischemia, Vasculitis-induced stenosis of cerebral arteries
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- Acute watershed infarcts in right centrum semiovale (vascular territory is boundary zone, implying hypoperfusion).
- Ivy sign (sulcal hyperintensity) in right temporal region (suggests leptomeningeal collateral flow).
- Hypoplastic left carotid canal on CT (suggests congenital underdevelopment of left ICA).

Clinical History: A 51-year-old female patient, a farmer, presented with two years history of abdominal pain, vomits, diarrhoea, asthenia, weight loss (12%), and arthralgias. Laboratory findings showed

 , increased erythrocyte sedimentation rate, mild leukocytosis, thrombocytosis, C-reactive protein 6 mg/dL
 
 (normal value < 0.5 mg/dL)
 
 and hypoalbuminemia 2.5 g/dL.
 Imaging Findings: Abdominal-pelvic computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed cystadenocarcinoma of the right ovary with
 
 suspicion of lymph node metastases
 
 and the patient was referred to our oncology institute.
 
 The exams were re-assessed and the malignant ovarian tumour was excluded, as the MRI was highly suggestive of cystadenofibroma
 
 (Figure 1).
 
 In addition, the CT demonstrated low-attenuation lymph nodes (fatty infiltration) in the left supraclavicular region, mediastinum, retroperitoneum and mesentery
 
 (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, 2d, 3a, and 3b). Furthermore, prominent jejunal folds were seen on T2-weighted images (Figure 4).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 40-year-old man with lower abdominal pain received a CT abdomen/pelvis in the emergency department to rule out appendicitis. An incidental fat attenuation mass was present in the right ischiorectal fossa. Five years later, the patient presented again with pelvic pain and received a CT pelvis.
 Imaging Findings: The initial CT study identified a right ischiorectal fossa fat attenuation mass displacing the anal canal towards the left without enhancing soft tissue, fluid or calcifications (Figures 1a and 1b). There was a thin capsule surrounding the mass.
 
 When the patient presented again five years later, a CT pelvis showed that the mass had significantly enlarged (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c). It contained two well-defined cystic compartments with homogeneous fluid attenuation (15 HU) and an irregular enhancing soft tissue rim in the posterosuperior compartment.
 
 An MR pelvis done shortly thereafter recapitulated these findings but revealed that the apparently homogeneous central collection on CT contained a mix of fluid and soft tissue, with both thin and thick septations (Figures 3a, 3b, 4a, and 4b). Additionally, the entire mass had a thick, irregular rim of enhancement.
PostDescription:

An incidental fat attenuation mass was present in the right ischiorectal fossa. Five years later, the patient presented again with pelvic pain and received a CT pelvis.
 Imaging Findings: The initial CT study identified a right ischiorectal fossa fat attenuation mass displacing the anal canal towards the left without enhancing soft tissue, fluid or calcifications. There was a thin capsule surrounding the mass. When the patient presented again five years later, a CT pelvis showed that the mass had significantly enlarged. It contained two well-defined cystic compartments with homogeneous fluid attenuation (15 HU) and an irregular enhancing soft tissue rim in the posterosuperior compartment. An MR pelvis done shortly thereafter recapitulated these findings but revealed that the apparently homogeneous central collection on CT contained a mix of fluid and soft tissue, with both thin and thick septations. Additionally, the entire mass had a thick, irregular rim of enhancement.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Extragonadal germ cell tumour, Ischiorectal liposarcoma, Dermoid cyst, Tailgut cyst, Perineal epidermal cyst, Anterior sacral meningocele

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 40-year-old man with lower abdominal/pelvic pain.  
- Initial CT: right ischiorectal fossa fat attenuation mass displacing anal canal, thin capsule, no enhancement, fluid, or calcifications.  
- Five-year follow-up CT: significant enlargement of mass.  
- New features: two well-defined cystic compartments with homogeneous fluid attenuation (15 HU), irregular enhancing soft tissue rim in posterosuperior compartment.  
- MRI recapitulated CT findings, but the central collection on CT had mixed fluid and soft tissue with thin and thick septations; entire mass had thick, irregular rim of enhancement.

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  
1) Extragonadal germ cell tumour  
- Pro: Can occur in pelvis; can grow over time.  
- Con: Typically solid/mixed solid and cystic; not described as fat-density mass. No mention of typical tumor markers or typical histology. Uncommon in ischiorectal fossa. 

2) Ischiorectal liposarcoma  
- Pro: Fat attenuation mass in ischiorectal fossa; liposarcoma can be fatty with solid components and may enlarge.  
- Con: Liposarcoma usually solid, can enhance; presence of cystic compartments, septations, and irregular enhancing rim less typical.  

3) Dermoid cyst  
- Pro: Can have fat attenuation; can contain cystic components and fat.  
- Con: Dermoids often contain fat and calcifications (e.g., teeth, bone); not described here. Also, usually stable, benign lesions and less likely to enlarge dramatically in 5 years.  

4) Tailgut cyst  
- Pro: Located in presacral region; can be cystic and multiloculated, often enhancing septa; can enlarge over time.  
- Cons: Typically presacral rather than ischiorectal fossa; location may be borderline but less likely for exact right ischiorectal fossa.  
- However, tailgut cysts can extend laterally; though more typically midline, they can be lateral.  

5) Perineal epidermal cyst  
- Pro: Fat attenuation; can be cystic.  
- CONS: Typically well-defined, slowly enlarging; less likely to have irregular enhancing soft tissue rim.  

6) Anterior sacral meningocele  
- Pros: Can be cystic and midline presacral; communicates with sacral thecal sac and contains CSF (low attenuation).  
- Cons: Usually not in ischiorectal fossa; no mention of communication or neural tissue; fat attenuation not typical.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Ischiorectal liposarcoma (fat attenuation mass in ischiorectal fossa, enlarging, irregular enhancing rim suggests malignant potential).  
2) Tailgut cyst (cystic, septated, enhancing, location proximity).  
3) Dermoid cyst (fat attenuation, but lack of calcifications).  
4) Perineal epidermal cyst (less likely given enhancement).  
5) Extragonadal germ cell tumour (unlikely top due to lack of solid components and typical markers).  
6) Anterior sacral meningocele (least likely due to location and lack of typical features).

Given the large, fat attenuation mass in a precise location that grew over five years and now shows complex cystic components with thick enhancing rim and septations, an ischiorectal liposarcoma appears most consistent.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old female patient was admitted to our oncology centre with follicular lymphoma. She had a seven-kilogram weight loss over one year, loss of appetite, and fever of unknown origin. There is no significant medical history, and physical examination revealed only cervical lymphadenopathy.
 
 The patient’s blood workup revealed a slight increase in C-reactive protein (1.2 mg/dL) and creatine kinase (151 U/L), with no other abnormal laboratory findings.
 Imaging Findings: Computed tomography detected a large lesion, measuring 7.5 x 5 centimetres, heterogeneous, with fluid densities and lobulated margins in the superior segments of the right hepatic lobe (Figure 1). Due to the
 
 patient’s
 
 clinical context, an ultrasound-guided biopsy was performed, which revealed an unrepresentative fragment of liver tissue, encompassing a fibro-hyalinised nodule.
 
 An abdominal magnetic resonance (MR) was performed to characterise the hepatic lesion further (Figures 2, 3a, 3b, and 4). The MR revealed a heterogeneous, complex cystic lesion with a thin wall, hypointense on T2-weighted images, and hyperintense on T1-weighted images, located within the right liver lobe. The lesion exhibited radiating septa towards the periphery, appearing as low-signal intensity on T2-weighted images and high-signal intensity on T1-weighted images. These septa delineated multiple smaller cysts, hypointense on T1 and hyperintense on T2-weighted images, occupying almost the entire lesion. After contrast administration, the lesion did not exhibit restricted diffusion and no significant enhancement of the soft tissue component.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 17-month-old boy presented with a 3-day history of sternal swelling and pain. No fever or any
 
 other
 
 symptoms. There was no history of trauma. Physical examination revealed a 2 cm swelling in the manubriosternal region. Inflammatory parameters were normal except for
 
 a slightly elevated value for C-reactive protein.
 Imaging Findings: The chest radiograph revealed a presternal soft tissue swelling without calcifications and with no evidence of bony anomalies at the sternum, such as bone erosions or a periosteal reaction (Figure 1).
 
 A CT scan showed a fusiform soft tissue density lesion, centred on the manubriosternal joint, involving both superficial and deep planes of the synchondrosis, with subcutaneous and mediastinal extension, measuring 46 x 27 x 37 mm. No significant irregularity of the bony surfaces at the manubriosternal synchondrosis is visible.
 
 However, a separation between the manubrial and sternal ossification centres is evident
 
 , accompanied by densification of the adjacent subcutaneous tissue (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 Ultrasound revealed a hypoechoic and heterogeneous, dumbbell-shaped lesion centred on the manubriosternal synchondrosis. This lesion crosses the synchondrosis, extending superficially into the subcutaneous tissue and deeply to the depth of the chest wall/anterior mediastinum. Colour Doppler ultrasound demonstrated no internal vascularisation. Additionally, there is a thickening of the soft tissues surrounding the manubriosternal joint (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c].
 
 Bone scintigraphy showed no significant abnormalities.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A newborn with ambiguous genitalia and multiple congenital anomalies was admitted in March 2023. Born preterm at 34 weeks, the infant faced respiratory distress, electrolyte imbalances, and sepsis.
 Imaging Findings: The patient presented with extensive congenital anomalies, including an omphalocele with liver and bowel herniating through a membrane-covered defect, bladder exstrophy, and imperforate anus.
 
 Spinal imaging revealed fusion defects in the lower thoracic and lumbar vertebrae, with an associated cystic mass with dystrophic neural elements. Cranial imaging showed a low-lying torcular Herophili, ventriculomegaly, fullness of the foramen magnum, and possible aqueductal stenosis. Other imaging findings included ectopic kidneys (one partially thoracic and another pelvic), midgut malrotation, sacral agenesis, rib synostosis, pubic symphysis diastasis, ambiguous genitalia, and other musculoskeletal abnormalities, highlighting extensive multi-system involvement.
 
 Due to the patient’s medical instability and eventual demise, as well as lack of resources, further imaging could not assess other abnormalities including undescended testes and detailed evaluation of the urinary system. These structural abnormalities posed considerable diagnostic and management challenges.

   - Pros: Many reported anomalies overlap (vertebral defects, anal atresia, cardiac defects, tracheoesophageal fistula, renal anomalies, limb anomalies). Our patient has vertebral defects, anal atresia, renal anomalies, skeletal anomalies.

   - Pros: Midline abdominal wall defects (omphalocele), ectopic heart, diaphragmatic abnormalities, pericardial defects, genitourinary anomalies.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 85-year-old woman with no significant past medical history presented with a rapidly growing mass on her left shoulder (Figure 1), with no history of trauma.
 
 On physical examination, the mass was hard and non-mobile.
 Imaging Findings: A shoulder X-ray and ultrasound scan were performed in order to rule out malignancy.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 71-year-old lady reported to the emergency with a history of a fall and was referred for CT brain. Retrospective history revealed recurrent swelling and itching over the left temporal region for the last 6 months.
 Imaging Findings: No trauma-related abnormality was seen on CT. However, a thick-walled cystic lesion was noted in the subcutaneous plane on the left temporal region of the scalp (Figure 1).
 
 Ultrasound of the lesion showed a cyst with actively mobile, tubular serpiginous structures, having echogenic walls showing
 
 a “filarial dance”
 
 sign (Figures 2 and 3).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 71-year-old lady reported to the emergency with a history of a fall and was referred for CT brain. Retrospective history revealed recurrent swelling and itching over the left temporal region for the last 6 months.
 Imaging Findings: No trauma-related abnormality was seen on CT. However, a thick-walled cystic lesion was noted in the subcutaneous plane on the left temporal region of the scalp. Ultrasound of the lesion showed a cyst with actively mobile, tubular serpiginous structures, having echogenic walls showing a “filarial dance” sign.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Dirofilariasis, Epidermal inclusion cyst, Cysticercosis, Foreign body granuloma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Patient is a 71-year-old lady with a history of fall, but no trauma-related abnormality on CT.  
- Notable finding: thick-walled cystic lesion in the subcutaneous plane over the left temporal scalp.  
- On ultrasound: cyst with actively mobile, tubular serpiginous structures with echogenic walls.  
- “Filarial dance” sign described on ultrasound.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Dirofilariasis  
   - Pro: Dirofilaria can form cystic subcutaneous lesions; mobile worms can be seen as tubular structures on ultrasound.  
   - Con: Dirofilaria is rare in humans, usually subcutaneous or pulmonary nodules; no “filarial dance” sign typically described.  

2) Epidermal inclusion cyst  
   - Pro: Common cystic lesion in subcutaneous tissue; thick-walled cystic lesion fits.  
   - Con: Ultrasound typically shows simple anechoic or complex cyst without mobile echogenic linear structures; no "filarial dance" sign.  

3) Cysticercosis  
   - Pro: Subcutaneous cystic lesion; worms can be seen on ultrasound.  
   - Con: Typically the scolex appears dot-like centrally, not tubular serpiginous mobile structures; “filarial dance” sign is not characteristic.  

4) Foreign body granuloma  
   - Pro: Foreign bodies can lead to cystic lesions with granulation tissue.  
   - Con: No typical mobile echogenic linear structures; no sign of parasite.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Dirofilariasis – most compatible with description of mobile, tubular serpiginous structures and "filarial dance" sign in a cystic lesion.  
2) Cysticercosis – worm seen but typically different morphology and pattern.  
3) Epidermal inclusion cyst – no mobile structures expected.  
4) Foreign body granuloma – least likely.  
</analysis>

<final>Dirofilariasis</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 18-year-old female presented to the maxillofacial surgeon with persistent primary teeth and a palpable left pre-auricular mass, which she first noticed two years prior. No other symptoms were present.
 Imaging Findings: An
 
 orthopantomogram
 
 showed a large exophytic sclerotic bone lesion in the left mandible ramus, persistent primary teeth, caries, and multiple smaller sclerotic bone lesions interspersed in the mandible, some with cortical expansion (Figure 1). A CT scan of the maxillofacial region confirmed the presence of a
 
 dense ivory-like exophytic lesion growing out of the left mandibular ramus
 
 (Figure 2), compatible with an osteoma, and multiple well-delineated lesions of mature bone between the roots of the teeth compatible with hypercementosis.
 
 During clinical examination, a mass was palpated on the forehead leading to a CT scan of the skull, revealing multiple small, sessile sclerotic lesions protruding from the outer table (Figure 3), also consistent with osteomas.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: An 18-year-old female presented to the maxillofacial surgeon with persistent primary teeth and a palpable left pre-auricular mass, which she first noticed two years prior. No other symptoms were present.
 Imaging Findings: An orthopantomogram showed a large exophytic sclerotic bone lesion in the left mandible ramus, persistent primary teeth, caries, and multiple smaller sclerotic bone lesions interspersed in the mandible, some with cortical expansion. A CT scan of the maxillofacial region confirmed the presence of a dense ivory-like exophytic lesion growing out of the left mandibular ramus, compatible with an osteoma, and multiple well-delineated lesions of mature bone between the roots of the teeth compatible with hypercementosis. During clinical examination, a mass was palpated on the forehead leading to a CT scan of the skull, revealing multiple small, sessile sclerotic lesions protruding from the outer table, also consistent with osteomas.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Cemento-osseous dysplasia, Osteomatosis, Odontomas, Gardner’s syndrome, Hypercementosis, Hereditary multiple exostoses, Fibrous dysplasia
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 18-year-old female with persistent primary teeth and multiple sclerotic bone lesions in the mandible, including a large exophytic "ivory-like" lesion suggestive of an osteoma.  
- Multiple smaller sclerotic lesions interspersed in the mandible with cortical expansion.  
- Hypercementosis observed around tooth roots (suggested by well-delineated lesions of mature bone between roots).  
- Palpable left pre-auricular mass and a mass on the forehead; CT skull reveals multiple small sessile sclerotic lesions protruding from the outer table (features typical of osteomas).  
- Presence of both osteomas in jaw and skull in a young patient.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Carmotosteous dysplasia  
   - Pro: Can present as sclerotic bone lesions in jaw.  
   - Con: Usually involves replacement of normal bone with fibrous and cementoid tissues, not exophytic ivory-like osteomas; does not produce osteomas in skull; no mention of cemental lesions; not typical in young female.  
2) Osteomatosis  
   - Pro: Osteomas are present, could be multifocal.  
   - Con: Typically systemic disease with numerous bone lesions throughout skeleton, not limited to jaw and skull; no mention of skeletal lesions elsewhere; no skeletal pain or deformities.  
3) Odontomas  
   - Pro: Might be associated with delayed tooth eruption.  
   - Con: Odontomas are hamartomatous lesions derived from odontogenic tissues forming tooth-like structures; not described as ivory-like exophytic osteomas or hypercementosis; usually appear as mixed radiolucent/radiopaque lesions within or at the end of a tooth socket, not widespread sclerotic bone lesions.  
4) Gardner’s syndrome  
   - Pro: Classic triad includes intestinal polyps, osteomas, and cutaneous lesions. Osteomas frequently appear in mandible, skull, and paranasal sinuses. Hypercementosis and multiple osteomas (jaw, skull) fit classic features. Patient age (young) fits typical presentation.  
   - Con: No mention of intestinal polyps or cutaneous findings (can be absent or not yet evident).  
5) Hypercementosis  
   - Pro: Hypercementosis present.  
   - Con: Does not explain multiple osteomas in jaw and skull or delayed tooth eruption.  
6) Hereditary multiple exostoses (HME)  
   - Pro: Multiple osteochondromas (exostoses) can be present.  
   - Con: Exostoses are cartilage-capped lesions near growth plates; jaw and skull osteomas not typical; age and site inconsistent.  
7) Fibrous dysplasia  
   - Pro: Can affect craniofacial bones in adolescents.  
   - Con: Lesions are usually radiolucent or mixed, ground glass appearance, not ivory-like exophytic osteomas; hypercementosis not typical.  

C) Ranking with tie-break:  
1) Gardner’s syndrome: Multiple exophytic osteomas in jaw and skull, hypercementosis, and persisting primary teeth fit classic radiological and clinical picture, even though polyps/cutaneous lesions not reported.  
2) Osteomatosis: Osteomas present but lack systemic multifocal bony lesions and typical age/site.  
3) Others have significant inconsistencies.  
Thus, best diagnosis is Gardner’s syndrome.  
</analysis><final>Gardner’s syndrome</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A nine-year-old female child presented with fever, restriction of neck movements and posterior bulging of the mucosa of the posterior oropharyngeal/hypopharyngeal wall lateralised to the left side on nasopharyngolaryngoscopy. Piriform sinuses were inspected and did not show any irregularities.
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced CT scan of the neck revealed a well-defined oval fluid collection, with rim enhancement, in the retropharyngeal space, measuring 2 x 2 x 1 cm
 
 (ap/axial/cc) (
 
 rrows in Figures 1
 
 and
 
 2). Retropharyngeal space oedema was also present, from the skull base to the hypopharynx level (
 
 small arrow in Figure 2
 
 ). Additionally, a thin well-defined rim-
 
 enhancing
 
 fistulous tract could be identified, running from the inferior left border of the retropharyngeal collection to the upper pole of the left thyroid lobe (arrows on Figure 3).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A nine-year-old female child presented with fever, restriction of neck movements and posterior bulging of the mucosa of the posterior oropharyngeal/hypopharyngeal wall lateralised to the left side on nasopharyngolaryngoscopy. Piriform sinuses were inspected and did not show any irregularities.
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced CT scan of the neck revealed a well-defined oval fluid collection, with rim enhancement, in the retropharyngeal space, measuring 2 x 2 x 1 cm. Retropharyngeal space oedema was also present, from the skull base to the hypopharynx level. Additionally, a thin well-defined rim-enhancing fistulous tract could be identified, running from the inferior left border of the retropharyngeal collection to the upper pole of the left thyroid lobe.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Retropharyngeal abscess, Atypically located third or fourth infected branchial cleft cyst, Necrotic retropharyngeal adenopathy, Suppurative thyroiditis
FinalDiagnosis: Atypically located third or fourth infected branchial cleft cyst

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:</analysis>
- Patient: 9-year-old child with fever and restricted neck movement.
- Nasopharyngolaryngoscopy: posterior bulging of posterior oropharyngeal/hypopharyngeal wall mucosa on left side, normal piriform sinuses.
- Imaging: contrast-enhanced CT neck.
  - Well-defined oval fluid collection with rim enhancement in retropharyngeal space (2 x 2 x 1 cm).
  - Retropharyngeal space edema extending from skull base to hypopharynx.
  - Thin, well-defined rim-enhancing fistulous tract from the inferior left border of retropharyngeal collection to the upper pole of the left thyroid lobe.

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:

1) Retropharyngeal abscess
   - Pro: Fluid collection with rim enhancement and retropharyngeal edema fits features of an abscess. Fever and neck restriction support infection.
   - Con: Presence of fistulous tract extending to thyroid lobe not typical for primary retropharyngeal abscess; retropharyngeal abscess usually unrelated to thyroid involvement. Moreover, lateralized bulging may also be explained by other processes.

2) Atypically located third or fourth infected branchial cleft cyst
   - Pro: Presence of a cystic lesion with rim enhancement; branchial cleft anomalies can present in neck and may become infected. Fistula tract may be associated with branchial cleft anomalies.
   - Con: Branchial cleft cysts are usually lateral neck cysts; retropharyngeal space location is atypical. Branchial cleft remnants are usually superficial, not deep retropharyngeal; and a fistulous tract to thyroid usually seen in fourth branchial cleft cyst.

3) Necrotic retropharyngeal adenopathy
   - Pro: Retropharyngeal lymph node necrosis can cause rim-enhancing collection; retropharyngeal space involvement and edema support this.
   - Con: Fistulous tract to thyroid is unusual for necrotic adenopathy; clinical presentation less likely with fever and neck restriction, though possible but less typical.

4) Suppurative thyroiditis
   - Pro: Fistulous tract connecting retropharyngeal collection to thyroid lobe suggests origin or spread to thyroid; classic for suppurative thyroiditis in children via congenital fistula from branchial cleft anomalies.
   - Con: Fluid collection primarily described in retropharyngeal space rather than thyroid; bulging originates from retropharyngeal side; but fistulous tract to thyroid is characteristic. Retropharyngeal edema and fluid collection may be secondary to thyroid infection.

C) Ranking:
1) Suppurative thyroiditis - This best explains the fistulous tract connecting retropharyngeal collection to thyroid lobe, typical for acute suppurative thyroiditis with congenital connection (often from fourth branchial cleft cyst). Clinical presentation with fever and neck symptoms is consistent.
2) Retropharyngeal abscess - While clinical and imaging features fit abscess, fistulous tract to thyroid is not typical and suggests another etiology.
3) A typically located third or fourth infected branchial cleft cyst - Though possible, the retropharyngeal location is atypical; fistulous connection to thyroid suggests fourth branchial cleft anomaly.
4) Necrotic retropharyngeal adenopathy - Less likely given fistulous tract and typical inflammation signs.

<final> Suppurative thyroiditis</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: We present the case of a 62-year-old man with a distal oesophageal adenocarcinoma treated with neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy who developed rapid swelling in the left preauricular region within hours after Ivor Lewis oesophagectomy. CT was performed to rule out active bleeding and postoperative haematoma.
 Imaging Findings: CT showed diffuse enlargement of the left parotid gland with increased heterogeneous uptake of contrast material, fat stranding in the left parotid space, and thickening of the adjacent superficial layer of the deep cervical fascia (Figures 1 and 3). Oedema and fat stranding extended slightly into the soft tissues of the ipsilateral buccal and masticator spaces, and the sternocleidomastoid muscle was enlarged (Figures 2 and 4). There was no involvement of deeper compartments.
 
 No haematomas or signs of active bleeding were observed.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: We present the case of a 62-year-old man with a distal oesophageal adenocarcinoma treated with neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy who developed rapid swelling in the left preauricular region within hours after Ivor Lewis oesophagectomy. CT was performed to rule out active bleeding and postoperative haematoma.
 Imaging Findings: CT showed diffuse enlargement of the left parotid gland with increased heterogeneous uptake of contrast material, fat stranding in the left parotid space, and thickening of the adjacent superficial layer of the deep cervical fascia. Oedema and fat stranding extended slightly into the soft tissues of the ipsilateral buccal and masticator spaces, and the sternocleidomastoid muscle was enlarged. There was no involvement of deeper compartments. No haematomas or signs of active bleeding were observed.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Acute postoperative parotitis, Haematoma and active bleeding, Postsurgical infection (e.g., abscess), Viral parotitis (mumps), Bacterial sialadenitis

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 62-year-old man post Ivor Lewis oesophagectomy with rapid swelling in left preauricular region  
- CT shows diffuse enlargement of left parotid gland with increased heterogeneous contrast uptake

- Edema and fat stranding in ipsilateral buccal and masticator spaces; sternocleidomastoid muscle enlargement

- No deeper compartment involvement  

B) Candidates:  
1. Acute postoperative parotitis  
   - Pros: Clinical context (postoperative swelling), diffuse parotid enlargement with enhancement, fat stranding typical of inflammation, no haematoma or bleeding, involvement limited to superficial planes as expected in parotitis.  
   - Cons: None major.  
2. Haematoma and active bleeding  
   - Pros: Postoperative setting, possible hematoma formation.  
   - Cons: No haematoma or bleeding seen on CT; fat stranding and diffuse gland enlargement unlikely without hematoma, no active contrast extravasation.  
3. Postsurgical infection (e.g., abscess)  
   - Pros: Potential postoperative infection with swelling.  
   - Cons: No abscess cavity or rim-enhancing collection seen, fat stranding is diffuse rather than localized; clinical timeline within hours is early for abscess formation, and enhancement pattern favors oedematous/inflammatory changes rather than abscess.  
4. Viral parotitis (mumps)  
   - Pros: Parotid gland swelling and enhancement are typical.  
   - Cons: Unlikely in postoperative setting; unilateral involvement and acute postoperative setting favour postoperative etiology; mumps is typically bilateral and viral infection less common postoperatively.  
5. Bacterial sialadenitis  
   - Pros: Parotid gland infection/inflammation with enhancement and fat stranding possible.  
   - Cons: Usually bacterial sialadenitis presents with more diffuse or focal suppuration, potential abscess formation; rapid onset postoperative swelling likely inflammatory rather than bacterial infection; no mention of pain, pus, or other bacterial signs.

C) Ranking:

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-day-old male presented with non-bilious emesis and a left thigh cutaneous lesion. The patient was found to have pyloric atresia requiring gastroduodenostomy; this procedure led to intestinal malrotation, and the appendix was removed prophylactically. During surgery, the cutaneous lesion was also biopsied. The patient was transiently jaundiced following surgery; this resolved spontaneously, but a liver ultrasound was requested as part of the workup of this.
 Imaging Findings: Liver US showed a normal biliary tree and 2 hypoechoic liver lesions: the larger a 6 mm lesion in segment IVb (lesion 1). Contrast-enhanced ultrasound (CEUS) was performed to further characterise. Three lesions were examined with 4 boluses of ultrasound (Total vol. 1.1mL, Optison, GE Healthcare Inc., using a Phillips EPIQ machine). Lesion 1 had increased in size (seg. IVb, now 32 mm) (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d); Lesions 2 (seg. II, 7 mm) (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d) and 3 were also seen (seg. I,10 mm) (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d).
 
 To further characterise, a liver MRI with hepatobiliary contrast was performed. Lesion 1 (34 mm) was T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, infiltrative, rectangular area with capsular retraction (Figures 4a, 4b and 4c). Lesions 2 (Figure 4d) and 3 were T1 hypointense with central T2 hyperintensity relative to the liver. No lesions showed enhancement, macroscopic fat, or diffusion restriction.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 19-year-old male presented to the orthopaedic surgeon with complaints of recurrent left shoulder pain. The patient practices combat sports and associates the complaints with a recent fall during training. A shoulder magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was conducted, followed by MRI arthrography (MRA) due to inconsistent findings.
 Imaging Findings: MRI revealed oedematous changes in the inferior glenohumeral joint capsule, with increased signal intensity on the fat-saturated proton-density (PDFS) sequence, suggestive of an
 
 inferior glenohumeral ligament
 
 (
 
 IGHL) lesion (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c).
 
 Additionally, there was an increased signal intensity in the superior labrum, which may indicate a labral lesion.
 
 However, this finding was considered non-specific due to the absence of intra-articular fluid or contrast (Figure 1a).
 
 Subsequent MRA revealed an intrasubstance full-thickness rupture of the anterior band of the IGHL with contrast extravasation (Figures 2a and 2b). This finding was not documented on the previous conventional MRI, which only indicated capsular oedema.
 
 Additionally, the axillary pouch changed from its normal U-shape to a J-shape on fat-saturated T2-weighted sequences (Figures 3a and 3b),
 
 indicating IGHL laxity and loss of its stabilising function
 
 .
 
 MRA also identified a
 
 superior labrum from anterior to posterior (
 
 SLAP) lesion (Figures 2c, 2d, and 3c), which was not clearly visible on MRI due to lack of fluid joint distention.

However, this finding was considered non-specific due to the absence of intra-articular fluid or contrast. Subsequent MRA revealed an intrasubstance full-thickness rupture of the anterior band of the IGHL with contrast extravasation. This finding was not documented on the previous conventional MRI, which only indicated capsular oedema. Additionally, the axillary pouch changed from its normal U-shape to a J-shape on fat-saturated T2-weighted sequences, indicating IGHL laxity and loss of its stabilising function. Clinical History: A 67-year-old female patient diagnosed with a malignant renal mass underwent a CT examination for cancer staging. Her general symptoms were interpreted at that time within the oncological context.
 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced CT examination of the thoracic, abdominal and pelvic regions was performed. Multiple images with tissular densities, mostly located peripherally, were identified in both lung areas. The described images ranged in size from a few millimetres (Figures 1a and 1b) to around 22x15 mm (Figures 2a and 2b), and enhanced during the arterial phase similarly to pulmonary vascular structures (Figures 3 and 4), with feeding vessels from lobar arteries and drainage into lobar veins (Figures 5a, 5b, 6a, and 6b).
PostDescription:

A) Key findings:  
- Female, 67 years old, with known malignant renal mass (oncological context)  
- Contrast-enhanced CT of thorax, abdomen, pelvis  
- Multiple well-defined, tissular density peripheral lung nodules ranging from a few mm to 22x15 mm  
- Nodules enhance during arterial phase, similar to pulmonary vascular structures  
- Feeding vessels from lobar arteries; drainage via lobar veins  

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  
1) Hypervascular lung metastases  
   - Pro: Oncologic context (renal cancer), multiple peripheral nodules, hypervascular appearance  
   - Con: Typically no feeding vessels from pulmonary arteries; metastases don't usually have feeding/draining vessels as vascular structures; enhancement pattern different  

2) Pulmonary sequestration  
   - Pro: Systemic arterial feeding, typically found in lower lobes, cystic or solid mass; may enhance
   - Con: Usually congenital anomaly, often single lesion, systemic arterial supply from the aorta (not lobar, pulmonary arteries); venous drainage systemic or pulmonary; not multiple peripheral nodules  

3) Bronchocoele  
   - Pro: Air-filled bronchial dilation can appear as tissular lesions on CT  
   - Con: Typically not hypervascular, no feeding arterial pattern or enhancement; no such vascular structures; usually central or bronchial distribution, not peripheral nodules  

4) Pulmonary varix  
   - Pro: Dilated pulmonary vein can enhance like vascular structures  
   - Con: Usually solitary, central or perihilar location; not multiple or peripheral nodules; venous, not arterial feeding pattern  

5) Pulmonary arterial aneurysm  
   - Pro: Enhances with arterial phase; vascular structure  
   - Con: Usually solitary lesions; aneurysms, not multiple nodules; feeding and draining vessels present but usually larger and identifiable as aneurysms  

6) Pulmonary arteriovenous malformations (PAVMs)  
   - Pro: Parenchymal nodules with feeding artery and draining vein visible; enhance in arterial phase; commonly peripheral; multiple lesions in oncologic settings possible (though usually congenital)  
   - Con: Usually congenital or hereditary context; still, imaging appearance matches perfectly  

7) Aberrant systemic to pulmonary communications  
   - Pro: Vascular anomalies with abnormal communications; may enhance  
   - Con: Typically not presented as multiple small peripheral nodules; often single lesion; systemic artery feeding, not lobar pulmonary artery

1) Pulmonary arteriovenous malformations (best match: multiple peripheral nodules, feeding arteries from lobar vessels, draining veins, arterial enhancement)  
2) Hypervascular lung metastases (oncologic context, but lack typical feeding/drainage pattern)  
3) Pulmonary arterial aneurysm (possible but generally solitary lesions)  
4) Aberrant systemic to pulmonary communications (rarer presentation)  
5) Pulmonary varix (central, usually solitary)  
6) Pulmonary sequestration (different arterial origin, typically solitary)  
7) Bronchocoele (does not fit vascular enhancement or feeding drainage)  

Aberrancy: The classic imaging of pulmonary arteriovenous malformations is multiple peripheral nodules with feeding arteries (from pulmonary arteries) and draining veins (pulmonary veins), enhancing in the arterial phase, exactly as described.

Clinical History: A 39-year-old man presented to the emergency department with tibial and fibular fractures and a traumatic brain injury (TBI) after a motor vehicle accident.
 Imaging Findings: Imaging studies obtained after the trauma revealed a comminuted wedge fracture of the tibial diaphysis with mild displacement and angulation, along with an overlapping fibular fracture (Figure 1), and multifocal subarachnoid haemorrhage in the right frontoparietal and left parietal regions on brain CT (Figure 2).
 
 Six months after the initial injury, during which the patient remained bedridden without any new trauma history, shoulder X-rays and CT scans were performed due to complaints of shoulder pain (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). The imaging revealed extensive bilateral heterotopic ossification in the lower glenohumeral joint, indicating mature ossification.

   - Pro: Can cause heterotopic bone formation in soft tissue.  
   - Con: Usually after direct muscle trauma, localized, often unilateral, no TBI link.

   - Pro: May cause calcifications or ossifications.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: 81-year-old woman who was presumed to be suffering from polymyalgia rheumatica and was receiving 2.5mg/week of ledertrexate. The patient suffered from chondrocalcinosis of the wrist joints, bilateral arthritis in her hands and repeated bouts of plurifocal synovitis in her wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints. The patient presented for an ultrasound due to Achilles tendon pain due to suspected bilateral Achilles tendonitis.
 Imaging Findings: Multiple hyperechogenic elongated deposits with acoustic shadowing are located within the central part of the right Achilles tendon and following its long axis (Figures 1 and 2), as well as in the left Achilles tendon (Figures 3 and 4). The Achilles tendons are thickened and hypoechogenic. The findings were identical in both Achilles tendons.

   + Pros: Possible cause of intra-tendinous calcifications.

   - Cons: None significant.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old athlete with chest discomfort and palpitations presented to the cardiologist. Electrocardiogram showed low-density ventricular extrasystoles at rest, which increased in density with stress. Echocardiography revealed mild mitral regurgitation and mild left ventricular hypertrophy
 
 with a basal septal thickness of 12 mm.
 Imaging Findings: Cardiac magnetic resonance was performed on a 3 Tesla MRI scanner. Steady-state free precession (SSFP) sequences identify billowing of both mitral leaflets, with mitral valve prolapse (MVP) of the posterior leaflet (5 mm), mitral annular disjunction (6 mm) and “curling” (Figures 1a and 1b; yellow arrow). Mitral regurgitation was not significant. Biventricular function was normal.
 
 Late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) was present in the subendocardial–intramyocardial layer of the basal inferolateral wall, suggesting replacement fibrosis (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d; white arrows). Native T1 mapping in the same region was elevated: 1360 ms (local normal range 1150–1250 ms), confirming myocardial fibrosis (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d; yellow arrow). Cardiac CT ruled out significant stenosis in the circumflex artery and posterolateral artery (Figure 3; circumflex artery: yellow arrow).

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 45-year-old athlete with chest discomfort and palpitations.
- Low-density ventricular extrasystoles at rest, increased with stress.
- Echocardiogram: mild mitral regurgitation, mild left ventricular hypertrophy, basal septal thickness 12 mm.
- Cardiac MRI: both mitral leaflets show billowing, posterior leaflet prolapse (5 mm), mitral annular disjunction (6 mm), and "curling".
- Biventricular function normal.
- Late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) in the subendocardial-intramyocardial layer of the basal inferolateral wall (replacement fibrosis confirmed by elevated native T1 mapping 1360 ms vs normal 1150–1250 ms).
- Cardiac CT: no significant stenosis in circumflex artery or posterolateral artery.

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) Myocardial replacement fibrosis secondary to Ischemia in LCx territory
   - Pros: LGE matches LCx perfusion territory (basal inferolateral wall); subendocardial fibrosis typical of ischemic injury.
   - Cons: Cardiac CT rules out significant stenosis in LCx and posterolateral artery; no history of coronary artery disease; patient is young (45) without significant risk factors; echocardiogram does not show ischemic heart disease features.

2) Malignant mitral valve prolapse with subendocardial late enhancement
   - Pros: Severe forms of MVP (malignant) can cause replacement fibrosis, often subendocardial; typical sites include basal inferolateral wall.
   - Cons: Echocardiogram shows only mild mitral regurgitation and mild LV hypertrophy; "malignant" MVP typically associated with severe regurgitation or significant fibrosis; no mention of arrhythmia risks beyond LV outflow tract ectopy.

3) Mitral valve prolapse related replacement fibrosis
   - Pros: Patient has MVP with mitral annular disjunction and "curling", classic for complex MVP; fibrosis in basal inferolateral wall matches typical MVP fibrosis locations; native T1 mapping confirms fibrosis.
   - Cons: Mild mitral regurgitation may seem mild, but fibrosis can occur even with mild regurgitation; mild arrhythmias and structural heart changes support this.

4) Replacement fibrosis related to sarcoidosis
   - Pros: Sarcoidosis can cause fibrosis; non-specific fibrosis sites possible.
   - Cons: No other sarcoid-specific findings (e.g., lymphadenopathy, pulmonary involvement); basal inferolateral wall location not typical; no clinical or imaging signs of sarcoidosis.

5) Not specific replacement fibrosis
   - Pros: Non-specific fibrosis possible.
   - Cons: Specific site, clinical context (MVP with annular disjunction), and confirmed imaging findings suggest a specific etiology; too generic.

6) Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
   - Pros: Patient has mild LV hypertrophy; LV hypertrophy is seen in HCM.
   - Cons: No typical HCM features such as asymmetric septal hypertrophy, outflow tract obstruction, or typical LGE distribution; mitral valve prolapse can co-exist but not primary cause here.

C) Ranking:
1) Mitral valve prolapse related replacement fibrosis
   - Most likely given MVP with mitral annular disjunction and fibrosis in classic MVP location; mild MR does not exclude fibrosis.

2) Malignant mitral valve prolapse with subendocardial late enhancement
   - Possible but less likely as malignant MVP usually involves severe MR or high arrhythmia risk; no severe MR or major arrhythmias noted.

3) Myocardial replacement fibrosis secondary to Ischemia in LCx territory
   - Less likely due to negative coronary CT.

4) Not specific replacement fibrosis
   - Too generic; less likely given specific findings.

5) Replacement fibrosis related to sarcoidosis
   - Unlikely due to lack of supporting clinical/imaging data.

6) Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
   - Unlikely as primary diagnosis, given mild LVH and absence of typical HCM features.

<final>Mitral valve prolapse related replacement fibrosis</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 4-month-old male infant presented with a palpable mass on his arm. The parents noticed it since he was a month and a half old. Clinical examination showed a painless mass without signs of inflammation.
 Imaging Findings: On ultrasound (US), the mass was poorly defined and hyperechoic compared to muscle, located in the subcutaneous tissue. The lesion exhibited a serpentine pattern of hypoechoic bands with a hyperechoic border [1] (Figure 1a).
 
 It showed low vascularity on colour Doppler (Figure 1b).
 
 Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed a non-encapsulated soft tissue mass in the subcutaneous tissue of the arm, resting on the fascia without involving the underlying muscle. T1 and T1 fat-saturated images showed isointense tissue relative to muscle, with interspersed fatty foci (Figures 2a and 2b). No enhancement was noted after gadolinium injection (Figure 2c).
PostDescription:

The lesion exhibited a serpentine pattern of hypoechoic bands with a hyperechoic border [1]. It showed low vascularity on colour Doppler. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed a non-encapsulated soft tissue mass in the subcutaneous tissue of the arm, resting on the fascia without involving the underlying muscle. T1 and T1 fat-saturated images showed isointense tissue relative to muscle, with interspersed fatty foci. No enhancement was noted after gadolinium injection.

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 4-month-old infant with a palpable, painless, and non-inflammatory subcutaneous mass on the arm, present since 6 weeks of age.  
- Ultrasound: poorly defined, hyperechoic subcutaneous mass with a serpentine pattern of hypoechoic bands and a hyperechoic border; low vascularity on Doppler.  
- MRI: non-encapsulated soft tissue mass in subcutaneous tissue lying on fascia, not involving underlying muscle; T1 and T1 fat-saturated images show isointensity to muscle with fat foci interspersed; lack of enhancement after gadolinium.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Fibrous hamartoma of infancy:  
   Pros: Age fits (infancy), commonly in subcutaneous tissue, often involving the arm region, serpentine pattern of hypoechoic bands on US often described as “tangled cords” is classic. Low vascularity and non-encapsulated, resting on fascia while sparing muscle, is typical.  
   Cons: None major.  

2) Rhabdomyosarcoma:  
   Pros: Could occur in infants; mass in soft tissue.  
   Cons: Typically shows aggressive features, potential enhancement on MRI, vascularity on Doppler, usually intramuscular or involving musculature and deep structures; this case lacks these. Additionally, the serpentine pattern and low vascularity are not typical.  

3) Lipoblastoma:  
   Pros: Occurs in infants and young children; fatty composition expected on MRI.  
   Cons: Typically well-defined, encapsulated lobulated masses composed of fatty tissue; on MRI, the mass would show fat signal characteristics and often enhance; US findings not typical.  

4) Liposarcoma:  
   Pros: Fat containing mass, enhancement possible.  
   Cons: Extremely rare in infants; more common in adults; aggressive lesion with enhancement and higher vascularity; not fitting well.  

5) Lipoma:  
   Pros: Benign fatty tumor, low vascularity, common mass.  
   Cons: In infants, lipomas are uncommon; usually well-encapsulated and homogeneous; serpentine pattern on US and low vascularity atypical; MRI would show homogeneous fat signal.  

C) Ranking and tie-breaking:  
1) Fibrous hamartoma of infancy: Classic age, location, US serpentine pattern, low vascularity, non-encapsulated mass adherent to fascia but separate from muscle are textbook.  
2) Lipoblastoma: Rare in infant arm region, less characteristic US pattern.  
3) Lipoma: Possible, but less likely due to age and imaging features.  
4) Liposarcoma: Very unlikely in infant.  
5) Rhabdomyosarcoma: Least likely due to benign imaging features and lack of aggression/vascularity.

Final choice: Fibrous hamartoma of infancy</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 63-year-old male, who had a renal transplantation from a cadaveric donor 3 months earlier due to diffuse mesangial proliferative glomerulonephritis.
 Imaging Findings: In view of worsening renal function indices and hyperpyrexia, the patient underwent an ultrasound which highlighted grade III hydro-nephrosis. The patient was admitted to our hospital to undergo a unilateral percutaneous nephrostomy
 
 procedure that was performed under fluoroscopy guidance and intravenous deep sedation. Because the graft was in the right iliac fossa
 
 , t
 
 he patient was supine.
 
 The antegrade pyelography revealed a stricture at the ureteral-bladder anastomosis and a
 
 tortuosity of the ureter
 
 , causing an occlusive narrowing of the ureter that prevented the contrast medium from passing into the bladder.
 
 Despite several attempts through multiple angles of the C-arm, from 18° cranial (CRA) to 34° caudal (CAU) and from 52° right (RAO) to 19° left (LAO) angulations, the ureteric bud cannot be visualised
 
 (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d). After a failed attempt to overcome the ureteral narrowing, an 8.5 Fr nephrostomy drainage was placed in the renal pelvis.
 
 The second attempt was scheduled for 2 weeks later.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 63-year-old male, who had a renal transplantation from a cadaveric donor 3 months earlier due to diffuse mesangial proliferative glomerulonephritis.
 Imaging Findings: In view of worsening renal function indices and hyperpyrexia, the patient underwent an ultrasound which highlighted grade III hydronephrosis. The patient was admitted to our hospital to undergo a unilateral percutaneous nephrostomy procedure that was performed under fluoroscopy guidance and intravenous deep sedation. Because the graft was in the right iliac fossa, the patient was supine. The antegrade pyelography revealed a stricture at the ureteral-bladder anastomosis and a tortuosity of the ureter, causing an occlusive narrowing of the ureter that prevented the contrast medium from passing into the bladder. Despite several attempts through multiple angles of the C-arm, from 18° cranial (CRA) to 34° caudal (CAU) and from 52° right (RAO) to 19° left (LAO) angulations, the ureteric bud cannot be visualised. After a failed attempt to overcome the ureteral narrowing, an 8.5 Fr nephrostomy drainage was placed in the renal pelvis. The second attempt was scheduled for 2 weeks later.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Post-transplant renal artery stent, Post-transplant renal vein stenting, Post-transplant ureteral stent removal, Post-transplant ureteral stent placement
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 63-year-old male with renal transplant in right iliac fossa 3 months prior.  
- Clinical signs: worsening renal function, hyperpyrexia.  
- Ultrasound: grade III hydronephrosis.  
- Antegrade pyelography: stricture at ureteral-bladder anastomosis, tortuous ureter causing occlusive narrowing, preventing contrast passage to bladder.  
- Ureteric bud not visualised despite multiple fluoroscopy angulations.  
- After failed attempts to pass the stricture, a nephrostomy tube was placed.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  
1) Post-transplant renal artery stent  
- Pros: Common complication, can affect renal function.  
- Cons: Imaging findings do not mention any vascular stent or vascular compromise. No mention of artery issues, no stent seen on fluoroscopy. No evidence of arterial stent-related findings. Ruled out.  
  
2) Post-transplant renal vein stenting  
- Pros: Can cause renal dysfunction if thrombosed or stenosed.  
- Cons: No mention of any vascular stent or vein issues on imaging. No filling defects or venous stent seen. Ruled out.  
  
3) Post-transplant ureteral stent removal  
- Pros: Prior ureteral stent may have been placed and removed.  
- Cons: Imaging does not mention any removable stent presence or removal attempts. The current imaging focuses on ureteral stricture and occlusion rather than stent removal. No evidence to support this.  
  
4) Post-transplant ureteral stent placement  
- Pros: Common prophylactic measure after renal transplant to prevent ureteral obstruction; current issues with ureteral stricture and hydronephrosis could be related.  
- Cons: The imaging does not specifically show a stent placed; however, given the obstructing stricture at the ureteral-bladder anastomosis and difficulty visualizing the ureteric bud, stent placement was likely attempted or planned (failed attempts, subsequent nephrostomy). The clinical context supports that stent placement was attempted or required.  
  
C) Ranking and final choice:  
- 1st: Post-transplant ureteral stent placement (most consistent with the scenario: stricture at ureteric anastomosis with hydronephrosis, failed attempts to decompress, placement of nephrostomy subsequently planned).  
- 2nd: Post-transplant ureteral stent removal (possible, but no evidence of removal attempts or recent removal).  
- 3rd: Post-transplant renal vein stenting (no evidence).  
- 4th: Post-transplant renal artery stent (no evidence).  
</analysis><final>Post-transplant ureteral stent placement</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 24-year-old lady presented to the emergency department with accelerated hypertension. She had a history of recurrent episodes of sudden onset palpitations, headache and giddiness for the past two years, with uncontrolled hypertension despite being on multiple antihypertensive medications. Her blood investigations revealed elevated serum normetanephrine (1990 pg/mL).
 Imaging Findings: Trans-abdominal ultrasound (Figures 1a and 1b) showed a well-defined heterogeneously hyperechoic lesion in the right adnexa, seen separate from the right ovary, measuring approximately 4.7 x 5 x 4.8 cm, and with high internal vascularity.
 
 CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis (Figures 2a, 2b, 3a, and 3b) showed a hyperdense mass (average HU of 29) with intense heterogeneous arterial enhancement and few central non-enhancing areas suggestive of necrosis. It was located in the right adnexa and paracervical region, abutting the uterus (which was displaced to the left). It was abutting the posterior urinary bladder wall anteriorly and the rectum posteriorly with no invasion of the right ureter, iliac vessels and lateral pelvic side wall.
 
 On MRI of the pelvis, the lesion was deep to the lateral pelvic fascia on the right, heterogeneously iso to hyperintense on T1WI (Figures 4b and 5), heterogeneously hyperintense on T2WI (
 
 “light bulb sign”
 
 ) (Figure 4a), with areas of restricted diffusion on DWI and maintained fat planes with the adjacent structures (Figures 6a and 6b).
 
 On 68Ga-DOTATATE scan, there was intense homogeneous SSTR (somatostatin receptor) expression in a large well-defined soft tissue density mass in the right adnexa and paracervical region with maximum standardised uptake value (SUV max) of 20.19 (Figure 7).

Choosing Pelvic paraganglioma as it perfectly matches the clinical picture, biochemical, and imaging findings including intense SSTR expression, characteristic MRI signals, and location.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 63-year-old woman diagnosed with recurrent meningioma located in the foramen magnum consulted for headache and paraesthesia of the upper limbs. It was decided to perform decompressive surgery and, in the immediate postoperative period, she presented tetraplegia with preservation of consciousness and requirement of mechanical ventilation.
 Imaging Findings: The preoperative MRI showed an extra-axial expansive lesion located in the foramen magnum, measuring 3.9 x 2.1 cm, and producing a mass effect on the bulb and spinal cord, displacing them posteriorly and to the left. It also compromised the V4 segment of the right vertebral artery (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d).
 
 The MRI on day +2 after the surgery showed signs of tumour persistence of the partially removed lesion (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c) and signal alteration in the bulb and upper spinal cord, with hyperintensity in T2/FLAIR sequences (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c) and hypointensity in T1. Diffusion sequences showed signs of mixed oedema (vasogenic-cytotoxic), all in relation to ischemic-oedematous phenomena (Figures 4a to 4i).
