<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 63-year-old female with history of recurrent meningioma at foramen magnum.
- Symptoms: headache, paraesthesia of upper limbs pre-op.
- Surgery performed, postoperative tetraplegia though consciousness preserved; mechanical ventilation needed.
- Imaging: Pre-op MRI shows extra-axial lesion compressing bulb and spinal cord, displacing post-left, compressing right vertebral artery (V4.
- Post-op MRI (day +2): residual tumor, bulb and upper spinal cord T2/FLAIR hyperintensity (edema/ischemia), T1 hypointensity (likely edema or ischemia), and diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) showing mixed vasogenic-cytotoxic edema (ischemic- edematous changes).

B) Candidates:
1) Central cord syndrome
- Pros: Symptoms can include tetraplegia with upper limb involvement; occurs from cervical spinal cord injury commonly.
- Cons: Typically results from hyperextension injuries; no mention of trauma; MRI would show central cord edema without external lesion; no tumor involvement. Not typical after decompressive surgery of meningioma.

2) White cord syndrome
- Pros: Known as postoperative complication after spinal decompression, presenting with new neurological deficits; T2 hyperintensity indicating spinal cord edema or ischemia after sudden decompression; mixed vasogenic-cytotoxic edema on DWI typical; fits clinical and imaging context.
- Cons: Rare but recognized entity.

3) Compressive myelopathy
- Pros: Preoperative tumor causing compression; but postoperative MRI shows residual tumor but also ischemic changes.
- Cons: Symptoms worsened postoperatively despite surgery; also pre-op imaging didn't correspond to acute deficit; more likely pre-op deficit caused gradually rather than tetraplegia.

4) Epidural haematoma
- Pros: Post-op epidural hematoma can cause spinal cord compression and deficits.
- Cons: Imaging doesn't mention epidural hematoma; typical hyperdense MRI mass; no mention of such findings on post-op MRI.

C) Ranking:
1) White cord syndrome - Fits postoperative tetraplegia despite decompression surgery, presence of ischemic changes and edema in bulb and cervical spinal cord; known complication after surgical decompression in severe stenosis.
2) Compressive myelopathy - Relevant due to tumor, but postoperative deficits occurred despite partial tumor removal; not usual for acute worsening post-op if tumor remains.
3) Central cord syndrome - Doesn't fit surgical scenario; no trauma.
4) Epidural haematoma - No imaging evidence.

<final>White cord syndrome</final><|return|>
options: Central cord syndrome | White cord syndrome | Compressive myelopathy | Epidural haematoma

 spine

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 50-year-old female with a known history of neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1) presented with atraumatic pain and weakness of the left leg. She had previously had a focal neurofibroma excised from the left thigh. MRI imaging of the left femur and knee was performed to identify a cause for the pain.
 Imaging Findings: MRI scan of the left knee (Figures 1, 2, and 3) demonstrated diffuse T2 bright and iso-hypotense T1 tissue infiltrating the subcutaneous and intermuscular planes at the posterior aspect of the knee and reaching the periosteal surface of the distal femur, causing bone dysplasia with widening of the posterior intercondylar notch. CT imaging also demonstrated the marked widening of the intercondylar notch (Figures 4 and 5).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 29-year-old healthy woman presented with a 4-day history of right breast pain and periareolar bruising associated with a 1-day periareolar indurated tumefaction. She denied any other symptoms, trauma, or regular medication use. Physical examination revealed a 2-cm indurated, painful cord-like structure in the periareolar region associated with bruising and petechial lesions.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasonography of the right breast revealed a subcutaneous, non-compressible, tubular hypoechoic structure with low-level internal echoes along an extension of approximately 28 mm (Figures 1 and 2). No blood flow was detected on colour or spectral Doppler studies (Figure 3). Furthermore, the adjacent soft tissues showed increased echogenicity. No other anomalies were seen in the right breast. Ultrasound scans of the left breast and axillary regions yielded no significant findings.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 53-year-old male patient presented with a lump in his left jaw, with noted enlargement in 2 months. There were no associated symptoms or relevant clinical history. Physical examination disclosed an expansile lesion in the left mandibular region and excluded palpable lymph nodes.
 Imaging Findings: Non-enhanced computed tomography (CT) dental scan showed an expansile lytic lesion centred to the angle and ascending ramus of the left mandible, measuring 4 cm at its largest axis, with well-defined, multilobulated borders and ground-glass ossified matrix (Figures 1a and 1b). The final report included osteosarcoma and chondrosarcoma as diagnostic hypotheses and the patient was referred to our tertiary hospital.
 
 Contrast-enhanced cervical CT performed at our hospital better depicted that the lesion did not invade adjacent structures (Figure 2) and enabled evaluation of the infra-hyoid neck. A solid, hypodense, 3.5 cm mass was identified posterior to the right thyroid lobe (Figure 3) and a provisional diagnosis of parathyroid adenoma was proposed.
 
 On ultrasound, the referred nodule was well-defined and hypoechoic (Figures 4a and 4b).
 
 Laboratory findings revealed elevated parathyroid hormone (PTH) (301pg/mL) and elevated calcium levels (13mg/dL).
 
 Parathyroid scintigraphy with technetium-99m methoxy-isobutyl-isonitrile (
 
 Tc-99m MIBI)
 
 was performed and an adenoma of the right inferior parathyroid was confirmed (Figures 5a and 5b).

- 53-year-old male with a progressively enlarging lump in left mandible over 2 months.

Parathyroid adenoma with hyperparathyroidism is present, pointing towards primary hyperparathyroidism. In hyperparathyroidism, brown tumours (osteitis fibrosa cystica) are common skeletal manifestations presenting as lytic lesions.

Key histological possibilities among the list:
- Giant cell tumour: typically epiphyseal/metaphyseal lesions, expansile, usually no ground-glass matrix, and not typical in mandible; usually lacks hyperparathyroidism.
- Giant cell reparative granuloma: lytic, expansile, often in jaw, but less commonly with ground-glass matrix, and not characterized by hyperparathyroidism.
- Fibrous dysplasia: ground-glass matrix, expansile lesion in craniofacial bones, but not associated with hyperparathyroidism and brown tumour features.
- Brown tumour: lytic, expansile lesion, due to hyperparathyroidism; can occur in mandible; often associated with elevated PTH and hypercalcemia.
- Ossifying fibroma: well-defined sclerotic lesion, often in jaw; not associated with hyperparathyroidism; ossifying pattern differs; no ground-glass matrix.

3. Fibrous dysplasia: PASS? Ground-glass matrix fits but no hyperparathyroidism, and lesion location possible but less likely with hyperparathyroidism and brown tumour is more specific.
4. Brown tumour: PASS. Lesion location fits mandible, hyperparathyroidism confirmed biochemically, ground-glass matrix can be seen, and lesion characteristics match.
5. Ossifying fibroma: FAIL. Usually shows mixed radiolucent-opaque with well-defined borders and possible calcifications; not related to hyperparathyroidism.

C) Ranking and final choice:
1. Brown tumour: strongest match given hyperparathyroidism, lesion characteristics (expansile, lytic, ground-glass matrix), location.
2. Fibrous dysplasia: similar matrix but lacks hyperparathyroidism correlation.
3. Giant cell reparative granuloma: similar jaw location but less consistent with hyperparathyroidism.
4. Giant cell tumour: unlikely.
5. Ossifying fibroma: least consistent.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 58-year-old male with worsening nausea for the past 72 hours presented to the emergency department with severe chest pain radiating to the back in the context of 4 recent episodes of non-bloody, non-bilious emesis. He was afebrile, diaphoretic, and mildly tachycardic. Cardiac workup, complete blood count, and electrolytes were normal.
 Imaging Findings: A chest X-ray followed by contrast-enhanced chest CT were performed. The X-ray demonstrated pneumomediastinum extending into the neck and a tiny left apical pneumothorax. Notably, left pleural effusion with atelectasis and consolidation were also seen (Figure 1a). The coronal CT images were closely correlated to the X-ray findings, clearly showing the pneumomediastinum extending superiorly towards the neck soft tissues. A pneumothorax at the left lower lung base, not previously seen on X-ray, is also demonstrated on coronal CT view (Figure 1b).
 
 The axial chest CT image on lung window showed evidence of pneumomediastinum with air around the oesophagus. Left pneumothorax and bilateral pleural effusions are also seen (Figure 2). On the soft tissue window, a thickened distal oesophagus was seen; however, no clear site of perforation was appreciated. Bilateral complex attenuating pleural effusions were also observed but there was no obvious contrast extravasation (Figure 3).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Case 1 is a 54-year-old female who experienced right ankle pain after an injury, leading to the clinical suspicion of Achilles tendon rupture, and was evaluated by radiograph and MRI after 1 week.
 
 Case 2 is a 62-year-old male who reported calf pain, raising concerns for deep vein thrombosis, further evaluated with ultrasound. He noted a trivial trauma
 
 occurred 10 days prior.
 Imaging Findings: In Case 1, the lateral ankle radiograph revealed increased soft tissue thickness around the Achilles tendon with small calcification (Figure 1). MRI indicated significant disruption (95%) of the distal 5.5 cm of the tendon, cystic areas, and signs of haemorrhage (Figures 2a, 2b, 3a, 3b, and 4). Extensive oedema in the posterior lower leg muscles led to a screening of the leg that identified a 2.8 x 1.6 x 5.4 cm collection in the intermuscular fascial plane, causing indentation on the soleus. The plantaris tendon was partially torn, while the soleus and medial gastrocnemius exhibited grade 1 strain without fibre discontinuity (Figures 5a and 5b).
 
 In Case 2, the ultrasound showed a bulky distal 6.0 cm Achilles tendon with altered echotexture, multiple calcific foci, and a high-grade partial thickness tear (Figures 6a, 6b, and 6c). An anechoic collection measuring 3.3 x 1.2 x 8.6 cm was observed in the intermuscular fascial plane, between the gastrocnemius and soleus muscles, with no increased vascularity noted on the Doppler study (Figures 7a and 7b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old male, with skin melanoma (IIIc), on adjuvant immunotherapy with nivolumab since 6 months,
 
 previously without comorbidities,
 
 presented with >5-fold increase in serum lipase and
 
 an onset of diabetes
 
 . Nivolumab was suspended till lipase decreased. After the reintroduction of nivolumab, another elevation of lipase
 
 and abdominal pain
 
 were
 
 noted.
 Imaging Findings: Computed tomography showed diffuse pancreatic parenchymal enlargement, heterogeneity (Figure 1), and slightly increased peripancreatic fat attenuation. There were no radiological signs of cholelithiasis or cholecystitis. Additionally, diffuse colon and rectal wall thickening and hyperenhancement were reported (Figure 2), as well as a triangular enhancement defect in the left kidney (Figure 3).
 
 At the second onset of symptoms, MRI was performed, demonstrating pancreatic volume increase, with signal alterations on T2-weighted images, especially in the pancreatic head, and diffuse restricted diffusion (Figures 4a, 4b, 5, 6a, and 6b). Additionally, mildly expressed peripancreatic and mesenteric fat stranding was noted. No pancreatic duct or biliary tree dilatation was observed. There were no fluid collections, free abdominal fluid or adenopathy.

   - Pros: Can cause diffuse pancreatic enlargement, T2 hyperintensity, and heterogeneity.

   - Pros: Known association with immune checkpoint inhibitors like nivolumab.    - Pros: Some lab or imaging changes can occur with treatment.

- Second: Autoimmune pancreatitis, but IgG4 normal and imaging not classic.
- Third: Viral pancreatitis, unlikely given no infection context.
- Fourth and below: Chronic pancreatitis and idiopathic pancreatitis, less likely given acute presentation and drug association.

Final choice: Immune-related pancreatitis.
</analysis>

<final>Immune-related pancreatitis</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 81-year-old male patient on anticoagulation therapy, with a history of hip fracture and acute renal insufficiency, underwent a standard chest X-ray as part of the preoperative evaluation for a total hip replacement. He was asymptomatic on the respiratory level, with no dyspnoea or chest pain.
 Imaging Findings: The initial X-ray revealed a likely pleural right peripheral opacity with smooth borders and no acute angles (Figure 1).
 
 A CT scan was then performed, demonstrating an extra-pleural collection measuring 30 × 72 × 87 mm (AP × TR × CC). The collection was spontaneously hyperdense, suggesting the presence of blood or fluid with a high protein content. Additionally, the “extra-pleural fat sign” was observed, confirming an extra-pleural process rather than a pleural or lung lesion (Figures 2 and 3).
 
 In the presence of renal insufficiency and an extra-pleural mass, contrast injection was not performed. Consequently, a mass with potential bleeding could not be definitively ruled out, and an MRI was conducted. T1-weighted MRI images showed the extra-pleural collection appearing hyperintense due to the presence of blood products (Figure 4). In T2-weighted images, the lesion was mildly hyperintense, consistent with the presence of subacute haemorrhage or an organised haematoma (Figure 5).
 
 On diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI), a hyperintense signal with ADC restriction was observed (Figures 6a and 6b). After gadolinium injection, a peripheral enhancement of the lesion’s capsule was observed, with no enhancement observed within the lesion itself (Figure 7).
 
 This supports the primary haemorrhagic nature of the lesion rather than an inflammatory or neoplastic process.
 
 Non-contrast CT and MRI scans of the thorax were performed using T1, T2, DWI, and T1 fat-saturated gadolinium sequences in a Philips Ingenia 1.5T high-field MRI scanner and a Philips Incisive 32-slice CT scanner.

- No clinical signs of infection (no pleuritic chest pain, no dyspnoea).

1) Encysted pleural effusion
   - Pros: Encysted effusion can be adjacent to pleura, smooth margins.
   - Cons: Typically fluid density, not hyperdense; no high protein or blood features; no peripheral capsule enhancement; DWI typically fluid signal but no restriction; patient on anticoagulation unlikely to have spontaneous large effusion; no clinical context.
2) Empyema
   - Pros: Pleural fluid collection, potential complex effusion could be hyperdense.
   - Cons: No clinical signs of infection; no pleural thickening or signs typically seen; no septations or split pleura signs; no fever or cough; DWI restriction may be seen with empyema, but enhancement pattern differs (usually both pleura and fluid wall enhance).
3) Pleural malignant neoplasm
   - Pros: Pleural-based lesion could be malignancy.
   - Cons: No internal enhancement; no nodules or masses with enhancement; hyperintense on T1 suggests blood; "extra-pleural fat sign" favors extra-pleural location; no aggressive signs.
4) Extra-pleural haematoma
   - Pros: Hyperdense on CT (blood), hyperintense on T1 MRI (blood products), peripheral capsule enhancement without internal enhancement, DWI restriction (common in hematomas), on anticoagulated patient, no infection signs; extra-pleural fat sign present.
   - Cons: None significant. No history of chest trauma but anticoagulation predisposes.
5) Parapneumonic effusion
   - Pros: Pleural collection in setting of infection.
   - Cons: Asymptomatic, no infection signs; not enhancing internally; hyperdense on CT less typical; no pleuritic pain; no fever.
6) Solitary fibrous tumour
   - Pros: Pleural neoplasm, possible smooth margins.
   - Cons: Typically enhances after contrast; no internal enhancement; hyperdensity and T1 hyperintensity not typical; extra-pleural location not typical.
7) Intercostal nerve tumour
   - Pros: Extra-pleural lesion possible.
   - Cons: Rare, not hyperdense or T1 hyperintense as hemorrahge; enhancement pattern not typical.

C) Ranking with pathognomonic findings:
1) Extra-pleural haematoma - matches spontaneous hyperdensity on CT, T1 hyperintense lesion (blood), peripheral capsule enhancement, no internal enhancement, DWI restriction, no infection or neoplastic signs, patient is on anticoagulation.
2) Empyema - some overlap but lacks clinical infection signs and enhancement pattern.
3) Encysted pleural effusion - less likely due to hyperdensity and enhancement pattern.
4) Parasymptomatic effusion - less likely due to no infection.
5) Pleural malignant neoplasm - enhancement pattern and clinical data not supportive.
6) Solitary fibrous tumour - less typical enhancement and imaging features.
7) Intercostal nerve tumour - rare, imaging less supportive.

<final> Extra-pleural haematoma </final>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 64-year-old man with a prior history of pulmonary tuberculosis presents with an acute onset of significant haemoptysis. He mentions experiencing weight loss, tiredness, and intermittent fever. His medical history is unremarkable, except for tuberculosis, which was treated a decade ago. A chest X-ray performed recently demonstrated cavitary abnormalities.
 Imaging Findings: CT angiography provided the main findings, consisting of a localised aneurysm of a pulmonary artery branch located near a tuberculous cavity (Figures 2a and 2b), representing the aneurysm. Hyperdense opacity with surrounding haematoma indicates active bleeding, while multiple air locules within the opacity are characteristic of a tuberculous cavity (Figures 1a and 1b), distinguishing it from other pulmonary lesions. Centrilobular ground-glass nodules may be observed around the haematoma, suggesting endobronchial dissemination of blood (Figure 4). Post-contrast imaging reveals focal enhancement of the aneurysm, aiding in precise assessment of its size, shape, and location (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-year-old female was admitted to the emergency department due to a 2-week history of constipation, drooling, and left eye strabismus due to oculomotor nerve paralysis. Further physical examination without clinically significant findings. Initial laboratory tests upon admission showed white blood count (WBC), neutrophils, and lymphocytes within normal ranges.
 Imaging Findings: An initial contrast-enhanced CT scan of the brain (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d) showed a lesion that extended to cranial structures and temporal lobes, involving deep neck spaces.
 
 An initial contrast-enhanced thoracoabdominal CT scan (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d) showed pleural thickening, subpleural nodules, pancreatic lesions with dilated pancreatic duct calibre, bilateral renal cortical lesions, and a right paraspinal lesion invading the spinal canal at L2–L3 with nerve roots involvement, suggestive of metastasis. Pelvic lymphadenopathies were identified.
 
 A 2-month follow-up contrast-enhanced MRI (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d) revealed a smaller lesion compared to the previous CT, with decreased intracranial and cerebral extension and a reduced cerebral and cerebellar volume.
 
 The 2-month follow-up contrast-enhanced thoracoabdominal CT scan (Figures 4a, 4b, 4c, and 4d) showed no pleural thickening areas or subpleural nodules. The pancreas with a size reduction of the focal lesions. The pancreatic duct calibre was preserved, and the kidneys had no infiltrative lesions. Generalised reduction of pelvic lymphadenopathy. The paraspinal lesion was not visible. A not previously identified generalised fatty infiltration was shown.

Clinical History: In an ambulatory appointment, a 29-year-old female patient presented with chronic and persistent cough. No smoking history was referred, and the physical examination was unremarkable. An ambulatory chest x-ray was performed in our institution and further evaluation with computed tomography (CT), followed by magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), was later requested.
 Imaging Findings: The chest x-ray (Figures 1a and 1b) revealed an opacity, with rounded and well-defined borders, localised in the posterior and medial regions of the right middle lung field.
 
 Contrast-enhanced thoracic CT /Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d) revealed in the right paravertebral region, next to the posteromedial aspect of the 7
 
 th
 
 and 8
 
 costal arches, a large nodular lesion, with 47 x 49 x 41 mm (L x AP x T), ovoid with regular and well-defined borders, heterogeneous and with marked peripheral contrast uptake. It is localised in an extrapulmonary topography
 
 , as it is centred on the chest wall and shows obtuse angles with the thoracic wall, and it is near proximity to
 
 nerve structures. There were no signs of invasion of adjacent structures, particularly bone structures.
 
 The contrast-enhanced thoracic MRI images (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, and 3e) confirmed the localisation and nature of the mass, further characterising it as a soft tissue lesion showing heterogeneity of signal in the T2-weighted sequences, with areas of interstitial hyperintensity and markedly heterogeneous uptake after contrast administration.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 46-year-old male patient presented to our department for routine follow-up imaging after left radical orchiectomy 19 years earlier due to a testicular germ cell tumour, and with retroperitoneal recurrence 11 years after surgery. He had a previous history of left orchidopexy during childhood to repair cryptorchidism.
 Imaging Findings: In this patient, who underwent a left radical orchiectomy for a testicular germ cell tumour, the abdominal-pelvic computed tomography (CT) revealed tubular-shaped splenic tissue along the left paracolic gutter, representing a retroperitoneal splenic cord connecting the spleen to the remaining mesonephric structure in the left lower quadrant (Figures 1a to 1i). Additionally, this condition was associated with congenital absence of lower limbs (Figure 2).
 
 Retrospectively, the described abdominal features were considered stable compared to the oldest examination available in our database, which was performed at the time of the retroperitoneal recurrence (Figures 3a to 3f).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 3-year-old female with poor feeding toleration, aspirating all consistencies of food, with failure to thrive, was planned for gastrostomy tube placement, and an upper gastrointestinal study was requested for the anatomic assessment.
 Imaging Findings: A fluoroscopic upper gastrointestinal study was done by injection of barium via the indwelling enteric tube. The stomach exhibited a normal appearance and was normally emptied through a normal pylorus, duodenal bulb, and descending duodenum (Figure 1). After an initial descent of the duodenum, the third part of the duodenum did not cross the midline as expected. Instead, it ascends on the right side of the spine posterior and parallel to the descending duodenum up to the level of the duodenal bulb and then completes its transverse course and reaches the left upper abdomen (Figures 2 and 3).

A) Key findings:

- Third part of duodenum does not cross the midline as expected; instead, ascends on the right side of the spine posterior and parallel to the descending duodenum up to the level of the duodenal bulb, then completes its transverse course to the left upper abdomen.  
- This anatomic variation suggests an abnormal orientation of the third part of the duodenum, but not a full malrotation since the duodenum crosses midline eventually and the pylorus and stomach are normal.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Duodenum inversum  
   - Pro: Described pathognomonic abnormal course of the third part of the duodenum, ascending on the right side and crossing midline higher up, which matches classic duodenum inversum description.  
   - Con: None significant based on described findings; the rest of anatomy is normal, matching typical inversum cases.  
2) Redundant duodenum  
   - Pro: May have tortuous duodenum loops, but typically presents with redundancy or looping elsewhere in duodenum or small bowel.  
   - Con: No mention of elongation or redundancy; the abnormal course is specific and not just redundant loops.  
3) Intestinal malrotation  
   - Pro: Possible abnormal duodenum position in malrotation.  
   - Con: Malrotation usually involves abnormal position of duodenojejunal junction and may have other signs (e.g., failure of duodenal crossing, abnormal mesenteric root), not described here; stomach and pylorus are normal, and duodenum eventually crosses midline, which is less characteristic of malrotation.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Duodenum inversum - best fit with described abnormal course and normal rest of anatomy.  
2) Intestinal malrotation - less likely due to normal pylorus and eventual crossing of duodenum.  
3) Redundant duodenum - not strongly supported by findings.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 50-year-old female patient presented with headache, visual disturbances, and bitemporal hemianopia. Hormonal assays indicated abnormal levels of serum prolactin and growth hormone.
 Imaging Findings: The patient’s MRI brain showed a large lobulated sellar lesion (Figure 1) on post-contrast sagittal images, showing suprasellar extension and anteriorly extending into both sphenoid sinuses and ethmoid sinuses. Axial T2 image (Figure 2) shows the lesion to be predominantly involving the left cavernous sinus and encasing the left internal carotid artery, and seen extending into the left orbital apex and left frontal and temporal lobes. FLAIR coronal image (Figure 3) shows compression of adjacent brain parenchyma, lateral ventricle, and brainstem. In the post-contrast axial image (Figure 4), the lesion was seen extending into the right cavernous sinus with suprasellar extension into both frontal lobes and sphenoid sinuses.

FLAIR coronal image shows compression of adjacent brain parenchyma, lateral ventricle, and brainstem. Compression of adjacent brain parenchyma, lateral ventricle, and brainstem.  
- Post-contrast enhancement noted in sagittal and axial images.  

B) Candidate Pass/Fail:  
1) Meningioma  
   - Pros: Meningiomas can extend to parasellar regions and involve cavernous sinuses; lobulated shape possible; can compress brain parenchyma; may enhance with contrast.  
   - Cons: Typically not sellar-based; meningiomas do not originate in pituitary region; hormonal assays are not characteristic; not the common tumor in sellar region with endocrine abnormalities.  

2) Pituitary macroadenoma  
   - Pros: Most common sellar lesion; prolactin and growth hormone elevation suggest pituitary adenoma; suprasellar extension, sellar region location matches classic pituitary macroadenoma; can enlarge and involve adjacent structures like cavernous sinuses, optic chiasm (bitemporal hemianopia).  
   - Cons: Very extensive invasion into ethmoid sinuses and orbital apex is less common but can be seen in giant invasive adenomas; however, no mention of typical calcifications or "snowman" shape sometimes seen in macroadenomas. The extensive and bilateral cavernous sinus and sinus involvement suggests an aggressive lesion.  

3) Chordoma  
   - Pros: Can involve clivus and skull base; may have bone involvement; can be lobulated and enhance with contrast.  
   - Cons: Typically in clival region, not primary sellar lesions; hormonal abnormalities not typical; location and described extensions are not characteristic.  

C) Ranking with Justification:  
1) Pituitary macroadenoma: The combination of sellar mass with hormonal disturbances (prolactin, GH), suprasellar extension with bitemporal hemianopia, and invasion into adjacent structures is classic for giant invasive pituitary macroadenoma.  
2) Meningioma: While invasion of cavernous sinus and adjacent structures is plausible, the primary sellar location and endocrine dysfunction are not typical.  
3) Chordoma: Least likely given the location and hormonal involvement.

Tie-breaks: Pituitary macroadenoma is specific to sellar region and endocrine abnormalities, matching the clinical and imaging features.

<final>Pituitary macroadenoma</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Five days after an uncomplicated embolisation of an asymptomatic, growing right middle cerebral artery (MCA) aneurysm, a 56-year-old woman comes to the ER due to headache, a seizure and fluctuant left upper extremity weakness and left campimetric defects. A Todd’s paralysis is suspected, and the patient is admitted for a workup.
 Imaging Findings: Initially, a brain CT is performed, which shows no significant abnormalities apart from the embolisation material (Figure 1).
 
 Then a brain MRI is performed, including the following sequences: DWI (Figures 2a and 2b), SWI (Figure 3), T2, FLAIR (Figure 4), 3D TOF and pre and post-gadolinium T1 (Figures 5a and 5b). The MRI discloses multiple round lesions varying in size in the right hemisphere. The lesions are spontaneously T1 hypointense and T2 hyperintense, the largest ones showing a small, central T2 hypointensity. Post-gadolinium enhancement is demonstrated in all the lesions. No restricted diffusion or susceptibility artefacts are present.
 
 Follow-up imaging after 6 months (Figures 6, 7, 8a, and 8b) shows overall improvement with resolution of the enhancing foci. However, T2 hyperintensity is still present, and central punctate susceptibility artefacts have appeared, interpreted as microhaemorrhagic foci.

- Initial CT negative except for embolisation material; MRI performed.  
- MRI shows multiple round lesions in the right hemisphere, spontaneously T1 hypointense and T2 hyperintense.  
- Some lesions have a small central T2 hypointense focus.  
- All lesions show post-gadolinium enhancement.  
- No diffusion restriction (no DWI abnormality) and no susceptibility artifacts on initial MRI.  
- Follow-up MRI after 6 months: overall improvement, resolution of enhancement, persistent T2 hyperintensity, and new central punctate susceptibility artifacts (microhaemorrhagic foci).  

B) Candidate pass/fail with 1–2 pros/cons each:  
1) Non-ischemic cerebral enhancing (NICE) lesions  
- Pros: This is a broad category that often includes various enhancing lesions not related to ischemia, particularly after an embolisation procedure. The lack of diffusion restriction and enhancement patterns fits NICE lesions' variable radiographic manifestations.  
- Cons: The term "NICE lesions" is very broad and nonspecific, but the key is to match the pattern to known entities under this umbrella.  

2) Subacute cerebral infarcts  
- Pros: Subacute infarcts can enhance, show T2 hyperintensity, and may have corresponding clinical deficits.   
- Cons: Lack of diffusion restriction on DWI argues against acute or subacute infarction (typically would see restriction), and there are multiple multiple lesions rather than a single vascular territory. Also, the patient had an embolisation procedure, but no evidence of new infarctions; plus the central T2 hypointensity is atypical.  

3) Cerebral microabscesses  
- Pros: Microabscesses can appear as multiple round lesions with enhancement and T2 hyperintensity.  
- Cons: Usually associated with diffusion restriction and may have surrounding edema. The absence of diffusion restriction here argues against infection/abscess. Also, no fever or infection signs reported, and the patient had trauma-related, not infectious, history.  

4) Brain metastases  
- Pros: Can present as multiple enhancing lesions.  
- Cons: Typically show diffusion restriction if necrotic, often show a heterogeneous ring enhancement pattern. Also, unlikely given the patient’s recent embolisation and lack of known primary tumour; improvement over 6 months also less typical.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks: 
1) Non-ischemic Cerebral Enhancing (NICE) lesions: It captures a broad pattern including post-embolisation changes, with multiple enhancing lesions, T2 hyperintensity, lack of diffusion restriction, and the clinical context of recent embolisation. The evolution without restriction and later microhemorrhagic foci also fits a benign, resolving, non-ischemic process.
2) Subacute cerebral infarcts: No diffusion restriction, and distribution/multiple lesions argue against.
3) Cerebral microabscesses: No infection signs or DWI restriction, making it less likely.
4) Brain metastases: No known primary, and improvement over time is unlikely.  

In conclusion, the pattern fits best with Non-ischemic cerebral enhancing (NICE) lesions.  
</analysis><final>Non-ischemic cerebral enhancing (NICE) lesions</final><|return|>

- Pros: Microabscesses can appear as multiple round lesions with enhancement and T2 hyperintensity.  
- Cons: Usually associated with diffusion restriction and may have surrounding edema. The absence of diffusion restriction here argues against infection/abscess. Also, no fever or infection signs reported, and the patient had trauma-related, not infectious, history.  

4) Brain metastases  
- Pros: Can present as multiple enhancing lesions.  
- Cons: Typically show diffusion restriction if necrotic, often show a heterogeneous ring enhancement pattern. Also, unlikely given the patient’s recent embolisation and lack of known primary tumour; improvement over 6 months also less typical.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks: 
1) Non-ischemic Cerebral Enhancing (NICE) lesions: It captures a broad pattern including post-embolisation changes, with multiple enhancing lesions, T2 hyperintensity, lack of diffusion restriction, and the clinical context of recent embolisation. The evolution without restriction and later microhemorrhagic foci also fits a benign, resolving, non-ischemic process.
2) Subacute cerebral infarcts: No diffusion restriction, and distribution/multiple lesions argue against.
3) Cerebral microabscesses: No infection signs or DWI restriction, making it less likely.
4) Brain metastases: No known primary, and improvement over time is unlikely.  

In conclusion, the pattern fits best with Non-ischemic cerebral enhancing (NICE) lesions.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 75-year-old female presented with a four-day history of sudden onset frontal headache, vomiting, ophthalmalgia, photophobia and blurred vision. Her past medical history included migraines, right-sided phacoemulsion and an intraocular lens implant six years ago. Fundoscopy demonstrated choroidal folds and effusions. Laboratory blood workup revealed only elevated serum ACE levels.
 Imaging Findings: Initial computerised tomography (CT) head imaging and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the head were unremarkable. Specifically, CT did not identify any orbital pathology. MRI of the orbits revealed bilateral effusions in the suprachoroidal space, with low signal intensity on T1 sequences (Figure 1) and high-intensity signal on T2 sequences (Figure 2). Findings were more pronounced on the right side. Effusions extended anteriorly past the ora serrata. Contrast-enhanced sequences with fat suppression visualised choroidal and scleral enhancement (Figure 3). There was no evidence of intraocular mass or retinal detachment.
 
 A subsequent high-resolution CT chest scan revealed biapical scarring, mild bronchial wall thickening and subtle fissure nodularity in the upper zones (Figure 4) and a 10 mm subcarinal lymph node (Figure 5). Thoracic imaging findings were non-specific and equivocal for sarcoidosis.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 13-year-old boy presented with pain over the left hip for the last 3 years and a gradually enlarging mass for the last 2 years. Upon physical examination, the left hip range of motion was reduced. Lab tests showed normal calcium, high serum phosphorous of 7.07 Eq/dL, and normal parathormone levels.
 Imaging Findings: A radiograph of the left hip demonstrated multilobulated periarticular calcifications (Figure 1). MRI (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, and 3e) showed a well-defined, lobulated lesion affecting the glutei, vastus lateralis, and obturator externus muscles. Multiple fluid-calcium levels, termed the “sedimentation sign”, were noted, with calcifications appearing hypointense on both T1 and T2-weighted sequences, and fluid appearing hypointense on T1-weighted and hyperintense on T2-weighted sequences. These lesions exhibited heterogeneous contrast enhancement. CT imaging revealed a multilobulated calcific mass without bone or joint involvement (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 Histopathological examination (Figures 4a and 4b) confirmed the diagnosis of tumoral calcinosis, revealing fibro-collagenous tissue with granular calcifications arranged in lobules with surrounding multinucleated giant cells, foamy macrophages, lymphocytes, and plasma cells.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 73-year-old woman presented with abdominal pain, mild jaundice and dark urine for 3 days. Initial blood tests revealed anaemia and elevation of serum creatinine, total bilirubin, and lactate dehydrogenase. Further analytical evaluation excluded autoimmune and microangiopathic haemolytic anaemias. Flow cytometry analysis of peripheral blood showed a deficiency of GPI-anchored proteins. Abdominal ultrasound did not reveal any significant findings, so an abdominal magnetic resonance (MRI) was subsequently conducted.
 Imaging Findings: MRI excluded acute biliary pathology but revealed abnormal bilateral renal cortical hypointensity on both T2 (Figure 1) and T1-weighted (Figure 2) sequences. In gradient-recalled echo (GRE) imaging, the renal cortex demonstrated a relative increase in signal intensity on the out-of-phase sequence (Figure 3) compared to the in-phase sequence (Figure 2). Reduced cortical enhancement was observed after intravenous contrast administration (Figures 4a, 4b, and 4c). The spleen and liver showed no significant parenchymal signal abnormalities (Figure 5). Isolated renal haemosiderosis was presumed.
 
 Abdominal ultrasound and computed tomography did not reveal significant abnormalities.

A) Key findings:  
- 73-year-old woman with abdominal pain, mild jaundice, dark urine, acute rise in serum creatinine, bilirubin, and LDH.  
- Absence of autoimmune and microangiopathic hemolytic anemia. Presumably, a hemolytic process is ongoing (elevated LDH, bilirubin).  
- Flow cytometry reveals deficiency of GPI-anchored proteins ==> classic for paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria (PNH), as GPI-anchored complement decay-accelerating factor deficiency is hallmark.  
- MRI brain: Abnormal bilateral renal cortical hypointensity on T1 and T2.  
- GRE imaging: renal cortex increased signal intensity on out-of-phase vs in-phase => suggests abnormal intracellular iron content (since iron causes T2 shortening and is out-of-phase signal change).  
- Reduced renal cortical enhancement after contrast: renal hypoperfusion or dysfunction, possibly due to microvascular obstruction or hemoglobinuria.  
- No significant findings in liver or spleen.  
- Abdominal ultrasonography and CT negative.

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  

1. Transfusion-related hemosiderosis  
- Pass: MRI signs of iron deposition (cortical hypointensity, GRE signal changes) can be seen.  
- Con: No history of transfusions mentioned; kidney location would be unusual; hemosiderosis generally affects liver, spleen, bone marrow; no parenchymal abnormalities there.  

2. Mechanical valvular hemolysis  
- Pass: Hemolytic anemia with bilirubin and LDH elevation; possible hemoglobinuria.  
- Con: No mention of heart murmurs or valvular disease; GPI-anchored protein deficiency not explained. Negative spleen/liver findings and MRI kidney abnormalities are not typical.  

3. Paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria (PNH)  
- Pass: Deficiency of GPI-anchored proteins on flow cytometry is pathognomonic.  
- Pass: Clinical triad of hemolysis (jaundice, bilirubin, LDH), dark urine (hemoglobinuria), renal dysfunction (creatinine rise).  
- Pass: Renal cortical siderosis due to chronic hemoglobinuria leading to iron deposition matches MRI findings (hypointensity, GRE changes).  
- Con: MRI findings of renal cortex iron deposition are supportive but not exclusive; however, correlation with GPI deficiency and clinical presentation is strong.  

4. Chronic hemolytic anemia  
- Pass: Chronic hemolysis can lead to iron deposition (hemat hemosiderosis).  
- Con: No mention of chronic anemia, no transfusion dependency; GPI deficiency and acute renal dysfunction less typical; kidney findings not specific.  

5. Hemochromatosis  
- Pass: Iron overload causing organ deposition with MRI hypointense signals.  
- Con: Typically involves liver, pancreas, spleen, and heart; no liver/spleen findings here; clinical context lacks iron overload signs; GPI deficiency unrelated.  

C) Ranking:  
1. Paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria  
2. Transfusion-related hemosiderosis  
3. Chronic hemolytic anemia  
4. Mechanical valvular hemolysis  
5. Hemochromatosis  

The combination of GPI protein deficiency on flow cytometry, clinical hemolysis findings, and MRI evidence of renal cortical iron deposition is pathognomonic for PNH.  

</analysis><final>Paroxysmal nocturnal haemoglobinuria</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old male with a history of glioblastoma, submitted to surgical resection followed by Stupp protocol 11 years ago, and organic psychosis, with therapeutic noncompliance and multiple hospitalisations in recent years, presented to the emergency department with acute onset gait disturbance, right-sided muscle weakness, numbness and behavioural changes.
 Imaging Findings: A head CT performed upon admission revealed a status post left parietal craniotomy, with a cortico-subcortical temporoparietal surgical site exhibiting gliosis, stable when compared to previous imaging. No new hypodensities suggestive of stroke were observed, and CT angiography excluded arterial occlusion.
 
 Subsequent brain MRI T1-weighted post-contrast images demonstrated
 
 de novo
 
 cortical gyriform enhancement in the previously treated surgical site, specifically in the temporoparietal region, with extension into the occipital, insular and remaining temporal lobe. Perfusion arterial spin labelling (ASL) study indicated increased cerebral blood flow (CBF) in the affected area. Importantly, there were no signs of acute stroke nor tumour progression, as evidenced by the absence of restricted diffusion, an extension of the high T2 FLAIR signal or nodular enhancement.

Subsequent brain MRI T1-weighted post-contrast images demonstrated de novo cortical gyriform enhancement in the previously treated surgical site, specifically in the temporoparietal region, with extension into the occipital, insular and remaining temporal lobe. Perfusion arterial spin labelling (ASL) study indicated increased cerebral blood flow (CBF) in the affected area. model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>   
A) Key findings:  
- 45-year-old male with a past history of glioblastoma treated surgically and with Stupp protocol 11 years ago.  
- Acute onset gait disturbance, right-sided muscle weakness, numbness, and behavioral changes.  
- CT: Chronic postoperative changes with gliosis; stable, no new hypodensities indicating stroke.  
- MRI T1 post-contrast: New de novo cortical gyriform enhancement in the left temporoparietal region, including occipital, insular, and remaining temporal lobes.  
- ASL perfusion: Increased cerebral blood flow (CBF) in the region of enhancement.  
- No restricted diffusion or nodular enhancement on MRI to suggest acute infarction or tumor recurrence.  
- No signs of tumour progression based on stable gliosis and lack of nodular enhancement or diffusion restriction.  

B) Pass/fail with pros/cons:  
1) Tumour recurrence  
- Pro: History of glioblastoma, postoperative changes in the same surgical site.  
- Con: No nodular enhancement, no restricted diffusion or new lesion suggestive of tumor; stable imaging otherwise.  
2) Left hemisphere stroke  
- Pro: Acute onset right-sided neurological deficits.  
- Con: No hypodensities on CT, no diffusion restriction on MRI; arterial occlusion excluded, no acute stroke signs.  
3) Organic psychosis exacerbation  
- Pro: Patient has known organic psychosis and non-compliance.  
- Con: Does not explain cortical gyriform enhancement or increased CBF on imaging.  
4) SMART syndrome  
- Pro: History of prior brain irradiation/treatment, acute or subacute neurologic deficits, characteristic cortical gyriform enhancement in post-treatment glioma site; increased perfusion; absence of tumor recurrence or stroke findings; involvement of posterior regions including occipital, temporal, and parieto-temporal regions commonly described.  
- Con: None significant.  
5) Post-ictal changes  
- Pro: Some enhancement can be seen post seizure.  
- Con: No restricted diffusion, typically no extensive cortical gyriform enhancement or hyperperfusion in such widespread pattern; clinical picture less compatible.  
6) Posterior reversible encephalopathy  
- Pro: Acute neurological signs.  
- Con: Usually seen in diffuse posterior white matter edema; typically no gyriform enhancement; no clinical context of hypertension or toxic etiology.  

C) Ranking:

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 3-month-old girl was brought to casualty with a history of intermittent fever for 1 month, abdominal distension, and straining while passing urine and stools for 4 days. On examination, a soft, tender, non-mobile midline mass was palpated in the suprapubic region.
 Imaging Findings: Transabdominal greyscale ultrasound revealed a large, well-defined cystic lesion in the pelvis with dense mobile echoes (Figure 1). The mass was seen posterior to the bladder and anterior to the rectum and was extending into the abdominal cavity up to the umbilicus. The cystic structure was in continuity with the stretched uterine cavity (Figure 2). Suprapubic probe tenderness was positive. There was no evidence of any solid component or calcifications within. Both the ovaries were seen separately from this lesion. Bilateral kidneys showed mild hydronephrosis. Bowel loops were normal in course, calibre, and peristalsis and were displaced to the periphery. On transperineal ultrasound examination, there was a thin membrane of 1 mm thickness at the vaginal orifice (Figure 3).

A) Key findings:

- Ultrasound shows a large, well-defined cystic pelvic lesion with dense mobile echoes.  
- The mass is posterior to the bladder, anterior to the rectum, and extends to the umbilicus.  
- Continuity of cystic structure with stretched uterine cavity.  
- No solid components or calcifications within the mass.  
- Both ovaries seen separately from the lesion.  
- Bilateral mild hydronephrosis due to mass effect.  
- Normal bowel loops displaced peripherally.  
- On transperineal ultrasound, a thin membrane (1 mm) at the vaginal orifice.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  
1. Pyometrocolpos:  
   Pros: Age-appropriate for congenital obstructive lesions; cystic pelvic mass continuous with uterus; presence of vaginal membrane suggests imperforate hymen causing obstruction and accumulation of secretions; fever and mass effect (hydronephrosis) explained.  
   Cons: Dense mobile echoes could suggest infected or hemorrhagic content but not exclusive.  

2. Hematometrocolpos:  
   Pros: Similar presentation to pyometrocolpos but with hemorrhagic content.  
   Cons: In a neonate/young infant, hemorrhagic collection rare; no history of trauma or bleeding disorders; no mention of echogenic clot material typical for blood; no adnexal cysts or endometriosis.  

3. Hydrometrocolpos:  
   Pros: Fits with clear or thin secretions accumulations; presence of cystic lesion continuous with uterus; imperforate hymen or vaginal membrane consistent with cause; fits signs and imaging.  
   Cons

4. Distended urinary bladder:  
   Pros: Midline cystic mass in pelvis.  
   Cons: The mass is posterior to the bladder, rather than filling the bladder; continuity with uterine cavity suggests uterine origin; also bladder distention alone wouldn't cause hydronephrosis in setting of normal voiding (though severe outflow obstruction might, but less likely with normal external genitalia findings).  

5. Ovarian neoplasm:  
   Pros: Pelvic cystic mass possible.  
   Cons: Ovaries seen separately; mass continuous with uterine cavity rather than adnexal; ovaries not origin; patient age extremely young for ovarian neoplasm; no solid components or features to suggest neoplasm.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Pyometrocolpos: Classic presentation of imperforate hymen causing accumulation of mucus/urine/secretions distending uterus and vagina; confirms with vaginal membrane finding and mass continuity with uterine cavity.  
2) Hydrometrocolpos: Same as above but emphasizes fluid content; essentially clinical and imaging overlap, but pyometrocolpos with imperforate hymen specifically fits best.  
3) Hematometrocolpos: Less likely due to age and lack of classic hemorrhagic features.  
4) Distended urinary bladder: Anatomically inconsistent with imaging findings.  
5) Ovarian neoplasm: Least likely due to location and separation from ovaries.  

Overall, pyometrocolpos best explains the cystic pelvic mass continuous with the uterine cavity, imperforate vaginal membrane, mass effect causing hydronephrosis, fever (infection risk), and the age and presentation.  
</analysis><final>Pyometrocolpos</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A middle-aged patient presented with what were initially suspected to be new-onset migraines but then developed auditory and visual hallucinations and complex paranoid beliefs. They became confused and intermittently incoherent with deteriorating coordination, becoming unsteady and sustaining several falls. The clinical picture was further complicated by the onset of myoclonus and a rapidly progressive cognitive decline, which raised suspicion for a neurodegenerative condition.
 Imaging Findings: The patient initially underwent a CT which showed only generalised cerebral atrophy.
 
 They then had further investigations with an MRI, including diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) and corresponding apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) images. This revealed widespread restricted diffusion involving almost the entire cortex except the superior temporal gyrus and also affecting the caudate bilaterally and the left lentiform nuclei. Fluid attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) imaging showed a more subtle high signal in these areas. Other than some small and non-specific foci of high signal in the deep white matter, there were no further positive findings.

A) Key findings:

- CT showed generalized cerebral atrophy.  
- MRI DWI and ADC showed widespread cortical restricted diffusion sparing the superior temporal gyri, with bilateral caudate nuclei and left lentiform nuclei involvement.  
- FLAIR showed subtle high signal in the same areas.  
- No focal lesions beyond generalized atrophy and small nonspecific white matter hyperintensities.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Hypoxic encephalopathy  
   - Pros: Diffuse symmetric diffusion restriction in cortex and basal ganglia is typical.  
   - Cons: Usually involves the cerebellar hemispheres and basal ganglia more symmetrically; no clear hypoxic event mentioned clinically.

2) Hyperammonaemia (Hepatic encephalopathy)  
   - Pros: Diffuse cortical changes possible.  
   - Cons: Typically shows high T2/FLAIR signal in cingulate gyrus with less diffusion restriction; patient clinical history does not indicate liver failure.

3) Hypoglycaemic encephalopathy  
   - Pros: Can show diffuse cortical and basal ganglia diffusion restriction.  
   - Cons: No clinical or lab evidence of hypoglycemia; typically see symmetric involvement, often without severe basal ganglia changes.

4) Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease  
   - Pros: Classic MRI findings include diffuse cortical (cortical ribbon) diffusion restriction and basal ganglia involvement; sparing of superior parietal and occipital cortices; rapid progression with myoclonus, hallucinations, and worsening dementia is typical.  
   - Cons: None significant; most consistent clinical and imaging features.

5) Encephalitis  
   - Pros: Can cause cortical and basal ganglia diffusion restriction.  
   - Cons: Usually more focal or asymmetrical patchy changes; no infectious symptoms or pleocytosis mentioned; DWI changes in CJD are more characteristic.

C) Ranking:  
1) Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease (fits clinical progression and characteristic MRI with diffuse cortical and basal ganglia restricted diffusion sparing superior temporal gyri).  
2) Hypoxic encephalopathy (diffusely symmetric changes possible but clinical history not fitting).  
3) Hypoglycaemic encephalopathy (less likely without hypoglycemic event).  
4) Encephalitis (less suggestive given clinical scenario and imaging pattern).  
5) Hyperammonaemia (least likely given no liver dysfunction).  

Twin rank tie between 1 and 2; clinical history and superior temporal gyrus sparing favors CJD.  
Sanity check: CJD is most consistent with hallmark imaging findings and rapid neurodegeneration with myoclonus.

<final>Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 41-year-old woman presented with worsening perineal pain and pustular skin lesions. Examination revealed extensive erythematous and pustular lesions in the perineal and perianal regions, with rope-like dermal thickening and induration. Multiple fistulous openings with purulent and haemoserous discharge were noted.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound of the perineal and perianal regions revealed dermal and hypodermal band-like structures indicative of fistulas/tunnels, with multiple dermal collections around hair follicles containing hyperechoic hair fragments (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c). Some hypodermal fluid collections were connected to the epidermis via sinus tracts (Figure 1d). Power Doppler showed increased vascularity, suggesting active inflammation (Figure 1e).
 
 MRI demonstrated skin thickening at the perineum and labia majora, hypertrophic scarring, and deep subcutaneous fistulas/tunnels extending into the ischioanal space and perianal region (Figures 2a and 2b), with left adductor muscle involvement suggestive of myositis (Figure 2c). The tunnels exhibited post-contrast rim enhancement, indicating active inflammation, but no fistulas communicating with the anal canal lumen were identified.
 
 After immunomodulatory therapy, significant lesion reduction was observed, with resolution of the fistulas and myositis. Residual skin thickening and scarring were noted, with minimal post-gadolinium enhancement, indicating minor active inflammation (Figures 3a and 3b).

   - Pros: Typical disease for apocrine gland-bearing areas like perineum/perianal region; presents with painful, erythematous, and pustular lesions; sinus tracts and fistulas are common.    - Pros: Group of skin and subcutaneous tissue infections with nodules, discharge.
   - Cons: Localized nodule infections, not involving deep tissue sinus tracts or fistulas; no mention of multiple, rope-like dermal thickening, or myositis; no mention of imaging findings typical for carbuncles.

1) Hidradenitis suppurativa (most consistent: typical lesions, sites, sinus tracts, scarring, fistulas without anal communication, hair follicle involvement, myositis as a severe complication)

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 52-year-old male presented to the outpatient clinic with swelling over the right greater trochanteric region, which progressively grew after a fall four months before. He had a previous history of neoadjuvant radiotherapy followed by resection of a soft tissue sarcoma in the left thigh seven years before.
 Imaging Findings: On the preoperative MRI performed seven years before (Figures 1, 2a, 2b, and 2c), an intramuscular lobulated mass was seen in the left tensor fascia lata, extending into the subcutis. The mass showed areas of high signal on T1WI (Figure 2a), which became hypo-intense after fat suppression (Figure 2b). The mass was heterogeneous, predominantly hyperintense on T2WI (Figure 2b) and showed internal areas of heterogeneous enhancement after gadolinium contrast (Figure 2c).
 
 The current MR study revealed a thick-walled, well-defined lesion superficial to the right tensor fascia lata, with heterogeneous high signal on T1WI (Figures 3a and 3b) and T2WI (Figures 4a and 4b), persisting after fat suppression. Multiple internal fluid-fluid levels were present. While post-contrast T1WI sequences (Figure 5) showed intralesional heterogeneous high signal, the dynamic subtraction sequence (Figure 6), only showed a thick peripheral enhancing rim. Therefore, the pseudoenhancement seen on the static sequences was due to a haemorrhage-related high native T1WI signal.

   - Pros: Morel-Lavallée lesions often develop after trauma (this patient had a fall); they can have fluid-fluid levels due to blood or seroma; they often have a persistent fat signal after fat suppression because of hemosiderin or fat; they can have thick walls and can be subcutaneous or superficial to muscles.

   - Pros: Vascular lesion with potential of hemorrhagic components.

- Angiosarcoma and metastasis are less likely without solid enhancing nodules or aggressive features.  

Hence, most consistent diagnosis is Morel-Lavallée lesion in a patient with previous myxoid liposarcoma.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 63-year-old female, with a history of anterior cervical diskectomy and fusion, had a CT head and cervical spine in 2007 for unrelated symptoms. Incidentally, imaging identified a heavily calcified lesion in the right cerebellar hemisphere. The patient remained asymptomatic and, therefore, opted for regular follow-up imaging as opposed to surgical management.
 Imaging Findings: The CT brain demonstrated a 2.5 cm (maximal diameter), highly calcified intra-axial lesion with no surrounding oedema or mass effect, within the right cerebellar fossa (Figures 1 and 2).
 
 The MRI demonstrated the same lesion within the right cerebellar hemisphere, measuring 2.5 cm (maximal axial measurement). The lesion abutted the inferior tentorial surface; however, a broad base against it was not exhibited. There was no notable mass effect or surrounding oedema. On T1 (Figure 3) and T2 (Figure 4) weighted images, the lesion displayed a low signal intensity. Additionally, blooming artefacts were observed on gradient imaging (Figure 5).
 
 During the 17-year follow-up period, the patient exhibited no symptoms. Biennially, MRI heads revealed no change in the lesion size or associated mass effect.

A) Key findings:
- 63-year-old female with a history of anterior cervical diskectomy and fusion.
- Incidentally found heavily calcified intra-axial lesion in right cerebellar hemisphere.
- Lesion size 2.5 cm, heavily calcified, no surrounding edema or mass effect.
- No bright or dark signal change to suggest necrosis, cavitation, or blood products.
- On MRI: low signal on T1/T2, blooming artifact on GRE (susceptibility, suggests calcification or blood products).
- Longest follow-up of 17 years with stable lesion size and no symptoms.
- Lesion abutted inferior tentorium but no broad dural base.

B) Candidate pass/fail:
- Calcified meningioma:
  Pros: common extra-axial calcified lesion; often adjacent to dura; can abut the tentorium.
  Cons: No broad dural base noted; intra-axial location is atypical; no mass effect or edema; extremely stable over 17 years without surgical indication (though asymptomatic meningiomas can be stable).
- Calcified cavernoma:
  Pros: intra-axial location; calcification and blooming artifact common due to hemosiderin; no edema could be present.
  Cons: Typically cavernomas have surrounding gliosis or microhemorrhages that cause susceptibility effects and sometimes edema; low signal on T1/T2 consistent, but more typical with hemosiderin and T2* blooming due to blood product. Highly calcified cavernomas are less common.
- Calcified pseudoneoplasm of the neuraxis (CAPNON):
  Pros: Intra-axial calcified lesion; can be heavily calcified; typically stable over time; often asymptomatic; no mass effect or edema.
  Cons: Rare; generally can be in multiple locations; less common in cerebellum but possible.
- Oligodendrogliomas:
  Pros: Can be calcified and intra-axial.
  Cons: Typically would cause mass effect and some edema; would not be highly calcified exclusively; patients often symptomatic; usually larger and progressive.
- Choroid plexus papilloma:
  Pros: Can be calcified; intraventricular/extraventricular.
  Cons: Usually intraventricular lesion; not intra-axial in cerebellar hemisphere; rare in cerebellum; not typical for this location.

C) Ranking:
1. Calcified pseudoneoplasm of the neuraxis: fits intra-axial, heavily calcified, longstanding stable lesion, no symptoms, no mass effect. While rare, fits well.
2. Calcified cavernoma: intra-axial lesion, calcification and blooming artifact likely due to blood products, but would typically have some gliosis or microbleeds. Lack of edema/heterogeneity and stability favor CAPNON over cavernoma.
3. Calcified meningioma: location is intra-axial and no dural base, less likely.
4. Oligodendroglioma: less likely due to massive calcification and no symptoms or progression.
5. Choroid plexus papilloma: location mismatched.

Therefore, favored diagnosis is Calcified pseudoneoplasm of the neuraxis.
</analysis>

<final>
Calcified pseudoneoplasm of the neuraxis
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 78-year-old male patient with a history of alcoholism and smoking was referred to the otorhinolaryngology department for dysphagia. Clinical examination revealed a painless 4 cm thickening lesion of the uvula. Cervical palpation revealed no adenopathy. A biopsy of the lesion was performed.
 Imaging Findings: CT scan revealed the presence of a uvula tumour (Figure 1), a suspicious hepatic lesion and a solid cystic lesion in the body of the pancreas. MRI showed a pancreatic body lesion, measuring 22 mm, with cystic and tissue components, intensely enhanced after contrast injection (Figure 2), and a 55 mm heterogeneous hepatic mass, of the segment IV, intensely enhanced after contrast injection (Figure 3). There was no dilatation of the pancreatic or biliary ducts. We performed an FDG PET-CT, which demonstrated an intensely hypermetabolic uvula (SUVmax at 9.2), with moderate hypermetabolism of the adenopathies (SUVmax at 2.5) and the liver lesion (SUVmax at 5.4) (Figures 4a, 4b, and 4c).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 47-year-old female patient presented at the Emergency Department with severe acute abdominal pain associated with nausea and vomiting. She also referred cessation of flatus and bowel movements for 4 days. Past medical history is unremarkable. On physical exam, the abdomen was distended and tender with no rebound or guarding.
 Imaging Findings: A plain erect abdominal radiograph revealed the presence of an air-distended large bowel loop in the left upper quadrant consistent with the inverted cecum, and multiple fluid levels (Figure 1).
 
 An abdominal contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CT) was later performed, with coronal and sagittal reconstructions (Figures 2, 3a, 3b, 4a, 4b, and 5). On CT images, there was a markedly distended large bowel loop in the left upper quadrant with air-fluid level, measuring a maximum calliper of 7.5 cm, adjacent to a small blind-ending tubular structure compatible with the ileocecal appendix, consistent with the inverted cecum. A clockwise whirl sign composed of spiralled loops of collapsed cecum and ileum, with twisting of central fatty mesentery with enhancing distended vessels, was present (Figures 4a and 4b). The gradual tapering of the obstructed cecum at the site of the whirl resulted in a stenosis, with the appearance of a bird’s beak (Figure 5). There was also dilation of small bowel loops, filled with fluid. There were no signs of bowel perforation, as well as no signs of pneumatosis intestinalis or other signs of vascular compromise.

A) Key findings:  
1. Acute abdomen with severe abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and obstipation.  
2. Plain radiograph: air-distended large bowel loop in left upper quadrant, suggesting displacement of the cecum (inverted cecum).  
3. Contrast-enhanced CT: marked dilatation of cecum and proximal ileum with air-fluid level; location in left upper quadrant; collapsed cecum and ileum forming a clockwise whirl sign with twisting of mesenteric vessels; "bird’s beak" tapering at the volvulus site; dilation of fluid-filled small bowel loops.  
4. No signs of perforation, pneumatosis intestinalis, or vascular compromise.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1. Sigmoid volvulus:  
   - Pros: Common cause of colonic volvulus; can present with volvulus signs.  
   - Cons: Inverted cecum and whirl sign involving cecum and ileum do not fit sigmoid volvulus anatomy; sigmoid would be in lower abdomen/pelvis, not left upper quadrant.

   - Pros: Key finding of inverted cecum in left upper quadrant, whirl sign involving cecum and ileum, bird's beak tapering at obstruction site, dilated cecum and ileum.  
   - Cons: None significant; fits perfectly with findings.  
3. Small bowel/large bowel mechanical obstruction:  
   - Pros: Dilated bowel loops, obstruction signs.  
   - Cons: Mechanical obstruction is broad and does not specify the exact pathology or mechanism; whirl sign and bird’s beak are more specific to volvulus; cecal inversion not explained.  
4. Intussusception:  
   - Pros: Telescoping bowel can cause obstruction signs.  
   - Cons: Typically shows characteristic "bowel-in-bowel" or "target"/"sausage" sign on CT, not whirl sign; intussusception of cecum and ileum less common in adults; not consistent with bird’s beak anatomy.

2) Small bowel/large bowel mechanical obstruction (less specific).  
3) Sigmoid volvulus (less anatomically consistent).  
4) Intussusception (unlikely with absence of classic imaging features).  
5) Paralytic ileus (contradicted by specific signs of volvulus).  

Therefore, the best diagnosis is cecal volvulus.  
</analysis>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Our patient is a 64-year-old male admitted to the intensive care unit (ICU) due to a prolonged cardiac arrest, who was on mechanical circulatory support with extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO). He was COVID-19-negative and presented with an encephalopathic syndrome.
 Imaging Findings: A brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was performed, and the susceptibility-weighted imaging sequence (SWI), revealed multiple small hypointense foci consistent with cerebral microbleeds (CBMs). These lesions were diffusely distributed across the subcortical white matter, splenium of the corpus callosum and posterior fossa, affecting the brainstem (midbrain and pons), as well as both cerebellar hemispheres. The cortex and periventricular regions were spared (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c). In addition, we found small watershed infarctions (Figures 2a and 2b).
PostDescription:

DifferentialDiagnosisList: Critical illness-associate cerebral microbleeds, Hypertensive encephalopathy, Cerebral amyloid angiopathy, Fat-emboli, COVID-19
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 64-year-old male, prolonged cardiac arrest, ICU, on ECMO, encephalopathy.  
- MRI SWI: multiple small hypointense foci = cerebral microbleeds.  
- Distribution: diffusely across subcortical white matter, splenium of corpus callosum, posterior fossa (brainstem - midbrain and pons, cerebellar hemispheres).  
- Cortex and periventricular regions spared.  
- Small watershed infarctions.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Critical illness-associate cerebral microbleeds  
   - Pros: Seen in critically ill patients, especially on ECMO, with microbleeds in corpus callosum, brainstem, cerebellum; sparing cortex and periventricular white matter is classic.  
   - Cons: None significant; fits well.  
2) Hypertensive encephalopathy  
   - Pros: Hypertension can cause microbleeds, often in basal ganglia, brainstem, thalami.  
   - Cons: Distribution here does not match classic hypertensive pattern (cortex and periventricular spared, involvement of corpus callosum and cerebellum less typical), also patient on ECMO, more suggestive of critical illness.  
3) Cerebral amyloid angiopathy (CAA)  
   - Pros: Can cause lobar microbleeds, often in cortex and subcortical U-fibers.  
   - Cons: Distribution typically cortical/subcortical, sparing deep white matter and infratentorial structures; not fitting the described pattern well.  
4) Fat-emboli  
   - Pros: Fat emboli can cause microhemorrhages, typically in watershed zones, and pulmonary embolic phenomena.  
   - Cons: Fat emboli usually present in trauma or orthopedic patients; no trauma history; typical distribution differs; patient on ECMO after cardiac arrest, not typical.  
5) COVID-19  
   - Pros: COVID-19-associated microbleeds have been reported in severe cases, sometimes with posterior predominant involvement.  
   - Cons: Patient was COVID-19-negative; pattern not typical for COVID-19 encephalopathy microbleeds which often involve superficial cerebral hemispheres and corpus callosum; patient had no infection.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Critical illness-associate cerebral microbleeds (most consistent with patient details and imaging pattern).  
2) Fat-emboli (less likely due to lack of trauma and typical distribution).  
3) Hypertensive encephalopathy (distribution less typical).  
4) Cerebral amyloid angiopathy (distribution atypical, and patient age and lack of typical cortical involvement reduce likelihood).  
5) COVID-19 (negative test and clinical context do not support).  

The distribution of SWI microbleeds (splenium, brainstem, cerebellum) in a severely ill patient on ECMO with subsequent encephalopathy fits the classic pattern of critical illness-associated cerebral microbleeds.  

</analysis>

<final>Critical illness-associate cerebral microbleeds</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 20-year-old woman presented with progressive shortness of breath and chest pain for 3 days. She was diagnosed with thrombosis involving the left popliteal vein to the common femoral vein with the possibility of a slow-flow vascular malformation of the left popliteal region. She was referred to our institute for further management.
 Imaging Findings: USG (ultrasonogram)
 
 : Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) involving the left popliteal vein (Figure 3a), superficial and common femoral veins (Figure 3b) with heteroechoic lesion around the popliteal vessels (Figure 3c).
 
 CTLL angiogram (computed tomography lower limb)
 
 : A lobulated heterogeneously enhancing lesion (Figure 4a) in the distal left thigh and proximal leg (intramuscular and intermuscular plane), extending into the popliteal vein (Figure 4b), superficial femoral, common femoral and common iliac veins (Figure 4c).
 
 CTPA (computed tomography pulmonary angiogram)
 
 : Eccentric filling defect involving the main and right pulmonary arteries (Figure 1a), extending to the right interlobar artery (Figure 1b), right basal trunk (Figure 1c), and segmental branches of the right upper lobe and left basal trunk [1]. Thrombus noted in the right atrium and ventricle. Multiple cavitary consolidations involving bilateral lower lobe segments (Figure 2).
 
 PET-CT (positron emission tomography – computed tomography)
 
 : High-grade metabolic activity in the left popliteal and thigh lesion (Figure 5b) and in the deep venous tumour thrombus (Figure 5a), bilateral pulmonary arteries (Figure 6b), right atrium (Figure 6a) and ventricle.
PostDescription:

 Imaging Findings: USG (ultrasonogram): Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) involving the left popliteal vein, superficial and common femoral veins with a heteroechoic lesion around the popliteal vessels. CTPA (computed tomography pulmonary angiogram): Eccentric filling defect involving the main and right pulmonary arteries, extending to the right interlobar artery, right basal trunk, and segmental branches of the right upper lobe and left basal trunk [1]. Thrombus noted in the right atrium and ventricle. Multiple cavitary consolidations involving bilateral lower lobe segments. PET-CT (positron emission tomography – computed tomography): High-grade metabolic activity in the left popliteal and thigh lesion and in the deep venous tumour thrombus, bilateral pulmonary arteries, right atrium and ventricle.

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>   
A) Key findings:   
- Young adult female (20 years old) with progressive dyspnea and chest pain.   
- DVT extending from left popliteal to common femoral veins, with a heteroechoic lesion around popliteal vessels on USG.   
- CT angiogram: lobulated heterogeneously enhancing lesion in distal left thigh and proximal leg (intramuscular and intermuscular), extending intraluminally into the popliteal, femoral, common femoral, and common iliac veins.   
- Pulmonary emboli throughout right pulmonary artery tree, with thrombus in the right atrium and ventricle, multiple cavitary lung consolidations.   
- PET-CT: high-grade metabolic activity within the soft tissue lesion and venous thrombus, involving pulmonary arteries and heart.   
- Imaging suggestive of a mass invading or occupying the deep venous system with tumor thrombus extending to the heart and pulmonary arteries.  
- Age and sex: young female, middle-aged tumor typical for intravascular sarcoma.

B) Candidates:   
1) Primary intravascular synovial sarcoma  
   - Pros: Rare tumor that can arise in the veins; typical intravascular location, often in young adults; heterogeneously enhancing soft tissue with intravascular extension; FDG-avid lesion on PET; tumor emboli in the heart and lungs is classical.  
   - Cons: Very rare entity, but matches demographics and imaging features well.  

2) Slow flow vascular malformation  
   - Pros: Imaging can show heterogeneous mass with slow flow and possible thrombosis.  
   - Cons: Slow flow vascular malformations typically enhance slowly and are less aggressive, rarely cause tumor thrombus or high FDG uptake; no typical tumor emboli expected.  

3) Leiomyosarcoma  
   - Pros: Aggressive malignant tumor; can arise from blood vessels; intravascular extension possible; FDG-avid.  
   - Cons: Usually occurs in older patients; commonly in retroperitoneal or large vessels like IVC; intramuscular presentation in lower limb less common.  

4) Vascular leiomyomatosis  
   - Pros: Benign smooth muscle proliferation within veins; intravascular extension possible; can extend into heart and lungs.  
   - Cons: Typically lower FDG uptake; benign nature does not align with intensely FDG-avid lesion and mass effect; usually occurs in premenopausal women, but the imaging features more aggressive here.  

5) Tumour thrombus (non-specific)  
   - Pros: Metabolically active clot fits with intravascular mass and emboli.  
   - Cons: Does not explain primary lesion in limb nor enhancement characteristics; less specific.  

6) Lymphoma  
   - Pros: Highly FDG-avid, aggressive; can involve veins as tumor thrombus.  
   - Cons: Less likely to present as lobulated mass intramuscular/intravisceral venous involvement of extremities; typical locations and patterns different.  

C) Ranking:   
1) Primary intravascular synovial sarcoma  
   - Most pathognomonic because of age, deep venous involvement with tumor thrombus, FDG avid mass, and emboli pattern.  
2) Leiomyosarcoma  
   - Similar features but less typical site and age.  
3) Vascular leiomyomatosis  
   - Benign, but with aggressive imaging features less likely.  
Others less compatible with imaging and clinical context.  
</analysis>

<final>Primary intravascular synovial sarcoma</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 26-year-old male with severe haemophilia A presented to the emergency department with a month-long history of haematuria, dysuria, and lower abdominal pain. The patient is an active smoker and reported a weight loss of 20 kg over the last year. The patient admitted recreational use of ketamine.
 Imaging Findings: Initial ultrasound imaging revealed a low bladder volume with symmetrical bladder wall thickening (Figure 1a) and bilateral hydronephrosis.
 
 CT intravenous urography (IVU) demonstrated mucosal enhancement and confirmed symmetrical bladder wall thickening, forming the classic “hourglass bladder deformity” (Figures 1b and 1c). Additionally, ureteral wall thickening, proximal urinary tract dilation, and hydronephrosis were observed (Figure 2).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 27-year-old male with progressive imbalance while walking and generalised tonic-clonic seizures from 5 years of age. No significant family history. Neurological examination revealed lower limb spasticity, brisk reflexes, extensor plantar reflex, features of ataxia, impaired pain, and touch sensations. Ophthalmic and fundus examinations were normal.
 Imaging Findings: MRI brain revealed the atrophy of the superior cerebellar vermis and the atrophy of the superior cerebellar cortex. The inferior vermis and the inferior cerebellar cortex are spared (Figures 2 and 3). T2/FLAIR linear hypointense stripes in the pons at the level of superior and middle cerebellar peduncle giving a tigroid/striped appearance (Figures 1a and 1b). Atrophy of the cervical and thoracic spinal cord is seen (Figure 3). Mildly atrophic pre-central and post-central gyri (Figure 4) with the thinned-out body of corpus callosum (Figure 2) also seen.

Cons: Spanning cerebellar and spinal cord involvement fits well; no conflicting data.

Pros: Typically involves spinocerebellar tracts, spinal cord atrophy, ataxia, corticospinal tract signs possible.  
Cons: MRI rarely shows tigroid pons or specific superior vermis atrophy; cerebellar atrophy more symmetric and diffuse rather than selective superior vermis involvement; no mention of cardiac involvement or other classic Friedreich features.

Pros: Can present with cerebellar ataxia and spastic features.  
Cons: Imaging typically not reported to have tigroid pons or selective superior vermis involvement; less common pattern; no mention of characteristic findings.

1) Autosomal recessive spastic ataxia of Charlevoix–Saguenay (ARSACS): The precise imaging findings match the known pattern for ARSACS (superior vermis atrophy, pontine stripes, spinal cord and corticospinal tract involvement).  
2) Friedreich’s ataxia: General ataxia and spinal cord atrophy fit, but lacking specific imaging hallmarks of ARSACS.  
3) Polymerase gamma-related ataxia: Rare, less specific imaging pattern fits worse.

The diagnosis with pathognomonic imaging findings is Autosomal recessive spastic ataxia of Charlevoix–Saguenay.
