OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 53-year-old male patient presented to our outpatient clinic with persistent dull pain in the right upper abdomen. Physical examination revealed a flat and soft abdomen with mild tenderness in the right upper quadrant.
 Imaging Findings: The patient had complete imaging data, including CT, MR, and PET-CT. All imaging examinations showed a mass in segment VII/VIII of the liver.
 
 Unenhanced CT scan showed that the lesion was low-density, with a CT value of about 23 HU, linear calcification at the edge, and mild shrinkage of the liver capsule. After enhancement, the lesion did not enhance, and the surrounding liver parenchyma was mildly enhanced in the arterial phase (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d).
 
 The mass contains mixed low signal on T1-weighted images, hyperintense on T2-weighted images and T2-weighted fat-suppressed images (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d). Coronal T2-weighted fat-suppressed images and coronal venous-phase T1-weighted fat-suppressed images showed that the intrahepatic bile duct beside the mass is dilated and connected to the lesion; the edge of the lesion and the adjacent bile duct showed mild enhancement (Figures 3a and 3b). Diffusion-weighted image (DWI, b = 800 mm2/s) showed a slightly higher signal (Figure 4a). The ADC map does not show restricted diffusion (Figure 4b). Similar to the CT-enhanced images, on T1-weighted enhanced arterial phase images, it can also be seen that the liver parenchyma around the lesion is slightly enhanced (Figure 4c).
 
 PET-CT showed a slight increase in FDG metabolism (Figures 5a, 5b, 5c, and 5d).

A) Key findings:  
- 53-year-old male with persistent dull pain in the right upper abdomen.  
- Imaging: Low-density liver lesion on CT (23 HU) in segments VII/VIII with peripheral linear calcification and mild liver capsule shrinkage.  
- No enhancement on contrast CT; mild enhancement of adjacent liver parenchyma in arterial phase.  
- MRI: Mixed low T1 signal, hyperintense T2 and T2 fat-suppressed images.  
- Intrahepatic bile duct dilatation adjacent to the lesion communicating with the mass; mild enhancement of lesion edge and adjacent bile duct.  
- Slightly hyperintense on DWI, but no ADC restriction (no true restricted diffusion).  
- PET-CT: Slightly increased FDG uptake.  
Overall features suggest a mass with mild enhancement, adjacent bile duct involvement, and slightly increased metabolism without overtly aggressive features. Mild shrinkage of liver capsule may suggest chronicity or fibrosis.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  
1) Hepatic cholangiocarcinoma  
- Pros: Involvement and communication with intrahepatic bile duct, peripheral calcification, mild enhancement (cholangiocarcinomas often show progressive enhancement).  
- Cons: Cholangiocarcinomas often show more aggressive enhancement patterns and diffusion restriction; lesion did not enhance strongly, only mild perilesional parenchymal enhancement noted.  
2) Primary hepatic adenosarcoma  
- Pros: Rare hepatic tumor; may show mixed solid and cystic components, and sometimes bile duct involvement.  
- Cons: Usually large, heterogenous mass with solid and cystic areas; no mention of cystic components; lack of prominent enhancement and typical imaging is not supportive.  
3) Undifferentiated carcinoma (sarcomatoid carcinoma)  
- Pros: Rare, aggressive, may involve bile ducts, often presents as hypodense mass with little enhancement.  
- Cons: Typically shows aggressive imaging findings, infiltration, restricted diffusion; our case lacks restricted diffusion and marked enhancement.  
4) Hepatic fibroadenoma  
- Pros: Benign liver lesion, often with mixed T1/T2 appearance and possibly fat.  
- Cons: Rare in liver, typically no bile duct communication or perilesional ductal dilation; no calcifications usually; fibroadenomas are more ovarian in nature.  

C) Ranking and tie-breaking:  
- Hepatic cholangiocarcinoma is most consistent with bile duct communication and mild enhancement.  
- Primary hepatic adenosarcoma is less likely due to lack of typical features.  
- Undifferentiated carcinoma lacks aggressive imaging signs typical for such tumors.  
- Hepatic fibroadenoma is least likely due to the bile duct involvement and vascular calcifications.  

Conclusion: Hepatic cholangiocarcinoma best fits the overall imaging findings with bile duct communication, peripheral calcification, mild enhancement, and FDG uptake.  
</analysis>  

<final>  
Hepatic cholangiocarcinoma  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 62-year-old male with anal swelling and bleeding of years of evolution. Physical examination revealed a mass of approximately 20 cm in perianal region, with several fistulous tracts covering anal and perianal area with purulent secretion.
 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced CT showed a voluminous low-density gluteal mass with areas of internal enhancement (Figures 1a and 1b). Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed a multiloculated, cauliflower-like 21 x 16 x 11cm gluteal mass. It extended through both ischioanal fossae and fistulised to the anal canal with two posterior tracts (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c). The lesion was hypointense with respect to the muscle in T1-weighted sequences and showed high signal intensity in T2, compatible with abundant myxoid-mucinous content. Contrast-enhanced fat-suppressed T1-weighted MR imaging showed linear and papillary mesh-like enhancing areas (Figure 2d).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 65-year-old male presented with abdominal distension and pain of 10 days’ duration. No vomiting or obstipation was seen.
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced CT (computed tomography) scan of the abdomen was performed. Small bowel loops appeared dilated with multiple air-fluid levels (Figures 1, 2a, and 2b). A transition zone was found on the right side of the urinary bladder. A closed-loop obstruction was found with distal ileal loops as content, causing indentation of the right superolateral wall of the urinary bladder (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). A few dilated loops show a small bowel faeces sign (luminal contents resembling colonic faecal matter with gas bubbles). The hernial sac was filled with a minimal amount of fluid. There was no abnormal enhancement of the small bowel loops within the hernial sac. No thickening of the bowel walls was noted. Large bowel loops were collapsed. The cecum appeared normal.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old presented with dysphagia and post-prandial vomiting since one month. She had a history of accidental corrosive ingestion 20 years back, for which she was managed surgically (an elective retrosternal oesophageal bypass surgery using a gastric pull-through procedure was performed). She underwent bougie dilatation of the esophagogastric anastomosis for similar presenting symptoms.
 Imaging Findings: Frontal chest radiograph showed a fairly well-defined, round soft tissue opacity in the retrocardiac region with a broad base towards the mediastinum (Figure 1). There were no calcifications or cavitation within the opacity. The lateral border of the descending thoracic aorta was obscured by the lesion. On esophagogram, the neo-oesophagus (pulled up stomach) was seen in the right paramedian retrosternal region (Figures 2a and 2b). There was no opacification of the native oesophagus. Unenhanced chest CT showed passage of oral contrast through the neo-oesophagus into the stomach. There was a well-defined fluid-filled elongated cystic lesion (max. diameter of 6.8 cm) in the posterior mediastinum, posterior to the heart and anterior to the descending thoracic aorta. It was communicating with the narrowed proximal and mid native oesophagus (Figures 3a and 3b).

   Pros: Cystic mediastinal lesion, well-defined
   Cons: Typically located adjacent to the native oesophagus, not communicating directly with narrowed oesophagus; patient has history of bypass surgery; communication with native narrowed oesophagus is unusual for duplication cyst; usually found on the right side, but antilogarithmic

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: We report a case of a 16-year-old patient living in a rural environment. The symptomatology goes back to 3 months with diffuse abdominal pain, distension, and with vomiting and fever 3 days before his admission to the ER. Clinical examination revealed a slight dullness of the flanks and hypogastrium.
 Imaging Findings: An abdominal ultrasound was performed and revealed multiple hepatic, splenic and peritoneal cystic lesions with large intraperitoneal effusion (Figure 1). Abdominal and pelvic CT scan in axial, sagittal and coronal sections showed multiple cystic lesions adherent to the peritoneal leaflets, falciform ligament, great omentum and mesentery (Figures 2 and 4). The largest cyst is located in the left iliac fossa, measuring 55.5 x 42 mm, containing some calcifications (Figure 3), and with a large intraperitoneal effusion. The CT scan also revealed hepatic and splenic cystic lesions.
PostDescription:

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 16-year-old patient from rural area.  
- Clinical symptoms: abdominal pain, distension, vomiting, fever.  
- Ultrasound shows multiple cystic lesions in liver, spleen, and peritoneum, with large intraperitoneal effusion.  
- CT shows multiple cystic lesions adherent to peritoneal leaflets, falciform ligament, omentum, mesentery.  
- Largest cyst in left iliac fossa (55.5 x 42 mm), contains calcifications and large ascites.  
- Hepatic and splenic cystic lesions present.  

B) Candidate evaluation:  
1) Mesenteric lymphangioma  
   Pros: Can present as multiple cystic lesions involving mesentery; common in pediatric patients.  
   Cons: Usually cystic lesions are thin-walled and do not contain calcifications; typically no hepatic or splenic involvement.  
2) Peritoneal hydatidosis  
   Pros: Multilocular cystic lesions in liver, spleen, and peritoneum; can have calcifications especially in cyst walls; endemic areas include rural environments.  
   Cons: More common in older patients, but can occur in teenagers; fits with intraperitoneal cystic lesions and calcifications.  
3) Mesenteric cyst  
   Pros: Cystic lesion in mesentery, usually singular or few; less likely to involve liver and spleen.  
   Cons: Usually does not cause multiple peritoneal cystic lesions involving liver and spleen; lack of calcifications common.  

C) Ranking and selection:  
- Peritoneal hydatidosis best fits the combination of hepatic, splenic, and peritoneal cysts, presence of calcifications within cysts, and clinical symptoms consistent with parasitic infection (abdominal pain, fever). Rural environment supports possible exposure to parasites.  
- Mesenteric lymphangioma lacks hepatic/splenic lesions and calcifications.  
- Mesenteric cyst does not match the extensive peritoneal involvement or hepatic/splenic lesions.  

Therefore, the best diagnosis is Peritoneal hydatidosis.  
</analysis>

<final>Peritoneal hydatidosis</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 71-year-old female presented with progressively worsening holocranial headache over 3 years, occasionally accompanied by photophobia and phonophobia, sometimes waking her at night, and refractory to mild analgesics. She denied visual symptoms, sinus surgery, or head trauma. Ear, nose, and throat (ENT) examination was unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: CT of the paranasal sinuses revealed complete opacification of the left sphenoid sinus by a hyperattenuating lesion, with moderate expansion and smooth thinning and remodelling of its osseous walls (Figure 1a). Calcifications were present within the lesion (Figures 1b and 3). A focal dehiscence of the sinus roof in close proximity to the anterior cranial fossa floor and sclerosis of the posterior and lateral walls of the sinus were seen (Figure 1b and 1c).
 
 On MRI, the lesion was iso to mildly hypointense on T1-W (Figure 2a) and markedly hypointense on T2-W (Figure 2b). Restricted diffusion was observed in the right sphenoid sinus on DWI/ADC (Figure 4a). Post-contrast T1-W imaging revealed a smooth peripheral rim of enhancement (Figure 5). Additionally, a thin dural enhancement was noted contiguous to the erosion of the sinus roof. The adjacent brain parenchyma appeared normal, with no signs of oedema or infiltration.
PostDescription:

Post-contrast T1-W imaging revealed a smooth peripheral rim of enhancement. Additionally, a thin dural enhancement was noted contiguous to the erosion of the sinus roof. The adjacent brain parenchyma appeared normal, with no signs of oedema or infiltration.

  - Pros: Hyperattenuating lesion with calcifications on CT, characteristic for mycetoma (fungus ball). The smooth peripheral rim enhancement and bone remodeling without aggressive invasion also fit.

2) Isolated chronic rhinosinusitis  
  - Pros: Chronic inflammation could cause sinus opacification.  
  - Cons: Chronic rhinosinusitis seldom produces hyperattenuation or calcifications, no restricted diffusion or bone erosion seen. Unlikely here.

  - Pros: Sinus expansion and bone remodeling could be mucocele.

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:

4) Isolated chronic rhinosinusitis (least likely given imaging findings).  

Select Sphenoid sinus mycetoma.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 32-year-old, 12-week postpartum female presented with an incidental finding of bilaterally enlarged ovaries on ultrasound. The patient was asymptomatic at the time of presentation, with no associated abdominal pain, fever, or systemic symptoms. There was no significant personal or family history of malignancy or other chronic illnesses.
 Imaging Findings: On the initial ultrasound, both ovaries were massively enlarged (right 444 cm³, left 120 cm³) and displayed a heterogeneous echo texture with hypervascularity. No significant free fluid or adnexal torsion was noted.
 
 On contrast-enhanced whole-body MRI, bilateral ovarian masses exhibited scattered bulky lesions with significant diffusion restriction and contrast enhancement, without signs of necrosis or haemorrhage (Figure 1). Additional bulky lesions were identified in the breasts, mesentery, intestinal serosa, peritoneum, and the wall of the terminal ileum.
 
 PET-CT demonstrated intense fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG) uptake (SUV max. 14 g/ml) in the same regions identified on MRI, confirming metabolic activity consistent with malignancy (Figure 2).
 
 Breast imaging (ultrasound and mammography) revealed numerous sharply circumscribed, hypervascular, hypoechoic masses in both breasts (Figure 3). The biggest lesion measured 140 cm³. Mammography confirmed these findings, identifying well-defined, radiodense masses without calcifications or architectural distortion (Figures 4a and 4b).
 
 Taking account of all the imaging features together, these lesions were categorised as BIRADS 5.

2) Burkitt lymphoma

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 9-year-old child presented with a complaint of intermittent lower abdominal pain and nocturnal enuresis for two months. There were no gastrointestinal issues, weight loss, asthenia, or lower urinary tract symptoms. The patient had no significant past medical history. During the physical examination, a palpable mass with a hard consistency and non-mobile nature was detected on the left flank. Laboratory studies were normal, including urine catecholamine levels. Tumour markers, including hCG, AFP, and CEA, were also assessed and found to be within normal ranges.
 Imaging Findings: Abdominal ultrasound revealed a solid mass inferior to the left kidney and anterior to the psoas muscle (Figure 1a). The mass was vascularised (Figure 1b), had smooth edges, was slightly heterogeneous, predominantly isoechogenic, with some echogenic foci inside, and measured 5 x 5 x 11 cm in the axial, anteroposterior, and longitudinal axes (Figure 1c).
 
 Further MRI studies (Figures 2a and 2b) showed a retroperitoneal mass inferior to the left kidney and between the left psoas and quadratus lumborum muscles. While there was no evidence of invasion into adjacent structures, it did result in anterior shifting of the psoas muscle. The lesion exhibited an intermediate and heterogeneous signal on T2-weighted images and a low signal on T1-weighted images (Figure 3), with no restriction on the diffusion study (Figures 4a and 4b). The dynamic study (Figures 5a, 5b, 5c, and 5d) presented heterogeneous and progressive uptake by the contrast product, being more evident in the later phase.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old male, with no prior relevant medical history, presents with atraumatic intermittent sharp pain in the lower segment of the right anterior thoracic wall. The symptoms began two months earlier when the patient started exercising (CrossFit), mostly triggered by training (especially abdominal crunches) whilst being asymptomatic in normal daily activities.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound imaging of the costochondral region of the patient showed a normal arrangement of ribs and rib cartilage while the patient was in a supine position (Figure 1). No abnormalities in bone or cartilage contours, nor any soft tissue lesions were identified.
 
 Dynamic ultrasound of the symptomatic area, performed while the patient replicated the movement that elicits pain (abdominal crunches), revealed hypermobility of the 9th rib cartilage, which moved upwards and became pinned beneath the 8th rib cartilage (Figures 2 and 3). This triggered the pain, with a popping sensation associated. When the patient returned to the supine position, the 9th rib returned to its normal position (Figure 4).

A) Key findings:  
- 39-year-old male with atraumatic intermittent sharp anterior thoracic wall pain, triggered by exercise (abdominal crunches).  
- Ultrasound in supine position shows normal ribs and rib cartilage anatomy with no abnormalities.  
- Dynamic ultrasound during painful movement reveals hypermobility of the 9th rib cartilage, moving upward and becoming pinned beneath the 8th rib cartilage with associated pain and popping sensation.  
- Pain resolves and 9th rib returns to normal position when supine.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Tietze syndrome  
   + Pro: Can cause costochondral junction pain.  
   - Con: Usually presents with swelling at the costochondral junction, which wasn't mentioned; static ultrasound showed normal anatomy; dynamic hypermobility not characteristic.  
2) Costochondritis  
   + Pro: Common cause of chest wall pain without swelling, often no abnormal imaging findings.  
   - Con: Does not typically involve dynamic hypermobility or popping of ribs; static ultrasound normal isn't sufficient; pain typically not reproduced with movement like described.  
3) Rib fracture  
   + Pro: Can cause localized chest pain.  
   - Con: No fracture seen on static ultrasound; ultrasound missed typical fracture signs such as discontinuity or deformity; pain is activity-related but not due to rib movement under adjacent ribs.  
4) Slipping rib syndrome  
   + Pro: Classic presentation includes hypermobility of costal cartilages, pain with movement, popping sensation; dynamic ultrasound can demonstrate hypermobility (exact site may be 8th-11th ribs, usually 8th-9th). No static abnormalities required; patient’s activity-triggered pain matches.  
   - Con: None significant.  
5) Pleuritic pain  
   + Pro: Chest wall pain, sharp pain with movement or breathing.  
   - Con: Not linked to rib cartilage movement; usually associated with respiration, not abdominal crunches; no imaging findings of pleural disease noted.  
6) Sternalis syndrome  
   + Pro: Sternalis muscle anomalies can cause anterior chest pain.  
   - Con: No mention of associated muscular findings; pain not related to rib cartilage movement; static and dynamic ultrasound findings not characteristic.

C) Ranking:
1) Slipping rib syndrome – most likely given motion-dependent pain and dynamic ultrasound showing the specific hypermobility of 9th rib cartilage under 8th rib cartilage with popping pain.
2) Costochondritis – common but no swelling, no static imaging abnormality, and not typical dynamic hypermobility.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 46-year-old woman was admitted with fever, night sweats, weight loss and abdominal pain for several months. Abdominal examination revealed tenderness in the lower quadrants. Past history included HIV infection under antiretroviral therapy, with viral load undetectable. The laboratory tests showed leukocytosis with neutrophilia and elevation of C-reactive protein.
 Imaging Findings: Abdominal and pelvic (suprapubic) ultrasound (US) revealed an intramural uterine leiomyoma and an intrauterine device (IUD) well placed in the endometrial cavity.
 
 Contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) revealed multiple areas of nodular thickening in the root of the mesentery and throughout the peritoneum (Figures 1 and 2). There are also signs of invasion of the abdominal wall. Small volume of ascites was identified in the parietocolic gutters and in the rectouterine pouch (Figures 3, 4, and 5).

- Cons: actinomycosis often mimics malignancy with infiltrative masses and abscess formation, and may present with extensive fibrosis and fat infiltration, which can match imaging findings of nodular thickenings and wall invasion.

C) Ranking and tie-breaks:  
1) Abdominopelvic actinomycosis: This diagnosis fits best with IUD presence, constitutional symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss), leukocytosis with neutrophilia, and CT findings of infiltrative nodular peritoneal disease with wall invasion. Actinomycosis is known for forming indurated masses and infiltrating fascia/muscle, matching the imaging findings and clinical presentation.

Clinical History: A 25-year-old female presents to the emergency department with disorientation. No neurological focality is observed. The patient has a history of migraine and blurred vision in the past year. No other relevant medical background.
 Imaging Findings: CT shows loss of grey-white matter differentiation in the left parietal lobe and hypointensity in the white matter (Figures 1a and 1b), which indicates a stroke lesion in the left parietal lobe. In CT with contrast (Figures 2a and 2b), stenosis of the right middle cerebral artery (arrow), a filiform course of the left middle cerebral artery and extensive collateral circulation in the region are seen (arrowheads).
 
 Angiogram of the left cerebral circulation (Figures 3a and 3b) confirms the presence of significant vascular collateralisation around the circle of Willis (arrows), giving the classic “puff of smoke” appearance. An irregular vascular course with multiple stenoses is also observed. The angiogram of the contralateral side (Figures 3c and 3d) shows similar findings.
 
 FLAIR-weighted (Figure 4a) and T1 post-contrast (Figure 4b) images show cortical hyperintensity and cortical enhancement, respectively, in the left parietal region (arrows).

An irregular vascular course with multiple stenoses is also observed. The angiogram of the contralateral side shows similar findings. A) Key findings:  
- Young female (25 years old) with acute neurological symptoms (disorientation).  
- Prior history of migraine and blurred vision.  
- CT findings of acute infarct (loss of grey-white matter differentiation) in left parietal lobe, with additional hypointensity in white matter suggesting ischemic stroke.  
- CT angiogram shows stenosis of bilateral middle cerebral arteries (bilateral involvement): stenotic right MCA and filiform course of the left MCA.  
- Extensive collateral vessels with "puff of smoke" appearance around circle of Willis on angiogram, classic for Moyamoya disease.  
- FLAIR and T1 post-contrast images show cortical hyperintensity and enhancement in the left parietal lobe, consistent with recent infarction and possibly reactive vascular changes.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  

1) Central nervous system vasculitides  
   Pros: Can cause strokes, vessel stenosis, and enhancement.  
   Cons: Usually no classic "puff of smoke" collateral circulation; systemic vasculitis often presents with systemic inflammatory signs; and bilateral symmetrical aneurysmal collaterals are less typical. Not the classic pattern.  

2) Systemic lupus erythematosus  
   Pros: Can cause vasculopathy and strokes.  
   Cons: No mention of systemic signs or any laboratory evidence; typical vascular findings don't include classical collateral formation; rare at this age as cause without systemic symptoms.  

3) Moyamoya disease  
   Pros: Classic bilateral stenosis of intracranial internal carotid arteries or their branches (here middle cerebral arteries) and "puff of smoke" appearance of collateral vessels around basal ganglia and circle of Willis.  
   Cons: Rare, but the exact finding fits perfectly, especially given bilateral disease, collateral network formation, and age/clinical presentation.  

4) Neurofibromatosis type I  
   Pros: Could involve vasculopathy, often in younger patients.  
   Cons: Usually also with clinical signs (café-au-lait spots, neurofibromas), not mentioned here; vascular changes more heterogeneous and less defined than here.  

5) Cranial radiotherapy  
   Pros: Can cause vasculopathy and strokes.  
   Cons: No history of prior cranial radiation; and angiographic findings do not match.  

6) Atherosclerosis  
   Pros: Can cause stenoses leading to strokes.  
   Cons: Typically older patients; collateral formation not classic; no mention of plaques; bilateral symmetrical stenoses less likely. Also not typical at this age with no risk factors.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
- Moyamoya disease is pathognomonic with "puff of smoke" bilateral stenoses and collaterals.  
- CNS vasculitis and systemic lupus are possible but less likely without systemic or inflammatory signs.  
- Neurofibromatosis type I, cranial radiotherapy, and atherosclerosis are less fitting.  

Therefore, Moyamoya disease is the best match, with classic imaging features, appropriate clinical age and presentation.  

</analysis><final> Moyamoya disease </final>
<|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 47-year-old man presented with a history of weakness in the right arm since a few weeks. There was a history of a road traffic accident two months back.
 Imaging Findings: MRI of the brachial plexus showed a pre-fixed plexus with supply from the C4 nerve root. There was diffuse oedema in the C4–C6 nerve roots with thickening of the C5 and C6 nerve roots and the superior trunk (Figures 1a, 1b, and 2c). The nerve roots were hyperintense on T2 sequences and bulky as compared to the contralateral side (Figures 2a and 2b). There was oedematous thickening of the right axillary nerve throughout its course with subacute denervation oedema in the deltoid and the teres minor muscles (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). Subacute denervation with grade I fatty infiltration is also noted in the supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 47-year-old man presented with a history of weakness in the right arm since a few weeks. There was a history of a road traffic accident two months back.
 Imaging Findings: MRI of the brachial plexus showed a pre-fixed plexus with supply from the C4 nerve root. There was diffuse oedema in the C4–C6 nerve roots with thickening of the C5 and C6 nerve roots and the superior trunk. The nerve roots were hyperintense on T2 sequences and bulky as compared to the contralateral side. There was oedematous thickening of the right axillary nerve throughout its course with subacute denervation oedema in the deltoid and the teres minor muscles. Subacute denervation with grade I fatty infiltration is also noted in the supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Traumatic brachial plexopathy, Brachial plexitis, Thoracic outlet syndrome, Cervical radiculopathy

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 47-year-old man with right arm weakness for a few weeks and a history of road traffic accident 2 months ago.
- MRI of brachial plexus showing a pre-fixed plexus, supplied by C4 nerve root.
- Diffuse edema and thickening of C4-C6 nerve roots, especially C5 and C6 roots and the superior trunk.
- Nerve roots hyperintense on T2 sequences and bulky compared to the contralateral side.
- Oedematous thickening of the right axillary nerve throughout its course with subacute denervation edema in deltoid and teres minor.
- Subacute denervation with grade I fatty infiltration in supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles.
- Muscle changes are subacute.

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) Traumatic brachial plexopathy
- Pros: History of recent trauma (2 months ago), involvement of brachial plexus nerve roots and segments (C5-C6 and superior trunk) with diffuse edema and thickening; subacute denervation changes in muscles innervated by these roots and axillary nerve.
- Cons: None significant; findings fit classic presentation.

2) Brachial plexitis (idiopathic or inflammatory)
- Pros: Brachial plexitis can cause edema and thickening of nerves, with denervation changes in muscles.
- Cons: Not accompanied by a clear cause or inflammatory markers; history of significant trauma points toward traumatic cause; distribution matches trauma rather than typical inflammatory plexitis which often involves multiple trunks or specific sites, not as focal as trauma.

3) Thoracic outlet syndrome
- Pros: Can involve neurogenic compression leading to plexus thinning/edema.
- Cons: Usually chronic presentation, clinical history of chronic pain, paresthesias, weakness; would not have diffuse nerve root edema with trauma history; MRI usually shows more compressive or compression-induced changes near the thoracic outlet.

4) Cervical radiculopathy
- Pros: Weakness and denervation changes in muscles innervated by cervical roots.
- Cons: Involves nerve root edema but not brachial plexus involvement; muscles involved extend beyond typical root distribution; history indicates brachial plexus involvement; MRI shows diffuse nerve root plus plexus findings, not typical for radiculopathy alone.

C) Ranking:
1) Traumatic brachial plexopathy - best matches trauma history, distribution, and MRI findings.
2) Brachial plexitis - plausible but less likely due to trauma history and site distribution.
3) Cervical radiculopathy - less specific due to plexus involvement and muscle pattern.
4) Thoracic outlet syndrome - least likely given acute trauma and MRI findings.

Final diagnosis: Traumatic brachial plexopathy

</analysis>

<final>
Traumatic brachial plexopathy
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 68-year-old male presented with a gradually enlarging swelling over the anterior aspect of the right ankle, first noticed two years earlier. He reported no pain, trauma, functional limitation, or gait disturbance. On examination, a ~5 cm, well-circumscribed, mobile, and non-tender soft tissue mass was observed, not adherent to deep planes and without skin changes or signs of inflammation.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound demonstrated a well-circumscribed soft tissue mass measuring 33 × 28 × 14 mm (L × AP × T) within the subcutaneous tissue, completely enveloping the tibialis anterior tendon. The lesion exhibited a heterogeneous echotexture, predominantly iso-echoic relative to the surrounding fat (Figures 1 and 2).
 
 Subsequent MRI revealed a fusiform, encapsulated mass located within the subcutaneous plane, anterior to the aponeurosis, and circumferentially enveloping the tibialis anterior tendon (Figure 3). The lesion appeared hyperintense on T1-weighted sequences with multiple internal hypointense septations (Figure 3), showed high-signal intensity on T2-weighted gradient-echo sequences (Figure 4), and demonstrated marked hypointensity on fat-suppressed proton density-weighted images (Figures 5 and 6). Across all sequences, the signal characteristics closely resembled those of subcutaneous adipose tissue, consistent with a lipomatous composition.
PostDescription:

 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound demonstrated a well-circumscribed soft tissue mass measuring 33 × 28 × 14 mm (L × AP × T) within the subcutaneous tissue, completely enveloping the tibialis anterior tendon. The lesion exhibited a heterogeneous echotexture, predominantly iso-echoic relative to the surrounding fat. Subsequent MRI revealed a fusiform, encapsulated mass located within the subcutaneous plane, anterior to the aponeurosis, and circumferentially enveloping the tibialis anterior tendon. The lesion appeared hyperintense on T1-weighted sequences with multiple internal hypointense septations, showed high-signal intensity on T2-weighted gradient-echo sequences, and demonstrated marked hypointensity on fat-suppressed proton density-weighted images. Across all sequences, the signal characteristics closely resembled those of subcutaneous adipose tissue, consistent with a lipomatous composition.

FinalDiagnosis: Lipoma of the tibialis anterior tendon sheath

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 68-year-old male with a gradually enlarging, painless, mobile, well-circumscribed subcutaneous swelling over the anterior right ankle.
- Ultrasound: well-circumscribed mass in subcutaneous tissue enveloping tibialis anterior tendon; heterogeneous echotexture, primarily isoechoic to fat.
- MRI: fusiform, encapsulated mass anterior to the aponeurosis, circumferentially enveloping tibialis anterior tendon.
- T1 hyperintensity with internal hypointense septations.
- T2 hyperintensity on gradient-echo sequences.
- Marked fat suppression on fat-suppressed proton density images, resembling subcutaneous adipose tissue.
- No signs of infiltration or adherence to deep structures.
- Lesion exhibits features consistent with lipomatous composition.

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:
1) Intramuscular or subfascial lipoma
   - Pros: Lipomatous lesion with encapsulation, well-circumscribed, non-adherent to deep planes.
   - Cons: Lipoma is intramuscular or subfascial, but here mass is subcutaneous and envelops the tibialis anterior tendon; intramuscular/subfascial locations less likely.

2) Lipoma of the tibialis anterior tendon sheath
   - Pros: Lesion is subcutaneous and directly envelops the tibialis anterior tendon; well-circumscribed and lipomatous.
   - Cons: No direct mention of sheath involvement rather than tendon itself; however, enveloping suggests sheath involvement.

3) Well-differentiated liposarcoma
   - Pros: Lipomatous lesion with focal septations, which can be seen in low-grade liposarcomas.
   - Cons: No infiltrative or aggressive behavior; no internal necrosis or irregular margins; low suspicion given clinical and imaging features; well-circumscribed and encapsulated; no pain or functional impairment.

4) Giant cell tumour of the tendon sheath
   - Pros: Commonly involve tendons, can be mobile and palpable.
   - Cons: Typically non-lipomatous; characterized by hemosiderin deposition, low to intermediate signal on T2, causing blooming artifact without high T1 signal; lesion here is hyperintense on T1 and fat-rich, inconsistent.

C) Ranking:
1) Lipoma of the tibialis anterior tendon sheath - fits location and composition best.
2) Intramuscular or subfascial lipoma - less precise site; mass is subcutaneous/enveloping tendon.
3) Well-differentiated liposarcoma - lack of aggressive features, low suspicion.
4) Giant cell tumour of the tendon sheath - inconsistent imaging characteristics.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 14-year-old male presented with progressive dysarthria, ataxia, gait disturbance, headache, dizziness, and a history of recurrent falls. Physical examination revealed hepatosplenomegaly without convulsions or neck rigidity. Liver enzymes were within normal limits.
 Imaging Findings: MRI of the patient revealed symmetrical abnormal T2WI and FLAIR hyperintensities in bilateral thalamus (with sparing of medial thalamic nuclei), bilateral caudate, putamen, claustrum, midbrain (with sparing of red nucleus and substantia nigra—“face of the giant panda” sign), pons and dorsal brainstem (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, 2, and 3). No evidence of blooming on FFE images or restriction on DWI and ADC maps. Abnormal T1WI hyperintensity noted within the globus pallidus (Figure 4).
 
 USG of the abdomen revealed altered echotexture of the liver with hepatosplenomegaly, consistent with chronic liver parenchymal disease /(Figure 5).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 14-year-old male presented with progressive dysarthria, ataxia, gait disturbance, headache, dizziness, and a history of recurrent falls. Physical examination revealed hepatosplenomegaly without convulsions or neck rigidity. Liver enzymes were within normal limits.
 Imaging Findings: MRI of the patient revealed symmetrical abnormal T2WI and FLAIR hyperintensities in bilateral thalamus (with sparing of medial thalamic nuclei), bilateral caudate, putamen, claustrum, midbrain (with sparing of red nucleus and substantia nigra—“face of the giant panda” sign), pons and dorsal brainstem. No evidence of blooming on FFE images or restriction on DWI and ADC maps. Abnormal T1WI hyperintensity noted within the globus pallidus. USG of the abdomen revealed altered echotexture of the liver with hepatosplenomegaly, consistent with chronic liver parenchymal disease.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Leigh syndrome, Wilson’s disease, Uremic encephalopathy, Hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy, Methanol intoxication
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 14-year-old male with neurodegenerative symptoms (dysarthria, ataxia, gait disturbance, headache, dizziness, recurrent falls).  
- Hepatosplenomegaly noted on physical exam and abdominal USG showing altered liver echotexture.  
- MRI findings:  
  - Symmetrical abnormal T2WI and FLAIR hyperintensities in bilateral thalamus (sparing medial thalamic nuclei), bilateral caudate, putamen, claustrum, midbrain (sparring red nucleus and substantia nigra)—exhibiting classic "face of the giant panda" sign, pons, and dorsal brainstem.  
  - No blooming on FFE images (no bleed or calcification) or diffusion restriction on DWI/ADC.  
  - Abnormal T1WI hyperintensity in globus pallidus.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  
1) Leigh syndrome  
   - Pros: Symmetrical basal ganglia and brainstem lesions; progressive neurodegeneration symptoms; typical age group.  
   - Cons: Usually associated with mitochondrial disorders without hepatosplenomegaly. No mention of lactate peaks on MR spectroscopy or typical brainstem lesions sparing specific nuclei.  

2) Wilson’s disease  
   - Pros: Hepatosplenomegaly with chronic liver disease findings; T2 hyperintensities in basal ganglia, thalamus, brainstem with sparing of red nucleus and substantia nigra (classic "face of the giant panda" sign); globus pallidus T2 and T1 changes are classic; age and neurologic symptoms fit well.  
   - Cons: No mention of other Wilson’s disease specific findings such as Kayser-Fleischer rings or ceruloplasmin levels, but imaging findings are pathognomonic.  

3) Uremic encephalopathy  
   - Pros: Brain involvement in systemic disease.  
   - Cons: No renal dysfunction reported; specific brain lesions are diffuse and not characteristic; no mention of basal ganglia or brainstem predilection or hepatosplenomegaly.  

4) Hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy  
   - Pros: Bilateral basal ganglia involvement can be seen.  
   - Cons: No history of hypoxic event; asymmetric involvement expected; typical areas include cortex and watershed zones rather than specific brainstem features.  

5) Methanol intoxication  
   - Pros: Basal ganglia lesions; could involve putamen and other deep gray nuclei.  
   - Cons: Clinical history lacking methanol exposure; typical symmetric putaminal necrosis, no globus pallidus T1 hyperintensity; no report of ocular symptoms; rare at this age.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Wilson’s disease (most pathognomonic findings with classic "face of the giant panda" sign and globus pallidus involvement plus hepatosplenic disease).  
2) Leigh syndrome (symmetrical basal ganglia and brainstem without liver disease).  
3) Methanol intoxication (basal ganglia lesions without specific T1 changes or clinical context).  
4) Uremic encephalopathy (lack of renal dysfunction, atypical lesions).  
5) Hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy (lack of precipitating event, less specific pattern).  

Conclusion: Wilson’s disease best fits the clinical and imaging findings.  
</analysis><final>Wilson’s disease</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 88-year-old patient presented with an incidental lung mass. A computed tomography-guided biopsy and a bronchoscopy were performed, but the results were inconclusive. Given the size and characteristics of the lesion, radiotherapy was considered. The patient was asymptomatic and exhibited a normal clinical examination. The third biopsy yielded diagnostic results.
 Imaging Findings: Conventional chest X-ray shows an enlarged right pulmonary hilum and magnification of the cardiac silhouette due to the superimposed right lower lobe mass (silhouette sign) (Figure 1).
 
 Computed tomography (CT) with a soft tissue window shows a right lower lobe mass with an approximate diameter of 38 x 36 x 66 mm, with ill-defined borders and calcifications within (Figure 2). The pulmonary window shows atelectasis adjacent to the mass, as well as bronchial contact without complete collapse (Figure 4). PET/CT (positron emission tomography) shows FDG (fluorodeoxyglucose) fixation in the lesion, suggesting malignancy (Figure 3).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: An 88-year-old patient presented with an incidental lung mass. A computed tomography-guided biopsy and a bronchoscopy were performed, but the results were inconclusive. Given the size and characteristics of the lesion, radiotherapy was considered. The patient was asymptomatic and exhibited a normal clinical examination. The third biopsy yielded diagnostic results.
 Imaging Findings: Conventional chest X-ray shows an enlarged right pulmonary hilum and magnification of the cardiac silhouette due to the superimposed right lower lobe mass (silhouette sign). Computed tomography (CT) with a soft tissue window shows a right lower lobe mass with an approximate diameter of 38 x 36 x 66 mm, with ill-defined borders and calcifications within. The pulmonary window shows atelectasis adjacent to the mass, as well as bronchial contact without complete collapse. PET/CT (positron emission tomography) shows FDG (fluorodeoxyglucose) fixation in the lesion, suggesting malignancy.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Malignant neoplasia, Metastatic, Rheumatoid nodules, Pulmonary amyloidosis, Fungal lesions, Lymphomas
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 88-year-old patient, asymptomatic.
- Incidental right lower lobe pulmonary mass, large (38 x 36 x 66 mm), with ill-defined borders and calcifications.
- Atelectasis on adjacent lung, but no complete bronchial collapse.
- Pulmonary hilum enlargement and magnification of cardiac silhouette due to mass.
- PET/CT shows FDG avid lesion, indicating metabolic activity suggestive of malignancy.
- Initially, biopsies were inconclusive; third biopsy confirmed diagnosis (specific diagnosis not given but implied malignant neoplasm given malignant features).

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:

1) Malignant neoplasia:
   - Pros: Large mass with ill-defined borders, FDG avid on PET, common in elderly. Calcifications can be seen in necrotic or mucinous adenocarcinomas or some sarcomas. Atypical lung mass in elderly is most likely malignant. Symptoms not needed for diagnosis; incidental lesions often malignant in this age group.
   - Cons: None significant.

2) Metastatic:
   - Pros: Multiple possible primary sites can present as lung metastasis; calcifications can be seen if the primary tumor calcifies (e.g., chondrosarcoma). Patient is elderly, but no known primary noted.
   - Cons: Usually multiple lesions or known primary; here, solitary lesion in right lower lobe without mention of other sites, less likely.

3) Rheumatoid nodules:
   - Pros: Can calcify and appear as solitary lung nodules.
   - Cons: Usually multiple nodules, patient lacks history of rheumatoid arthritis; no mention of joint symptoms or serology; FDG avidity less intense, and usually not so large or infiltrative.

4) Pulmonary amyloidosis:
   - Pros: Can present as nodules, sometimes calcified.
   - Cons: Rare; typically multiple, with different imaging characteristics; less likely in elderly; FDG uptake variable; not usually necrotic or aggressive.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 52-year-old woman presented to the emergency department with abdominal pain. The patient had a history of a complicated appendectomy several years prior. Surgeons requested a contrast-enhanced CT scan to clarify the diagnosis.
 Imaging Findings: The contrast-enhanced CT scan revealed dilated small bowel loops with an apparent calibre change in the lower quadrant of the abdomen (Figure 1). As no masses or bowel thickening were identified, the diagnosis was consistent with an incomplete small bowel obstruction, likely due to abdominal adhesions—a diagnosis of exclusion. The patient had a history of a complicated appendectomy several years prior.
 
 Additionally, the gastric wall showed thickened gastric folds (Figure 2). Subsequent gastroscopy confirmed the thickened folds, and biopsies were obtained (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). Histopathological examination revealed atrophic gastritis and tumoural cells positive for GATA 3, mammaglobin, and oestrogen receptors.
 
 Both conventional (Figures 4a and 4b) and contrast-enhanced mammography (Figures 5a and 5b) demonstrated bilateral diffuse masses with non-mass enhancement. Palpable areas were biopsied in the lower inner quadrant of the right breast—4 o’clock—(Figure 6a) and the junction of the outer quadrants of the left breast—9 o’clock—(Figure 6b). No axillary lymphadenopathy was detected (Figures 6c and 6d).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 13-year-old female came with complaints of a slowly growing suprapubic mass since a few months and lower abdominal discomfort. She was further advised to undergo ultrasound imaging and subsequently CT abdomen for renal anomaly, and MRI pelvis for further evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: MRI pelvis reveals a large tubular T1 hyperintense structure in the pelvis on the right side, consisting of haemorrhagic contents in an enlarged hourglass-shaped right uterine horn and cervix. The left uterine horn appears unremarkable. CT abdomen (Figures 1 and 2) reveals an empty right renal fossa with a cross-fused kidney seen on the left. No obvious free fluid or adnexal lesion is seen.

   - Pros: Classical triad includes uterus didelphys (double uterus with one obstructed hemivagina), ipsilateral renal agenesis, and obstructed hemivagina with hematocolpos (blood collection), matching the patient's enlarged, blood-filled right uterine horn and ipsilateral renal agenesis (empty right renal fossa).

4) Ovarian cystic mass  
   - Pros: Can show cystic lesions.  
   - Cons: No adnexal lesion seen; lesion is in the uterine horn and cervix, not the ovary; cystic ovarian masses are not typical for this presentation.

- Fourth: Ovarian cystic mass (fail) - no adnexal lesion seen; not likely.

Thus, the best diagnosis is OHVIRA / Herlyn–Werner–Wunderlich syndrome.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 34-year-old male with one week of sudden-onset fever, abdominal pain, cervicalgia, myalgia, palpitations and shortness of breath. Recent history of travel to Africa for 1 month, and medications for malaria prophylaxis and pre-exposure prophylaxis for HIV. On examination, he had a fever of 38,2°C, tachycardia and right lower quadrant abdominal tenderness. Initial workup was non-contributory, with normal CBC, BMP, chest x-ray, and abdomen ultrasound. With wide differentials, including endemic, infectious and neoplastic conditions, CT of the head, neck and chest was performed and was unrevealing. Serology and lumbar puncture were also performed and were unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed, and revealed a 3.7 x 3.1 x 5.1 cm, avidly enhancing paraduodenal mass with central calcification (Figures 1a and 1b), presumed to be an enlarged lymph node. Adjacent organs, including the bowel, were unremarkable. There was no lymphadenopathy elsewhere. An endoscopic ultrasound was done (Figures 2a and 2b) to obtain an aspiration biopsy that revealed B-cell follicles with no evidence of malignancy. After discussion, the patient opted for watchful waiting. A follow-up CT done after 4 months demonstrated no significant change in size or appearance of the mass. This was subsequently excised, and histopathology revealed the final diagnosis.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 75-year-old woman had unintended weight loss and anaemia for several months. There was no history of bowel or urinary changes, and an endoscopy was negative. A CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed to evaluate for malignancy.
 Imaging Findings: A CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed in a portal venous phase (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c). An ovoid, heterogeneous soft tissue mass with smooth, well-defined margins was present in the left upper mesorectal space, mildly displacing the rectum to the right. It measured 61 x 58 x 45 mm and did not contain cystic spaces or macroscopic fat. The mass did not herniate through the sciatic notch and did not appear along the expected path of a nerve or vasculature. Upon administration of IV contrast, the mass was hypervascular on the portal venous phase. There was free fluid in the pelvis, representing simple ascites, without evidence of complexity or active bleeding. No MRI was performed since the patient was lost to follow-up after her initial CT scan and biopsy.

- Angiosarcoma and desmoid less likely based on typical presentation and features.
- Other candidates less fitting based on morphology, location, or absence of hallmark features.

Final choice: Leiomyosarcoma.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 62-year-old woman with a 17-year history of diabetes mellitus presented to our institution with a 3-month history of progressive lower urinary tract symptoms, including urinary frequency, urgency, dysuria, and gross haematuria. Notably absent was acute right lower quadrant abdominal pain. After hospitalisation, it was found that there were pneumaturia and faeces-like changes in the urine (Figure 1).
 Imaging Findings: Transabdominal ultrasonography showed a gas-like strong echo and irregular hyperechoic stone-like mass with posterior shadowing in the bladder (Figure 2a). To exclude gynaecological diseases, a transvaginal ultrasound was performed. The right lateral wall of the bladder and the adjacent bowel were connected through a fistula. The fistula tract was about 3.8 cm long and 0.58 cm wide, characterised as a central hyperechoic and peripheral hypoechoic tract on sonogram (Figure 2b). These ultrasound findings supported the diagnosis of vesico-enteric fistula, but the specific part of the intestine could not be clarified.
 
 A computed tomography (CT) scan showed a small air-fluid level at the top of the bladder (Figure 3a). A tubular tract could be seen protruding from the right lateral wall of the bladder up to the neighbouring thickened bowel and this is filled with contrast-opacified urine (Figure 3b). A diagnosis of fistulous communication between the bladder and bowel was suggested following the findings on the excretory-phase CT scan. CT coronal scan showed faecal stones in the bladder (Figure 3c).
 
 Cystoscopy showed an oedematous right lateral wall of the bladder associated with inflammatory changes. It also revealed a fistulous tract opening with a diameter of 1 cm, with pus moss and yellow floc attached to the bladder mucosal surface. The yellow floc is the stone-like mass seen on the ultrasound. The cystoscope could be inserted via a sinus tract opening in the right lateral bladder wall, where the distal tip exhibited bifurcation. Despite attempts, the endoscope could only advance 2 cm beyond the entry point, with further progression prevented by this structural anomaly.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 68-year-old former smoker with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and hypothyroidism consulted for progressive exertional dyspnoea. Pulmonary function tests showed a restrictive pattern and reduced diffusing capacity of the lung for carbon monoxide (DLCO).
 Imaging Findings: High-resolution computed tomography (HRCT) of the chest with axial (Figures 1a and 1b), coronal (Figure 1c), and sagittal (Figure 1d) reconstructions demonstrated findings consistent with an interstitial lung disease. Bilateral involvement with a clear apicobasal gradient with extensive subpleural reticulation, traction bronchiectasis, and predominant honeycombing in the lower lobes was observed. Exuberant honeycombing was present, involving nearly the entire fibrotic pulmonary parenchyma (Figure 1b). A sharp and abrupt transition between normal and fibrotic lung parenchyma was noted in the craniocaudal direction (arrows in Figures 1c and 1d). The small areas of fibrosis of the upper lobes were limited to the anterior segments (arrowheads in Figures 1a and 1d).
 
 No evidence of superimposed infection or ground-glass opacities was revealed. No suspicious pulmonary nodules, masses, or mediastinal lymphadenopathy were identified. Mild dilation of the pulmonary arteries and a cardiac pacemaker were noted. No pleural or pericardial effusion was present.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 67-year-old woman with a longstanding history of rheumatoid factor-positive rheumatoid arthritis (RA) was being treated with subcutaneous methotrexate 10 mg. She went to the emergency department due to sudden dyspnoea and left chest pain.
 Imaging Findings: Chest X-ray showed a moderate left pneumothorax and a cavitated lesion in the subpleural region of the left upper lobe (LUL) (Figure 1a), which was present on a prior chest X-ray (Figure 1b). After a chest tube was placed (Figure 1c), the pneumothorax did not fully resolve, and symptoms persisted, prompting a CT scan.
 
 CT reported a left pneumothorax. The tube was correctly positioned. A cavitated nodule located in the LUL with communication to the pleural cavity was shown in axial (Figure 2a) and coronal planes (Figure 2b). Other broad-based nodules in the pleural surface were identified (Figure 2c). The rest of the parenchyma presented a slight thickening of the interlobular septa with a diffuse bilateral distribution, probably related to RA-associated interstitial lung disease.
 
 A subsequent revaluation, after reviewing the patient’s radiological history, concluded that the image of a cavitated nodule (Figures 2a and 2b) corresponded to the lesion visible in the previous X-ray (Figure 1b), and proposed the differential diagnosis between cavitated lung neoplasia, rheumatoid nodule and other cavitary lesions such as tuberculosis.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 40-year-old woman presented to the gastroenterologist with postprandial fullness and nausea persisting for one year, along with a 4 kg weight loss. She had no abdominal pain and reported normal faeces. Blood tests were normal.
 Imaging Findings: On ultrasound (Figure 1), a suspicion of a thickened stomach wall was raised.
 
 CT (Figures 2a and 2b) confirmed diffuse wall thickening and hyperaemic inner lining of the stomach with a small lumen. Lymphadenopathies were identified in the gastrohepatic and gastrocolic ligaments. No peritoneal metastases were observed on CT, but the lack of significant intra-abdominal fat limited definitive exclusion.
 
 During gastroscopy Figure 3), insufflation of the stomach was difficult, but no focal tumoral process was noted.
 
 Initial MRI (Figures 4a, 4b, and 4c) showed diffusion restriction of the stomach lining. Additionally, multiple subtle peritoneal, mesenteric, and serosal lesions were identified, which were confirmed via diagnostic laparoscopy. Follow-up MRI (Figures 5a and 5b) showed disease progression with increased wall thickening of the stomach in the antrum, pylorus, and duodenal bulb, as well as the development of bilateral ovarian lesions.

1) Metastases
- Pro: multiple peritoneal, mesenteric, serosal lesions and ovarian lesions can be metastases.
- Con: primary stomach wall thickening with diffuse mucosal hyperemia is less typical; no primary tumor found; age and clinical presentation less supportive; no definite primary primary tumor seen.

- Pro: gastric wall thickening with diffusion restriction on MRI could suggest lymphoma; lymph nodes present.
- Con: typically, lymphoma shows larger lymph nodes rather than small, and diffuse mucosal hyperemia less typical; no mention of nodular masses or typical lymphoma patterns.

3) Inflammation
- Pro: diffuse wall thickening and hyperemia could suggest gastritis.
- Con: would not cause lymphadenopathies and distant peritoneal spread; unlikely to cause ovarian lesions or weight loss.

4) Ménétrier disease
- Pro: gastric wall thickening and hyperemic mucosa align with characteristic excessive mucous cell hyperplasia in Ménétrier disease; typically involves enlarged rugal folds; weight loss and nausea are common; no focal tumor or lymphoma.
- Con: ovarian lesions and peritoneal spread not typical; but ovarian lesions could be related to inflammation or ascites in rare cases; however, peritoneal involvement is not classic.

- Pro: diffuse thickening of gastric wall (linitis plastica) is classic; small lumen, nodular infiltration, diffuse mucosal hyperemia; progression to adjacent duodenum and ovarian lesions (Krukenberg tumors) is typical; diffuse involvement fits. OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 62-year-old male patient presented to the emergency room with visual disturbances and right-hand weakness. He had a medical history of alcoholism, tobacco use, hyperlipidaemia, and traumatic splenectomy, and was hospitalised due to an ischemic stroke. He underwent routine diagnostics, where, from laboratory tests, only hyperlipidaemia was clinically significant.
 Imaging Findings: Routine abdominal ultrasound revealed an asymptomatic, well-defined, homogeneous, hypoechogenic mass measuring 46 x 36 mm in the 8th liver segment (Figure 1).
 
 On abdominal multidetector computerised tomography (MDCT), the mass was ill-defined and isodense to the surrounding liver parenchyma on unenhanced scans (Figure 2a). Following intravenous contrast, the lesion demonstrated increased attenuation (Figure 2b), whereas, in the latter phases, it was isodense with the surrounding liver parenchyma (Figure 2c). Other findings included two splenunculi, one near the lesser curvature of the stomach (Figure 2d) and one in the left subphrenic region (Figure 2e).
 
 Contrast abdominal MR scans confirmed a well-defined, slightly T2-hyperintense mass measuring 43 x 30 mm (Figure 3a). The mass showed poor uniform post-contrast enhancement in the arterial phase (Figure 3b), following washout in later phases (Figure 3c). The mass showed significant restricted diffusion on DWI (Figure 3d) and low ADC values (Figure 3e).

 Diffusion restriction on DWI with low ADC values (Figures 1d, 1e, 2d, 2e, 3d, 3e, 4d, and 4e).
 
 Additional involvement of the bilateral fronto-parieto-temporal cortex and corticospinal tracts involving the posterior limb of the internal capsule and midbrain.
 
 No post-contrast enhancement (Figures 6a and 6b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 64-year-old man with a history of smoking and occasional alcohol use presented with a foreign body sensation and odynophagia. Clinical examination showed a bulge on the right lateral pharyngeal wall with normal mucosa and palatine tonsil. Neurological exam and lab tests, particularly white blood cell count, SR and RCP, were unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: The patient underwent an MR scan showing a 20 x 35 x 43.5 mm cystic mass in the right retropharyngeal space (RPS) (Figures 1a and 1b), bulging the prevertebral muscles and impinging on the oropharynx, with leftward deviation and reduced airway patency. The mass was spontaneously hyperintense to muscle on T1W images (Figure 2), hyperintense on T2W images, with a thin septation on T2 FS images (Figure 3). There was no restricted diffusion on the ADC map (Figure 4), and no enhancement was noted on subtracted T1W images before and after gadolinium administration (Figures 5a and 5b). There was no oedema in the surrounding tissues, and no abnormalities were seen in the pharynx, larynx, oesophagus, thyroid, or cervical lymph nodes.
 
 A comparison with an MR scan from 20 years ago showed the lesion was already present but smaller (10 x 20 x 35 mm) (Figure 6). At that time, the MRI was performed for unrelated reasons, and the incidental retropharyngeal lesion was interpreted as a presumed vascular malformation. Since the patient was asymptomatic, follow-up observation and monitoring was implemented, but the patient was lost to follow-up after a few years.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 75-year-old male patient with a history of non-metastatic urothelial carcinoma and prostate cancer, both successfully treated surgically two years ago. During follow-up, a palpable, hard, and fixed cervical mass was detected. No history of neck trauma or radiation.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound showed a 6 cm solid and heterogeneous left mass with internal calcifications and intralesional vascularisation, not arising from the thyroid gland (Figures 1a and 1b). This mass was displacing and compressing the left internal jugular vein, which was still permeable (Figures 1c, 1d, and 1e). Findings were consistent with a calcified adenopathy.
 
 A subsequent non-contrast CT due to renal impairment revealed a solid left supraclavicular mass, with a heterogeneous density, irregular outlines, and thick calcifications. Thickening of the lateral-cervical ipsilateral musculature was also observed (Figures 2a and 2b). There was a pathological adenopathy with calcifications at the Va cervical level (Figure 2c).
 
 A core needle biopsy was performed, and histology reported infiltration of spindle-shaped, pleomorphic, and atypical cells, followed by the formation of an osteoid matrix and chondroid tissue (Figures 3a and 3b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A sixteen-year-old male patient presented with congenital deafness on the left side and normal hearing on the right side. There was a family history of deafness and hearing loss on the father’s side. He experienced relatively few difficulties in daily life. Audiometry showed near-total deafness on the left side and normal thresholds on the right.
 Imaging Findings: A 3T MR study of the temporal bones was performed. The thin, heavily T2-weighted images revealed a bud-like, cystic cochlea on the left side with absent normal turns, modiolus and interscalar septa (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c and 6). Additionally, there was no identifiable left cochlear nerve, and apart from the facial nerve, there was only one other nerve, smaller in calibre compared to the facial nerve, in the internal auditory canal, consistent with a hypoplastic vestibulocochlear nerve and cochlear nerve aplasia (Figures 3a, 4a, 4b, 4c, 4d, 4e, and 4f). The vestibular nerve did not bifurcate into a superior and inferior branch. The left inner ear canal was small in calibre as well, progressively narrowing from medial to lateral. The left vestibule appeared mildly globose (Figure 2). The left lateral semicircular canal exhibited focal signal loss, which could either be a true abnormality or an artefact. The inner ear structures and inner ear canal on the right side were normally developed (Figures 1a, 1b, 3b, 5a, 5b, 5c, 5d, 5e, and 5f).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 29-year-old male with a history of recurrent urolithiasis presented with persistent abdominal pain and unexplained fever. Blood tests revealed elevated inflammatory markers and impaired renal function.
 Imaging Findings: On the CT scan, the left kidney is markedly enlarged, and multiple kidney calculi, including a staghorn calculus, are identified within the calyces and renal pelvis (Figures 1a and 2a). Diffuse thinning of the renal cortex and non-homogeneous, poorly differentiated renal parenchyma, replaced by multiple hypodense fluid-filled cavities (mean attenuation ~ +11 HU) (Figure 1b), with rim enhancement (Figure 3), consistent with the “bear paw sign”, associated with minimal air-fluid levels. Fat-density areas (mean attenuation ~ -60 HU) within and adjacent to the fluid collections (xanthomas) are seen (Figure 1c). The renal pelvis and calyces are dilated. The perirenal fat stranding and thickening of both Gerota’s and Zuckerkandl’s fasciae are noted, indicating stage II–III inflammatory extension. The entire ureter shows diffuse wall thickening with fat stranding and linear calcifications in the mid-ureter (Figure 2b). On the delayed urographic phase, there is no contrast excretion, confirming impaired left kidney function (Figure 4a and 4b).

- Pro: fluid collections with rim enhancement; fever and inflammation.  
- Con: Typical abscesses: focal, not diffuse kidney involvement and multiple cavities throughout kidney; absence of described purulent collections; associated calculi and "bear paw sign" more typical of granulomatous disease; perinephric inflammatory changes but not typical of abscess.

- Pro: renal infiltration possible; homogeneous or variable enhancement.  
- Con: Lack of mass-effect, presence of calculi and typical inflammatory changes; "bear paw sign" not typical; lymphoma shows infiltrative mass/nodules, not fluid cavities with fat attenuation.

   → FAIL

2) Renal carcinoma (less specific and inconsistent with diffuse pattern)  
3) Renal tuberculosis (some features but lacking typical TB signs)  
4) Malakoplakia (rarer, atypical features)  
5) Abscess (focal disease unlikely to cause diffuse pattern and multiple cavities)  
6) Lymphoma (least compatible)

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 29-year-old female patient presented to the cardiology department with episodes of numbness in both her upper and lower extremities, which spontaneously resolved after 30 minutes. She also reported episodes of chest pain that she attributed to stress. Heart auscultation was normal. ECG showed diffuse ST-elevations.
 Imaging Findings: Echocardiography (ECG) demonstrated severe systolic heart failure, with a left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) of 30%. The exam showed basal hypokinesia and preserved apical contractility, along with irregularities in the lining of the left ventricular myocardium (Figure 1).
 
 A contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) scan revealed no coronary abnormalities but confirmed the irregularities in the left ventricle wall (Figure 2).
 
 Subsequently, a cardiac MRI was performed, which revealed moderate left ventricle dilation and irregularities in the ventricle wall, including protrusions of myocardial bridges, multiple crypts, and thinning of the inferolateral apex with diffuse dyskinesia (Figures 3 and 4). Severe left ventricular dysfunction was confirmed, with an LVEF of 20% and a global longitudinal strain (GLS) of -7.85%, as well as ventricular dyssynchrony. A non-ischemic fibrosis region was also present in the interventricular septum and the posterior hinge point. The right ventricle appeared to be normal in morphology and function.

- RV normal in morphology and function.  

Key points: severe LV dysfunction, basal hypokinesia with apical sparing (suggesting an inverted Takotsubo pattern or a specific cardiomyopathy), multiple crypts, myocardial bridges, focal fibrosis in septum and hinge points, and a distinctive pattern of wall irregularities.

   - Pros: Irregular LV wall, multiple crypts, myocardial trabeculations, LV dysfunction.  
   - Cons: No specific mention of noncompacted to compacted ratio or layered trabeculations typical for LVNC; protruding myocardial bridges and fibrotic scars are not classic.  

2) Saw-tooth cardiomyopathy  
   - Pros: Wall irregularities, myocardial crypts, fibrosis, severe LV dysfunction, typical patterns reported.  
   - Cons: Rare entity; specific diagnostic criteria not well standardized but fits the unusual wall irregularities and fibrosis.  

3) Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM)  
   - Pros: Can show fibrosis and crypts.  
   - Cons: No mention of hypertrophy or asymmetric septal hypertrophy; LV dysfunction with basal hypokinesia and apical sparing is atypical; no reported LV hypertrophy.  

4) Arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy (ACM)  
   - Pros: Fibrosis, wall abnormality, LV dysfunction possible.  
   - Cons: Usually involves RV dysfunction or RV wall abnormalities; here RV is reported normal; typical fat infiltration and RV changes absent.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Saw-tooth cardiomyopathy – most consistent with wall irregularities, multiple crypts, focal fibrosis, severe LV dysfunction with apical sparing.  
2) Left ventricular noncompaction – strong association with multiple crypts and LV dysfunction but less specific wall protrusions and fibrosis.  
3) Arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy – less likely given normal RV.  
4) Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy – less likely due to lack of hypertrophy and classic HCM findings.  

Hence, the most appropriate diagnosis given the findings is Saw-tooth cardiomyopathy.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 6-month-old infant presented with a history of on-and-off soft tissue swelling over the left cheek since 2 months (Figure 1). No history of trauma. Full-term normal delivery.
 Imaging Findings: Head and neck CT with bone reconstruction showed cortical irregularity with sclerosis, solid periosteal reaction, increase in cortical width and density, seen along the angle and ramus of the left hemimandible (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 On MRI, the corresponding area showed mild STIR hyperintensity (Figure 3) and T1 hypointensity (Figure 4). No evidence of any significant diffusion restriction was seen in this region (Figures 5a and 5b). Surrounding oedematous changes were seen in the left masticator muscles and infratemporal fossa. No focal discrete collection or solid component was seen.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-year-old male child was brought by his 34-year-old mother with complaints of abdominal pain and recurrent vomiting. The child was clinically stable, and the abdominal ultrasound was normal. However, we incidentally discovered that both the mother and the son had physical impairments in their bilateral upper and lower limbs.
 Imaging Findings: The child had a classical median cleft with rudimentary bilateral thumbs with loss of opposition movement (Figure 1). A frontal radiograph revealed a bilateral median cleft, and the right hand’s proximal phalanges of the middle and index fingers fused, resulting in syndactyly. An additional middle phalanx was observed in the right thumb. Bilaterally, the thumb and index finger were displaced toward each other (Figures 2a and 2b). The radiography of the child’s feet depicted the absence of three of his metatarsals (Figure 2c). The X-ray of the infant’s skull showed multiple convolutions, suggesting a copper-beaten appearance (Figure 3).
 
 Interestingly, his 34-year-old mother, too, suffered from a similar deformity. On clinical inspection, both her right and left hands showed the presence of only three fingers (Figure 4a). A hypoplastic index finger was seen in the left hand. Bilateral feet showed only two toes with possible syndactyly (Figure 4b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 33-year-old female presents with a 6-year history of abnormal involuntary movements of all limbs, face, and lips, which initially started as increased fidgeting and finger flicking. Cognitive decline began a year later, with memory loss, disorientation, and difficulty dressing and eating, and now is fully dependent for activities of daily living.
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced MRI (CEMRI) of the brain was performed with 1.5T with sequences of T1WI inversion recovery 3D, T2WI axial and coronal, 3D FLAIR, SWI, DWI, and MR spectroscopy (MRS). MRI revealed atrophy of the head of the caudate bilaterally (Figures 1 and 2) with decreased FH:CC ratio of 1.18 (frontal horn width to intercaudate distance ratio) (Figure 5) and increased CC:IT ratio of 0.25 (intercaudate distance to inner table width ratio) (Figure 6). Volume loss of the putamen and globus pallidus was noted bilaterally. An enlargement of the bilateral frontal horns of the lateral ventricles, giving a box-like configuration, is noted. There is evidence of T2/FLAIR hyperintensities (Figures 3 and 4) seen in the involved region with mild grade II mineralisation seen in globi pallidi, extending to bilateral posterolateral putamen and also in the substantia nigra. Mild prominence of sulci and ventricles with grey matter volume loss is suggestive of mild cerebral volume loss. MRS shows choline peak and mild NAA (N-acetyl aspartate) loss in the basal ganglia (Figures 7a, 7b, 7c, and 7d).

   - Pros: Abnormal involuntary movements and basal ganglia involvement typical. Chorea-acanthocytosis usually presents with orofacial dyskinesias, and abnormal movements.
   - Cons: The hallmark of acanthocytosis is peripheral blood smear findings with acanthocytes, which is not mentioned here. No mention of acanthocytosis in labs. Age fits the typical onset, but the presence of atypical mineralization argues against.

   - Cons: Again, no mention of acanthocyte presence in labs. However, the term "neuroacanthocytosis syndrome" is broader and not tied directly to specific imaging features but to clinical presentation and lab findings.

3) Neuroacanthocytosis syndrome: This is a broader term encompassing chorea-acanthocytosis and other related conditions. While clinical findings match, imaging does not specifically differentiate, and lab confirmation is missing.

Given the pathognomonic finding of box-shaped frontal horns due to caudate atrophy and the classic combination of movement disorder and cognitive decline, I would choose Huntington’s disease as the diagnosis.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: 8-year-old female with swelling in the dorsal aspect of the tongue on the left side, present since birth, with slight enlargement in the past 6 months. No history of fever, breathing difficulties, or bleeding tendency. On physical examination, it showed a bluish swelling in the anterior tongue measuring 1.5 x 1 cm, and it was a soft, non-tender mass on palpation (Figure 1).
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound of the dorsal aspect of the tongue showed a well-circumscribed hypoechoic lesion with smooth margins in the anterior aspect of the tongue on the left side. No evidence of calcifications (phleboliths) or cystic areas. No surrounding induration was seen. Colour Doppler studies showed mild internal vascularity (Figure 2).
 
 MRI showed a well-defined T2 intensely hyperintense (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c), and iso to slightly hyperintense signal in T1-weighted images (Figure 5) in the anterior aspect of the tongue on the left side. No blooming artefacts on the gradient echo sequences (GRE) images (Figure 4). No evidence of true diffusion restriction. Homogeneous contrast enhancement on post-contrast T1 images was seen (Figure 6). Delayed post-contrast images are not available due to patient motion artefacts.

   - Pros: Vascular lesion with mild internal vascularity on Doppler, homogeneous contrast enhancement, location in the tongue (common site for vascular lesions), congenital nature.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 6-year-old child with a diagnosis of Gorlin syndrome, so far manifested by medulloblastoma resected two years ago, and disease-free in subsequent follow-ups until now, presented with insidious hip pain. Given her medical history, a pelvic CT was performed.
 Imaging Findings: Brain MRI, performed 2 years previously, at the time of medulloblastoma diagnosis, revealed a mottled intraparenchymal mass in the posterior fossa with heterogeneous enhancement and hydrocephalus (Figures 1a and 1b).
 
 Actual pelvic CT showed extensive osteolytic involvement with a soft tissue component affecting all included bony structures (vertebral bodies, pelvic girdle, and femurs), with severe destruction of the pelvis and both femoral heads (Figures 2a and 2b). Given the presence of lytic metastases, a subsequent whole-body CT with intravenous contrast in the portal phase was performed, revealing retroperitoneal adenopathic conglomerates (Figure 3) and a single hepatic metastasis (Figure 4).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 56-year-old woman presented with a painless right breast lump, present for three months. She also reported easy fatigability, ptosis, and exertional dyspnoea for one year. She was diagnosed with myasthenia gravis, and was started on immunosuppressants five months ago. There was no history of tuberculosis (TB) or contact with TB patients.
 Imaging Findings: High-resolution CT of the thorax showed a well-defined soft tissue density in the anterior superior mediastinum. CT-guided biopsy revealed polygonal epithelial cells with interspersed lymphocytes, suggestive of thymoma type B1. Only non-contrast-enhanced CT of the thorax was performed (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c) due to contraindication to iodinated contrast in myasthenia gravis. An elliptical pleural-based hyperdense lesion with peripheral calcification and central fluid density was noted along the lateral right hemithorax, suggestive of empyema. A lobulated soft tissue lesion was seen in the lower inner quadrant of the right breast, extending into the underlying pectoral muscle. Ultrasound correlation was advised.
 
 Ultrasound of the right breast (Figures 2a, 2b, and 3) revealed an irregular, heterogeneously hypoechoic lesion with posterior enhancement, oriented parallel to the parenchyma from 3 to 5 o’clock position, 2 cm from the nipple–areola complex. No internal vascularity or calcification was seen. The lesion extended into the pectoralis muscle and appeared soft on elastography. It was categorised as BIRADS 4A.
 
 Fine-needle aspiration cytology was advised (Figures 4a and 4b).

- Pros: Inflammatory condition can present with hypoechoic mass and posterior enhancement.

- Cons: No necrosis or calcification mentioned (though calcifications can appear), no systemic TB symptoms reported; pleural effusion could be empyema; but TB empyema common with TB pleuritis; immunosuppression could predispose to atypical presentation.

- Cons: Rare in the breast; typically shows a sinus tract and characteristic radiologic signs; no mention of sinuses or typical features; not common in immunosuppressed patients; less likely.

However, the lesion lacks typical features of carcinoma such as microcalcifications, spiculated margins, and internal vascularity.
