OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A man in his mid-20s with a 2–3 week history of intermittent severe headache, photophobia, nausea and vomiting, presented with generalised seizures. Treatment for meningoencephalitis was given, and the patient improved. Two months later, he developed sudden bilateral lower limb weakness and pins and needles sensation below the waist.
 Imaging Findings: MRI showed diffuse cortical and subcortical white matter T2 hyperintensity involving the hippocampi and parahippocampal gyri bilaterally and cortical signal abnormality in the left superior frontal lobe (Figures 1a and 1b). Two weeks later, an MRI (not shown) showed near complete resolution of the abnormal signal intensities. No abnormalities were seen in the cervicothoracic spine.
 
 MRI whole spine two months later showed extensive increased signal of the central cord spanning from C2 to T8 vertebrae with cord expansion (Figures 2a and 2b), and areas of ring-enhancing foci within the cord at C2 (Figures 2c and d). There were also confluent and continuous lesions involving the medial aspect of the right temporal lobe, optic tract, corticospinal tract and hypothalamus. This extends into the right cerebral peduncle and the pyramids of the right pons (Figure 3).

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>	
A) Key findings: 
1) Young man with acute neurological symptoms including seizures, severe headache, photophobia, vomiting, followed by bilateral lower limb weakness and sensory changes. 
2) Initial MRI: diffuse cortical and subcortical T2 hyperintensity in hippocampi and parahippocampal gyri bilaterally, and left superior frontal cortex. Follow-up MRI showed near complete resolution of these changes. 
3) No spinal abnormalities initially, but two months later, extensive central cord T2 hyperintensity from C2-T8 with cord expansion and ring-enhancing lesions at C2. 
4) Brain MRI also showed lesions involving medial right temporal lobe, optic tract, corticospinal tract, hypothalamus, extending into the right cerebral peduncle and pyramids of the right pons. 
5) Ring-enhancing cord lesions suggest inflammatory or demyelinating etiology, and the extensive longitudinally extensive spinal cord lesion (LETM) is notable. Bilateral symmetrical hippocampal involvement and resolution of initial lesions suggest transient inflammation. 

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons: 
1) Multiple sclerosis (MS): 
Pros: Lesions in corticospinal tracts and brain lesions; optic tract involvement; typical demyelinating disease. 
Cons: MS rarely presents with LETM (long spinal cord lesion >3 vertebral segments) and ring-enhancing spinal cord lesions are uncommon. Spontaneous resolution of cortical/subcortical lesions and extensive unilateral brainstem involvement are atypical. 
2) Neuro-Behçet’s disease: 
Pros: Can present with optic tract lesions and brainstem involvement, brainstem lesions, and inflammatory spinal cord lesions are possible. 
Cons: No mention of typical systemic Behçet's features (oral/genital ulcers, eye inflammation); bilateral hippocampal lesions less typical; ring-enhancing lesions are not classic. 
3) Aquaporin-4 IgG-positive NMOSD (AQP4-NMOSD): 
Pros: Classic LETM from C2-T8, consistent with NMOSD criteria; optic tract involvement; frequent brainstem lesions. 
Cons: Typically spinal lesions are longitudinally extensive without ring enhancement (enhancement usually in active lesions but not necessarily ring-like). Hippocampal involvement less typical but can occur; bilateral symmetric lesions uncommon but not excluded. 
4) Myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein antibody disease (MOGAD): 
Pros: Can present with seizures, cortical involvement (including hippocampi), extensive LETM, and ring-enhancing spinal cord lesions; often includes brain cortical lesions; bilateral hippocampal involvement fits this. 
Cons: Less classic than NMOSD for spinal cord lesions; initial extensive lesions and rapid resolution are compatible with MOGAD; ring-enhancement is described in some cases. 
5) Neurosarcoidosis: 
Pros: Can affect multiple CNS sites, ring-enhancing lesions possible, spinal cord lesions can be LETM-type; brain lesions possible. 
Cons: Ring-enhancing lesions are usually nodular masses, not typically as extensive LETM; no systemic sarcoidosis evidence; hippocampal involvement uncommon. 

C) Ranking: 
1) Myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein antibody disease (MOGAD) -- Most consistent with combined bilateral hippocampal involvement, cortical/subcortical changes resolving, extensive LETM with possible ring enhancement, brainstem and optic pathway lesions.
2) Aquaporin-4 IgG-positive NMOSD -- LETM and optic tract lesions suggest NMOSD, but bilaterality and ring-enhancing spinal lesions less typical. 
3) Neuro-Behçet’s disease -- Less typical systemic features, less characteristic imaging.
4) Multiple sclerosis -- Unlikely given LETM and ring-enhancement pattern.
5) Neurosarcoidosis -- Least likely due to lack of systemic signs and typical lesion morphology. 
</analysis>

<final>Myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein antibody disease (MOGAD)</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 11-year-old child presented with a pricking type of upper abdominal pain on and off for 6 months. No history of fever, vomiting, or loss of appetite. No previous history of similar complaints. On examination, tenderness is present over the epigastric and left lumbar region. Routine laboratory investigations were normal.
 Imaging Findings: On ultrasound, a relatively well-defined heteroechoic lesion with a hyperechoic rim is noted in the retropancreatic area, with a focal exophytic hypoechoic area in the anterior aspect of the lesion (Figure 1). No obvious internal vascularity on colour Doppler.
 
 On contrast-enhanced (CE) CT, a ~ 6.8 x 7 x 8.1 cm well-defined, smoothly marginated, heterodense soft tissue density lesion (predominantly fat component in the posterior aspect with few small solid components and calcific focus in the anterior aspect) is noted in the retropancreatic region (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c).
 
 Regarding relations and mass effect, the lesion, superiorly, abuts the coeliac trunk and inferiorly displaces the third part of the duodenum. Anteriorly, it displaces the body and tail of the pancreas, abuts the splenic artery and mildly compresses the splenic vein. Posteriorly, abuts the left renal vein. Medially, there is a widening of the duodenal C loop, and laterally, a displacement of adjacent bowel loops. There is no obvious evidence of infiltration with the structures mentioned above or vascular complications (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 68-year-old woman presented with a 3-month history of progressive abdominal distension and vague pelvic discomfort. Physical examination revealed a palpable midline abdominal mass. Routine laboratory investigations were unremarkable. Abdominopelvic CT was performed for further evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: Unenhanced abdominopelvic CT demonstrated two large, well-defined, predominantly cystic pelvic masses (Figure 1).
 
 The right-sided lesion (22 × 15 × 19 cm), centred in the cul-de-sac and extending into the right iliac fossa, was intraperitoneal, displacing small-bowel loops superiorly and the uterus postero-leftward while preserving a fat plane with the right psoas muscle (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c).
 
 The left-sided mass (15 × 17 × 15 cm) occupied the left flank/iliac fossa, had a slightly irregular thick wall, and abutted the left psoas muscle, kidney, aorta and iliac vessels without an intervening fat plane, displacing ileo-jejunal loops anteromedially—features consistent with a retroperitoneal origin (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). Compression of the left ureter produced moderate pelvicalyceal and proximal ureteral dilatation (Figure 4). A morphologically normal left ovary was identified caudal to the mass (Figure 5), whereas the right ovary was obscured. No ascites, peritoneal implants or pathological lymph nodes were present.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 25-year-old male presented with headache and giddiness for 3–4 months. He previously experienced diplopia for one month, which resolved following a right-sided medium-pressure ventriculoperitoneal shunt surgery 1 year ago.
 Imaging Findings: MRI revealed a predominantly cystic lesion with minimal solid components, measuring approximately 24 × 33 × 73 mm, centred in the lower fourth ventricle. It appeared T1 hypointense (Figure 3a) and heterogeneously T2 hyperintense, with multiple fluid-intensity cystic areas (Figure 1a). The solid areas demonstrated minimal post-contrast enhancement (Figure 3b), while the cystic portions remained non-enhancing. Mild diffusion restriction on diffusion weighted imaging–apparent diffusion coefficient (DWI–ADC) sequences (Figures 4a and 4b), and blooming foci were noted on fast field echo sequences, with corresponding PHASE hyperintensities, suggesting haemosiderin deposition (Figures 5a and 5b). CT screening showed tiny calcific foci.
 
 The lesion caused anterior displacement of the posterior pons (Figure 1a), medulla, and upper cervical cord, and abutted the cerebellar hemispheres, vermis, and both middle and inferior cerebellar peduncles. Posteroinferiorly, it extended into the cisterna magna via the foramen of Magendie and into the spinal canal up to 28 mm below McRae’s line. Lateral extension into both cerebellopontine angles was noted via the foramina of Luschka, with associated mild upstream hydrocephalus (Figures 1b and 2a).

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
1. Predominantly cystic lesion with minimal solid components in the lower fourth ventricle; size approximately 24 × 33 × 73 mm.  
2. T1 hypointense, T2 heterogeneously hyperintense with multiple fluid-intensity cystic areas.  
3. Minimal post-contrast enhancement of solid components; cystic components non-enhancing.  
4. Mild diffusion restriction and blooming foci on fast field echo sequences (suggesting hemosiderin deposition).  
5. Tiny calcifications on CT.  
6. Mass effect causing anterior displacement of posterior pons, medulla, upper cervical cord, and abutting cerebellar structures.  
7. Posteroinferior extension into the cisterna magna via foramen of Magendie and spinal canal up to 28 mm below McRae’s line.  
8. Lateral extension into both cerebellopontine angles via foramina of Luschka and mild upstream hydrocephalus.  
9. Clinical: Young male with prior ventriculoperitoneal shunt for diplopia; headache and giddiness for months.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  

1. Pilocytic astrocytoma  
   - Pros: Commonly cystic with solid component; often T2 hyperintense; minimal enhancement solid component typical; typical in posterior fossa in young patients.  
   - Cons: Usually has "fleshy" solid component with more enhancement; classic mural nodule often enhances strongly; lack of hemosiderin (blooming) is unusual; calcification uncommon; does not typically involve fourth ventricle with extensive extension.  

2. Ependymoma  
   - Pros: Typical location includes the fourth ventricle; often shows heterogeneous signal with solid and cystic components; can have calcifications; hemosiderin due to previous hemorrhage common; can grow and cause hydrocephalus; often shows avid enhancement, but can have minimal peripheral enhancement; can extend through foramina (Luschka and Magendie).  
   - Cons: Usually shows more enhancement of solid components; diffusion restriction variable.  

3. Dysembryoplastic neuroepithelial tumour (DNET)  
   - Pros: Occurs in younger patients; cortical tumors often in temporal lobe; usually cystic with minimal solid component, often in supratentorial location.  
   - Cons: Rarely located in fourth ventricle; no hemosiderin typical; calcifications rare; location and extension inconsistent.  

4. Rosette-forming glioneuronal tumour (RGNT)  
   - Pros: Usually located in fourth ventricle; predominantly cystic with solid components; occurs in young adults; hemosiderin deposits possible; minimal enhancement of solid parts; can extend into nearby structures via foramina and involve pons and medulla; mild calcifications can be present.  
   - Cons: Slightly less well-known; but findings match well.  

5. Ganglioglioma  
   - Pros: Can occur in posterior fossa; sometimes cystic with solid enhancing nodule; seen in young adults.  
   - Cons: Typically supratentorial; calcifications variable; hemosiderin less typical; location less common.  

6. Medulloblastoma  
   - Pros: Common in children and young adults; typically in posterior fossa; can be solid or cystic; restricted diffusion typical.  
   - Cons: Usually solid enhancement, not cystic predominant; less commonly calcific; hemosiderin less typical; mass effect but extension into cerebellopontine angles via foramina less typical.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Ependymoma: Classic location (fourth ventricle), hemosiderin, calcifications, cystic-solid lesion, minimal enhancement of solid nodule, extension through foramina, mild hydrocephalus.  
2) Rosette-forming glioneuronal tumour (RGNT): Matches location, cystic predominance, minimal enhancement, hemosiderin presence, extension typical, similar age.  
3) Pilocytic astrocytoma: Location fits, cystic solid lesion, but classic imaging differs (more solid nodule enhancement, fewer hemosiderin and calcifications).  
4) Ganglioglioma: Less typical posterior fossa location and enhancement pattern.  
5) Medulloblastoma: Typically solid, more aggressive, enhancement strong; location possible but imaging pattern less typical.  
6) Dysembryoplastic neuroepithelial tumour (DNET): Not typical fourth ventricle location or invasion pattern.  

Considering classic imaging of fourth ventricle tumor in young adult with hemosiderin (blooming), calcifications, cystic nature, and extension through foramina, ependymoma fits best, but RGNT is also a strong contender and known to arise in fourth ventricle with similar imaging. However, ependymoma is more common overall and classic features match well.

Ependymoma  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 43-year-old female patient presented to the emergency department with acute cholecystitis. During imaging work-up, an incidental iliopsoas mass was detected on CT. MRI was subsequently performed for further characterisation.
 Imaging Findings: CT shows a well-defined, complex iliopsoas mass containing fat and soft-tissue components, demonstrating heterogeneous enhancement and prominent internal vessels (Figures 1a and 1b).
 
 MRI reveals an encapsulated lesion with high T1 signal intensity, slightly lower than subcutaneous fat, and heterogeneously high T2 signal intensity, with only partial fat suppression (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, 2d, 2e, and 2f). It exhibits relatively homogeneous hypervascular enhancement with curvilinear branching septations, with no restricted diffusion.
PostDescription:

MRI reveals an encapsulated lesion with high T1 signal intensity, slightly lower than subcutaneous fat, and heterogeneously high T2 signal intensity, with only partial fat suppression. It exhibits relatively homogeneous hypervascular enhancement with curvilinear branching septations, with no restricted diffusion.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Lipoma, Liposarcoma, Angiomyolipoma, Hibernoma, Myelolipoma, Fat necrosis
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Patient age: 43-year-old female.  
- Incidentally found well-defined complex iliopsoas mass containing fat and soft-tissue components.  
- CT: heterogeneous enhancement and prominent internal vessels.  
- MRI: encapsulated lesion, high T1 signal slightly lower than subcutaneous fat, heterogeneously high T2 signal, partial fat suppression.  
- Relatively homogeneous hypervascular enhancement with curvilinear branching septations.  
- No restricted diffusion.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
- Lipoma: 
  Pros: well-defined, composed of fat, homogenous fat signal.  
  Cons: Usually purely fatty, without soft tissue component or enhancement, no prominent internal vessels or septations. This mass has soft tissue and vascular components, making lipoma unlikely.  
  Pass: No.

- Liposarcoma:  
  Pros: can have heterogeneous fat and soft tissue, heterogeneous enhancement, internal vessels.  
  Cons: Typically larger, infiltrative margins, presence of restricted diffusion often seen, more aggressive. This lesion is well-capsulated and no restricted diffusion, making liposarcoma less likely.  
  Pass: No.

- Angiomyolipoma:  
  Pros: contains fat, soft tissue, and vessels ("triphasic"), often in extremities or retroperitoneum, hypervascular.  
  Cons: Typically in kidney, rare in iliopsoas muscle (although extrarenal AMLs reported), but can occur outside kidney.  
  Pass: Yes.

- Hibernoma:  
  Pros: fatty tumor with brown adipose tissue, hypervascular.  
  Cons: typically show high T1 and T2 signal not dropping on fat suppression, but this lesion shows only partial fat suppression and heterogeneous components, less typical.  
  Pass: No.

- Myelolipoma:  
  Pros: composed of fat and hematopoietic tissue, can be hypervascular, usually in adrenal glands.  
  Cons: unusual location in iliopsoas, usually not well encapsulated with internal vessels and enhancement.  
  Pass: No.

- Fat necrosis:  
  Pros: fat component with soft tissue and enhancement due to inflammation.  
  Cons: usually irregular, no well-defined capsule, often history of trauma or pancreatitis, and hypervascularity is not typical.  
  Pass: No.

C) Ranking and tie-breaks:  
1) Angiomyolipoma: matches fat-soft tissue-vessel triphasic nature, hypervascularity, encapsulated, location extrarenal but documented.  
2) Liposarcoma: less likely due to encapsulated nature and lack of restricted diffusion.  
3) Lipoma: does not match soft tissue or vessels.  
4) Hibernoma: less typical due to fat suppression and enhancement patterns.  
5) Myelolipoma and Fat necrosis: less likely due to location and imaging features.

<final>Angiomyolipoma</final><|return|>
options: Lipoma | Liposarcoma | Angiomyolipoma | Hibernoma | Myelolipoma | Fat necrosis

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 23-year-old woman presented with two weeks of abdominal distension, pelvic pain and urinary frequency. Emergency thoraco-abdominal computed tomography (CT) was performed, followed shortly by dedicated pelvic MRI for loco-regional characterisation.
 Imaging Findings: Initial contrast-enhanced pelvic CT (Figures 1a and 1b) demonstrated a 17 × 16 × 12 cm, predominantly solid, left retro-uterine mass with heterogeneous enhancement on the left ovary, anterior displacement of the uterus, and compression of both distal ureters, resulting in moderate bilateral hydroureteronephrosis. No macroscopic fat, calcification or pathological lymph-node enlargement was identified.
 
 Subsequent pelvic MRI (Figures 2a and 2b) showed mainly solid tissue with intermediate T2 signal (Figures 2a and 2b) and avid post-gadolinium enhancement (Figure 3), containing scattered cystic-necrotic foci. Diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) showed marked hyperintensity of the solid components with low ADC values, consistent with high cellularity (Figures 4a and 4b). The tumour–uterus interface was smooth, and the right ovary appeared normal, displaced anteriorly. Ascites was minimal and confined to the pouch of Douglas.
 
 No peritoneal implants or pathological lymphadenopathy were identified on either CT or MRI.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 11-year-old premenarche girl presented with a 3-week history of intermittent hypogastric pain, not being able to sleep for more than two hours at a time for the last 5 days. Clinical examination revealed a palpable pelvic mass and suprapubic tenderness. The patient had no other relevant findings on physical examination. Blood and urine tests were all within normal limits.
 Imaging Findings: A transabdominal pelvic ultrasound was performed and revealed a significant distension of the vagina, measuring 6.8 x 8.3 x 15.3 cm, with echogenic material occupying its lumen (approximately 450 ml). The distension extended to the uterus, which showed hyperechogenic material inside its lumen. Both annexes were of normal appearance. There was no evidence of acute appendicitis, hydronephrosis or other significant findings.

 Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) confirmed extensive bone marrow replacement in the proximal tibia with adjacent soft tissue mass showing heterogeneous contrast enhancement, cortical permeation, bone destruction with focal cortical breakthrough (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d).  18F-fluorodeoxyglucose positron emission tomography–computed tomography (FDG PET–CT) scan showed markedly increased uptake in the proximal right tibia, adjacent soft tissue, and right popliteal, inguinal, iliac and retroperitoneal lymph nodes (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). Other distant metastases were absent.

OriginalDescription:

Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) showed lymphocytic pleocytosis with hyperproteinorrachia, and a 2024 mediastinal node biopsy revealed non-necrotising granulomas.  Imaging Findings: Sagittal T2-weighted MRI of the spine (Figure 1) revealed diffuse hyperintensity and swelling from C2 to T10 with a sharp transition at T11. There was patchy nodular enhancement involving the posterior and circumferential pial surfaces. The conus was spared.
 
 Brain MRI demonstrated two punctate cortical–subcortical enhancing foci without mass effect (Figures 3a and 3b). CT chest (Figures 4a and 4b) confirmed bulky right paratracheal and mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Compared with prior studies (December 2020), cord swelling and enhancement were mildly reduced after steroid therapy.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 56-year-old patient with a history of type 2 diabetes, arterial hypertension, and knee osteoarthritis treated with intra-articular steroids was admitted due to fever of unknown origin. The patient reported lumbar pain for the past week, with no associated symptoms.
 Imaging Findings: Non-contrast and contrast-enhanced CT of the skull, thorax and abdomen was performed to identify a possible infectious focus. The key findings included heterogeneous density in both psoas muscles due to the presence of gas bubbles, with no collections detected following contrast administration (Figures 1a and 1b). In addition to the gas identified in the psoas muscles, gas bubbles were observed within the L3 vertebral body.
 
 Unenhanced and contrast-enhanced lumbar spine MRI was performed. The axial T2-weighted image (Figure 2a) shows an enlarged left psoas muscle due to multiple round collections with increased signal intensity. A similar collection is observed in the ipsilateral paraspinal muscle and the right psoas muscle. In the axial post-contrast T1-weighted sequence (Figure 2b), the described lesions, which exhibit enhancement after contrast administration, are also seen (marked with blue stars). Sagittal T1-weighted images pre (Figure 2c) and post-contrast (Figure 2d) demonstrate heterogeneous very subtle enhancement of the L2, L3, L4, and L5 vertebral bodies, as well as the intervertebral discs. The sagittal views demonstrate lesions in the psoas and paraspinal muscles, with hyperintense signal on T2-weighted (Figure 2e) and STIR (Figure 2f) sequences at the level of the L2, L3, L4, and L5 vertebral bodies.

   - Pros: Commonly presents acutely with fever; discitis and osteomyelitis of discular and vertebral body (L2-L5); presence of intraosseous and intramuscular gas bubbles (gas-forming organisms); multiple abscess collections in psoas and paraspinal muscles; diabetes and steroids are risk factors for pyogenic infections.

   - Pros: Disc tissue herniation into endplate.
   - Cons: Typically do not involve gas bubbles or abscesses; only disc protrusion without enhancement or collections.

6. Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH):
   - Pros: Can involve vertebral bodies.
   - Cons: Typically in younger patients; no gas bubbles or abscesses; no collections or enhancing lesions of that type.

3. Charcot joint – less likely.
4-6. Other entities are less fitting to multiple abscesses and gas collection.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old woman with no significant past medical history presented with long-standing recurrent epistaxis and progressive fatigue. Physical examination revealed mucocutaneous telangiectasias on the nasal mucosa and tongue, as well as mild hepatomegaly. Laboratory tests showed iron-deficiency anaemia and mildly altered liver function tests, with cholestasis but no hyperbilirubinemia.
 Imaging Findings: Colour-Doppler abdominal ultrasound revealed multiple dilated arterial vessels in both liver lobes, corresponding to branches of the hepatic artery, with one vessel forming a direct communication with the main portal vein (Figures 1a and 1b).
 
 Subsequent contrast-enhanced CT demonstrated diffuse dilatation of intrahepatic arterial branches and early filling of the dilated hepatic veins during arterial phase, suggestive of arteriovenous shunting, and multiple small hypervascular nodules consistent with hepatic telangiectasias. Mild intrahepatic biliary duct dilatation was also noted, likely due to extrinsic compression by dilated vessels (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c).
 
 Further evaluation with MRI confirmed the presence of intrahepatic arteriovenous malformations. Post-contrast T1-weighted Dixon sequences and MR angiography provided superior characterisation of the vascular anomalies, ruling out portal vein thrombosis and parenchymal changes typical of chronic liver disease (Figure 3).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 34-year-old patient undergoes a scheduled MRI study due to a one-year history of migraine-like headaches, accompanied by visual disturbances, paraesthesias in the right upper limb, and instability before and during the pain.
 Imaging Findings: The MRI reveals a lesion located in the right temporal horn of the lateral ventricle, with a prominent cystic component and a solid nodule that appears hypointense on T1-weighted images (Figures 1a and 1b) and hyperintense on T2-weighted images (Figure 2). This solid, heterogeneous nodule shows intense enhancement following gadolinium administration (Figures 1c, 1d, and 1e) and areas of microcystic architecture, without evidence of subependymal spread. Extensive T2-FLAIR hyperintensity is observed throughout the surrounding white matter, consistent with vasogenic oedema. The mass exerts a moderate compressive effect on the ventricular system, causing a slight midline shift (Figure 2).
 
 Notably, the lesion displays prominent draining veins, reflecting its hypervascular nature (Figures 3a and 3b). No diffusion restriction is identified on DWI sequences (Figure 4). Perfusion imaging confirms a highly vascularised tumour (Figure 5a, 5b, 5c).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 42-year-old lady presented with a gradually progressive, painless, cystic swelling in the left infra-auricular region for the last 12 months. No other swellings were noted in the neck. There was no prior history of ear discharge or parotitis. Clinical examination showed no evidence of skin changes or facial nerve palsy.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasonography reveals a cystic lesion with internal echoes within the substance of the left parotid gland with a beak-like projection on its deeper aspect (Figure 1).
 
 CT scan demonstrates a well-defined, homogeneous, smoothly marginated, non-enhancing lesion (density of 35 HU before and 36 HU after contrast administration) located in the left parotid gland, causing thinning and outward displacement of the residual gland parenchyma (Figure 2).
 
 MRI reveals a T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, and STIR hyperintense, intra-parotid lesion occupying superficial and deep lobes of the parotid with a small blind-ending tract projecting anteromedially into the parapharyngeal space. No fistulous tracts to the external auditory canal were noted. There was no evidence of fat stranding or enlarged lymph nodes (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c).

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 42-year-old female with painless, cystic swelling in left infra-auricular region (parotid region).  
- Clinical exam: no skin changes, facial nerve intact, no prior ear discharge or parotitis.  
- Ultrasound: cystic lesion with internal echoes in left parotid gland with beak-like projection deeper.  
- CT: well-defined, homogeneous, smoothly marginated, non-enhancing lesion with low attenuation (35-36 HU), causing thinning and outward displacement of residual parotid tissue.  
- MRI: T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, STIR hyperintense intra-parotid lesion involving superficial and deep lobes, with a small blind-ending tract projecting anteromedially into the parapharyngeal space. No fistulous tract to the external auditory canal. No fat stranding or lymphadenopathy.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with 1-2 pros/cons:  

1) First branchial cleft cyst  
   - Pros: Often located near external auditory canal or parotid region, can show cystic swelling.  
   - Cons: Typically has a fistulous tract to the external auditory canal; no such tract was found on MRI. The lesion is within the parotid gland and involves deep structures, which is less common. Lack of prior ear discharge or parotiditis typical of 1st branchial cleft pathologies.  
   - Pass/Fail: Fail (Key contraindication: no tract to external auditory canal, which is a classic feature).

2) Lymphepithelial cyst  
   - Pros: Occurs in parotid gland, cystic lesion, can be asymptomatic and painless.  Lymphepithelial cysts are parotid cystic lesions lined by pseudostratified columnar epithelium, often present in adults, typically nonenhancing, cystic.  The imaging characteristics match: T1 hypo, T2 hyper, nonenhancing. Also well-defined and homogeneous.  
   - Cons: The blind ending tract anteromedially could raise confusion with branchial cleft anomalies, but this can be explained by extension into parapharyngeal space. No other features contraindicative.  
   - Pass/Fail: Pass

3) Warthin’s tumour  
   - Pros: Typically found in parotid gland, generally cystic and enhancing lesions, with multiloculated or cystic components. Often shows enhancement on CT or MR with contrast due to lymphoid stroma. Usually occurs in older males with smoking history.  
   - Cons: The lesion is non-contrast enhancing, not typical for Warthin's tumor. No mention of papillary cystic or solid component. No enhancement seen. Also, typical age and gender not matching. Lack of lymphadenopathy or other typical features.  
   - Pass/Fail: Fail

4) Sialocele  
   - Pros: Cystic fluid collection due to parotid duct disruption often following trauma or parotitis.  Presence of fluid within parotid gland can be sonographically hypoechoic.  
   - Cons: Usually associated with history of trauma or recent surgery (none here). Also, sialocele is generally more ill-defined and not encapsulated. No indication of prior trauma. Imaging here shows well-defined, smoothly marginated lesion, unlike sialocele.  
   - Pass/Fail: Fail

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Lymphepithelial cyst (only pass and matches imaging and clinical findings best)  
2) First branchial cleft cyst (failed due to missing tract)  
3) Warthin's tumour (failed due to lack of enhancement and typical patient demographics)  
4) Sialocele (failed due to no trauma/history and imaging features)  
Final selection based on best match to imaging characteristics, clinical presentation, and pathognomonic features: Lymphepithelial cyst.  

</analysis><final> Lymphepithelial cyst </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 47-year-old male presented with complaints of headaches for 6 months. No history of loss of consciousness, projectile vomiting or seizures. No history of trauma. The patient is a known case of type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension on treatment for 10 years. On examination, macrognathia and frontal bossing were noted.
 Imaging Findings: The skull radiograph (Figure 1a) shows an enlarged sella turcica, frontal sinus hyperpneumatisation, and calvarial hyperostosis. Prognathism and widened mandibular angle are seen (Figure 1b). Hand and feet radiographs (Figures 2a and 2b) show spade-like distal phalanges with prominent ungual tufts. The chest radiograph (Figure 3) reveals widened glenohumeral joints and thickened scapular borders.
 
 CT brain shows calvarial hyperostosis (Figure 4a) and a hyperdense sellar mass (Figure 4b).
 
 MRI reveals a lobulated sellar-suprasellar lesion—T1 isointense (Figure 7), T2 and FLAIR hyperintense with “snowman” appearance—causing sella widening and displacing adjacent vessels (Figures 5a, 5b, and 6), with heterogeneous enhancement (Figures 9b and 9c).
PostDescription:

A) Key findings:  
- Patient reports chronic headaches, no neurological deficits or trauma.  
- Clinical features include macrognathia (prognathism) and frontal bossing.  
- Radiographs: enlarged sella turcica, thickened skull bones (calvarial hyperostosis), frontal sinus hyperpneumatization, spade-like distal phalanges with ungual tufts, widened glenohumeral joints, thickened scapular borders.  
- CT: calvarial hyperostosis and hyperdense sellar mass.  
- MRI: lobulated sellar-suprasellar lesion isointense on T1, hyperintense on T2/FLAIR, "snowman" appearance due to suprasellar extension and displacement of adjacent vessels, heterogeneous enhancement.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1. Acromegaly secondary to pituitary macroadenoma  
   - Pros: Enlarged sella, lobulated sellar macroadenoma causing bone changes (hyperostosis), clinical features of prognathism and frontal bossing are classic for acromegaly.  
   - Cons: Classic acromegaly does not typically include "spade-like" distal phalanges, widened joint spaces, and thickened scapular borders; these bone changes appear more systemic and symmetrical.  
   - Overall: Strong contender given pituitary macroadenoma and endocrine-related bony changes, but features suggest underlying bone disease.  

2. Pseudo-acromegaly  
   - Pros: Mimics acromegaly clinically without GH excess; may account for bony changes.  
   - Cons: Does not explain the sellar macroadenoma, enlarged sella, or typical pituitary mass. No lab data provided for GH/IGF-1, but radiological brain findings favor true pituitary adenoma with bone changes.  
   - Overall: Less likely due to pituitary mass and classic craniodigital findings.  

3. Thyrotropin-secreting adenomas  
   - Pros: Pituitary macroadenoma causing sella widening, clinical presentation may include skull bone changes.  
   - Cons: Clinical features of acromegaly (prognathism, frontal bossing) are GH-related; no mention of hyperthyroidism-related symptoms. Also no mention of enlarged thyroid or systemic bone changes classically described in thyrotropinomas.  
   - Overall: Unlikely based on clinical and radiological features.  

4. McCune–Albright syndrome  
   - Pros: Café-au-lait spots, fibrous dysplasia, endocrine abnormalities; bony changes may be present.  
   - Cons: No dermatological findings mentioned, no fibrous dysplasia of skull; no endocrine abnormalities described other than possibly acromegaly. Macroostosis and skull changes could be from polyostotic fibrous dysplasia; but lacks typical monostotic/fibrous dysplasia, and pituitary macroadenoma is not expected.  
   - Overall: Unlikely based on provided features.  

5. Paget’s disease of bone  
   - Pros: Calvarial hyperostosis, widened joints, thickened bones, increased skull bone density, and thickened scapular borders are classic for Paget’s disease; spade-like phalanges can be seen in Paget's disease due to bone overgrowth and remodeling.  
   - Cons: Paget's may cause skull thickening including the sella turcica, but does not typically cause sellar mass or macroadenoma. However, the "snowman" shape and isointense T1 hyperdense mass could also be due to sclerotic bone changes in the skull base mimicking a mass. No neurological deficits in the patient. However, the presence of a lobulated mass causing displacement of vessels favors a tumor rather than simple bone changes.  
   - Overall: The systemic bone findings strongly support Paget's disease; the sellar mass may be secondary or coincidental.  

C) Ranking and final decision:  
1) Acromegaly secondary to pituitary macroadenoma – Fits all key findings: sellar macroadenoma, enlarged sella, clinical bony changes consistent with acromegaly in skull, hands, and joints, plus macrognathia and frontal bossing.  
2) Paget’s disease of bone – Explains widespread calvarial hyperostosis, joint widening, and bone remodeling, yet does not fully account for the sellar macroadenoma.  
3) Pseudo-acromegaly – Does not explain pituitary macroadenoma.  
4) Thyrotropin-secreting adenoma – Inconsistent clinical picture.  
5) McCune–Albright – Lacks key clinical features and typical radiologic pattern.  

Given the presence of the classic "snowman" appearance sellar tumor causing sella widening and systemic bone changes characteristic of acromegaly, the best fit is Acromegaly secondary to pituitary macroadenoma.  
</analysis>

<final>Acromegaly secondary to pituitary macroadenoma</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 61-year-old male, with a history of a pontine stroke 10 weeks before, presented with new neurological symptoms of staccato speech and an uncoordinated gait. Neurological examination revealed nystagmus, mild dysarthria, truncal and limb ataxia, and an unsteady gait. An MRI was performed to rule out a new ischemic stroke.
 Imaging Findings: Brain MRI showed a cavitated lesion in the left paramedian pons, hypointense on T1-weighted images (WI) (Figure 1a), hyperintense on T2WI (Figure 1b), without diffusion restriction (Figure 1c), consistent with the previous pontine stroke. Additionally, symmetrical ovoid lesions in the middle cerebellar peduncles (MCPs), hypointense on T1WI (Figure 1d) and hyperintense on T2WI (Figure 1e), and mild T2WI hyperintensity in the left anterior pyramid (Figure 1g), without diffusion restriction (Figures 1f and 1h), were also noted.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 61-year-old male, with a history of a pontine stroke 10 weeks before, presented with new neurological symptoms of staccato speech and an uncoordinated gait. Neurological examination revealed nystagmus, mild dysarthria, truncal and limb ataxia, and an unsteady gait. An MRI was performed to rule out a new ischemic stroke.
 Imaging Findings: Brain MRI showed a cavitated lesion in the left paramedian pons, hypointense on T1-weighted images (WI), hyperintense on T2WI, without diffusion restriction, consistent with the previous pontine stroke. Additionally, symmetrical ovoid lesions in the middle cerebellar peduncles (MCPs), hypointense on T1WI and hyperintense on T2WI, and mild T2WI hyperintensity in the left anterior pyramid, without diffusion restriction, were also noted.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Bilateral middle cerebellar peduncles ischemic stroke, Wallerian degeneration of pontocerebellar tracts secondary to unilateral pontine infarction, Multiple system atrophy, Fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome, Wilson’s disease
FinalDiagnosis: Wallerian degeneration of pontocerebellar tracts secondary to unilateral pontine infarction

- Symmetrical ovoid lesions in the bilateral middle cerebellar peduncles (MCPs): hypointense on T1, hyperintense on T2, no diffusion restriction.  
- Mild T2 hyperintensity in the left anterior pyramid, no diffusion restriction.

   - Pros: Bilateral MCP lesions present.

Additionally, bilateral symmetrical lesions are less typical for bilateral simultaneous infarcts; also no mention of typical acute features on diffusion-weighted imaging.  
   - Verdict: Fail.  

2) Wallerian degeneration of pontocerebellar tracts secondary to unilateral pontine infarction:  
   - Pros: Lesions in MCPs and left anterior pyramid match tracts related to pontocerebellar fibers; symmetrical MCP lesions possible due to bilateral degeneration secondary to unilateral pontine lesion. Lack of diffusion restriction supports chronic degeneration rather than acute infarction.  
   - Cons: Typically asymmetric degeneration may be expected, but bilateral MCP involvement can occur due to bilateral descending or ascending fibers involvement. Prior pontine lesion supports causality.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old man presented to the Emergency Department with a 4-day history of right iliac fossa pain. He denied fever, nausea, or vomiting.  Imaging Findings: Ultrasound (Figure 1) revealed a blind-ending, aperistaltic, non-compressible tubular structure (luminal diameter ≈ 10 mm) arising from the caecum at the appendectomy bed. The wall was thickened with surrounding echogenic fat. A minimal amount of free fluid was also observed.
 
 In contrast-enhanced computer tomography (CT), multiplanar reformats demonstrated an enhancing tubular remnant arising from the caecum, capped by two metallic clips at its distal end (Figures 2a, 2b, and 3). The stump showed concentric mural thickening, pericaecal fat stranding, and a trace of free fluid. No evidence of abscess or pneumoperitoneum was identified.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 8-year-old male patient presented with chronic left lumbar discomfort, claudication, anaemia (Hb = 8 g/dL), and hyperleukocytosis (12000/mm3). He has been receiving therapy for anaemia for two years.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound (US) shows multiple thick-walled collections with echogenic fluid content occupying the left renal fossa and loss of renal parenchyma’s architecture with no corticomedullary differentiation. The thin remaining peripheric parenchymal bundle shows no limits with the thick-walled collections. There is a central pyelic stone measuring 20 mm with a posterior acoustic shadow and a hyperechoic aspect of the perirenal fat.
 
 CT (computed tomography) shows left renal enlargement with destruction of parenchymal architecture and dilated calyces consistent with the “bear paw sign” (Figure 1a), coexisting blurring of perirenal fat, psoas muscle enlargement and abscess formation (Figure 1b), as well as nephroureterolithiasis (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d). The left kidney was nonfunctional at the excretory phase scan (Figures 1e and 1f).
 
 On renal scintigraphy (Figure 2), DTPA injection demonstrates reduced relative renal function on the left (17.7% left and 82.3% right), with pyelocaliceal stasis at the excretory phase. Reduced cortical uptake, particularly in the left upper pole, was seen both at DMSA and DTPA scans.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 40-year-old male presented to the emergency department following the onset of acute right lower quadrant abdominal pain, later diagnosed as inflamed, nonperforated appendicitis. On CT abdomen and pelvis (CTAP), an incidental adrenal mass was found on the left kidney. Work-up for the adrenal mass was conducted.
 Imaging Findings: In 2018, a CTAP noted a 17 mm left adrenal nodule without normal washout characteristics (Figure 1). The work-up at the time revealed no functional secretion, and the patient was also asymptomatic.
 
 A new CTAP revealed that the previous nodule had grown to a size of 6.4 x 6 x 6.7 cm (Figure 2). A chest CT with contrast was unremarkable.
 
 An MRI was ordered for further characterisation of the mass. T1 MRI visualised a heterogeneous enhancing left adrenal mass that was suspicious of malignancy (Figure 3). The size of the lesion was consistent compared to the CTAP.

A) Key findings:  
1. Incidental left adrenal nodule noted on CTAP, initial size 17 mm, without normal washout characteristics (suggesting atypical or potentially non-benign).  
2. No functional secretion and asymptomatic on work-up (excluding functional lesions like pheochromocytoma at the time).  
3. Nodule grew significantly over time to 6.4 x 6 x 6.7 cm (substantial enlargement suggests potential malignancy or aggressive behavior).  
4. Chest CT with contrast unremarkable (no evidence of systemic metastasis from another primary source).  
5. MRI T1 shows heterogeneous enhancing left adrenal mass, suspicious of malignancy (suggestive of possible adrenal carcinoma or metastatic lesion).  

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  
1. Adrenal adenocarcinoma (malignant adrenal cortical carcinoma):  
   Pros: Large size > 4 cm; rapid growth; heterogeneous enhancement on MRI; lesion suspicious for malignancy; non-functioning initially (common in many cases).  
   Cons: Washout characteristics non-assessable, but typical benign adenomas show rapid washout; no functional signs but that is not exclude carcinoma.  
2. Adrenal adenoma:  
   Pros: Common incidental adrenal lesion; usually benign, homogenous on imaging; generally no significant growth.  
   Cons: No normal washout on CT; significant growth in size and suspicious heterogeneous enhancement argue against benign adenoma; adenomas rarely enlarge to >6 cm.  
3. Pheochromocytoma:  
   Pros: Enhances heterogeneously on MRI; can be larger; typically secretes catecholamines, causing symptoms.  
   Cons: No functional secretion on work-up; patient asymptomatic; pheochromocytomas are usually catecholamine-secreting and symptomatic.  
4. Adrenal myelolipoma:  
   Pros: Benign tumor; nonfunctional; typically contains fat (fatty density on CT).  
   Cons: No mention of fat content; growth to 6 cm is unusual unless complication; enhancing heterogeneous mass is not typical; would expect fat density on CT, not reported.  
5. Adrenal metastatic lesion:  
   Pros: Heterogeneous enhancement; larger size; suspicion of malignancy; growing lesion.  
   Cons: Chest CT negative for primary; no known history of primary cancer; less likely without known primary.  

C) Ranking and final decision:  
1. Adrenal adenocarcinoma (top choice: size, growth, suspicious MRI, no other primary source)  
2. Adrenal metastatic lesion (less likely without known primary or other lesions)  
3. Pheochromocytoma (less likely due to lack of functional symptoms/secretion)  
4. Adrenal adenoma (unlikely given growth and suspicious imaging)  
5. Adrenal myelolipoma (unlikely due to lack of fat density and suspicious features)  
  
Given the growth pattern, size, lack of washout, and suspicious heterogeneous enhancement, the most likely diagnosis is adrenal adenocarcinoma (adrenocortical carcinoma).  
</analysis>

<final>Adrenal adenocarcinoma</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 18-year-old healthy male presents with three months of left shoulder pain during activities, followed by a gradual restriction in arm abduction. He denies any trauma, fever, or involvement of other joints. Physical examination reveals swelling in the deltoid region and an inability to raise the left arm to 90°.
 Imaging Findings: Left shoulder x-ray (Figure 1) revealed a mixed sclerotic-lytic lesion involving the proximal diaphysis and metaphysis of the humerus, characterised by poorly defined margins, a wide transition zone, permeative osteolysis, cortical thinning, and a spiculated periosteal reaction with Codman’s triangle, consistent with a Bone-RADS 4 lesion.
 
 MRI demonstrated a large lobulated mass centred in the proximal humerus, hypointense on T1-weighted images (Figure 2a), hyperintense on T2 (Figure 2b) and STIR (Figures 2c and 2d) sequences, with associated extraosseous soft tissue extension and glenohumeral joint effusion. Post-contrast fat-suppressed T1-weighted imaging (Figure 2e) showed heterogeneous enhancement with central non-enhancing areas. Diffusion-weighted imaging (Figure 2f) revealed restricted diffusion with corresponding low signal on the ADC map (Figure 2g) indicating high cellularity.
 
 PET/CT (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d) confirmed an intraosseous mass with osteoid matrix and aggressive periosteal reaction, demonstrating hypermetabolic activity in the proximal humerus, adjacent soft tissues, and secondary foci in the lungs and spinal canal.

Post-contrast fat-suppressed T1-weighted imaging showed heterogeneous enhancement with central non-enhancing areas. Diffusion-weighted imaging revealed restricted diffusion with corresponding low signal on the ADC map indicating high cellularity. model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 18-year-old male with shoulder pain and limited arm abduction, swelling over deltoid region.  
- X-ray: mixed sclerotic-lytic lesion in proximal humeral diaphysis/metaphysis, poorly defined margins, wide transition zone, permeative osteolysis, cortical thinning, spiculated periosteal reaction with Codman's triangle.  
- MRI: large lobulated mass, T1 hypointensity, T2/STIR hyperintensity, extraosseous soft tissue extension, joint effusion; heterogeneous post-contrast enhancement with central necrosis; restricted diffusion indicating high cellularity.  
- PET/CT: hypermetabolism in primary lesion, soft tissue extension, secondary metastases in lungs and spinal canal.  
- Diagnosis is Bone-RADS 4 (high suspicion of malignancy); aggressive lesion with soft tissue component and metastases suggests high-grade primary bone sarcoma.  
- Age is consistent with osteosarcoma; location is proximal humerus.  
- Presence of osteoid matrix on PET/CT is typical for osteogenic sarcoma.  
- Chondrosarcoma and chondroblastic osteosarcoma are also possible but chondroblastic osteosarcoma shows chondroid matrix, which is not specifically indicated here; chondrosarcoma is less common in this age group and less aggressive with metastases at presentation; chondroblastoma is benign, occurs in epiphysis and is unlikely to metastasize or cause such aggressive radiologic features.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Chondrosarcoma  
   - Pros: Aggressive lesion possible; can have soft tissue mass.  
   - Cons: Less common in this age; chondrosarcoma shows matrix calcifications (rings and arcs), not described; metastases at presentation uncommon in low-grade disease.

2) Chondroblastoma  
   - Pros: Occurs in epiphysis in young patients.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 83-year-old gentleman presented to the Urosurgery department with a history of lower urinary tract symptoms in the form of increased frequency and incomplete evacuation of urine. No history of haematuria, burning micturition or fever. On digital rectal examination, the prostate gland was felt to be enlarged with nodularity within felt. On biochemical evaluation, prostate specific antigen (PSA) was marginally raised.
 Imaging Findings: On ultrasound examination, an enlarged prostate with a volume of 36 cc. There were features of chronic bladder outlet obstruction seen in the form of thickening of the bladder wall (maximum thickness of 8 mm) and a few trabeculations in the bladder wall.
 
 In view of the elevated PSA levels, an MRI prostate was done, which revealed a well-defined, solid, irregularly shaped lesion measuring 3.5 x 5.4 x 5.7 cm (AP x TR x CC) arising from the prostatic urethra and causing its widening and extending into the bladder. This lesion was homogeneously hypointense on T1-weighted images and hyperintense on T2-weighted and T2-weighted fat-saturated images (Figures 1 and 2). It was showing homogeneous restriction of diffusion on DWI (Figures 3a and 3b) and was heterogeneously enhancing on post-contrast images. Also, multiple well-defined T2 heterogeneous nodules were seen in the transitional zone (Figure 4). No T2 hypointense or diffusion-restricting lesion was seen in the prostate.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 74-year-old female undergoing immunosuppressive therapy was referred to the orthopaedic department for evaluation of a progressively enlarging mass on the medial side of the left knee, evolving over six months. Initially asymptomatic, the lesion became painful. Notably, she had undergone an articular puncture one year earlier.
 Imaging Findings: The patient presented with a large mass on the medial aspect of the left knee (Figures 1a and 1b), which was also evident on plain radiography (Figure 2).
 
 Ultrasonography demonstrated a sizable extra-articular heterogeneous lesion with no internal vascularisation, accompanied by a moderate joint effusion and synovial hypertrophy (Figures 3a and 3b).
 
 Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) confirmed a large extra-articular collection, hyperintense on proton density-weighted fat-suppressed sequences (Figures 4a and 4b) and hypointense on T1-weighted sequences (Figure 4c), with extensive peripheral enhancement following gadolinium administration (Figures 5a and 5b) and no enhancement of solid components. Additionally, MRI revealed extensive synovitis and bone marrow oedema, along with juxta-articular and intra-articular erosions exhibiting peripheral contrast enhancement. No blooming artefacts were identified (Figure 5c).

A) Key findings:

- Large, painful, progressively enlarging extra-articular mass on medial left knee with synovial hypertrophy and moderate joint effusion.   
- US: Heterogeneous lesion, no internal vascularisation (suggests fluid collection rather than solid tumor).  
- MRI: Large extra-articular collection, hyperintense on PD fat-suppressed, hypointense on T1, enhancing peripherally after gadolinium (consistent with abscess or granulomatous inflammation), no solid nodular enhancement.  
- Extensive synovitis, bone marrow edema, juxta-articular and intra-articular erosions with peripheral enhancement.  
- No blooming artefacts (less likely to be haemorrhagic or calcified lesions).  
- History of prior articular puncture one year previously.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:

1) Infection by Mycobacterium avium:  
- Pros: Immunosuppressed host predisposes to opportunistic mycobacterial infections. Extra-articular mass with synovitis and erosions can be seen.  
- Cons: Lesion characteristics (no internal vascularity, large cystic collection) are not classical for Mycobacterium avium, which typically presents with multifocal nodules or osteomyelitis, often in immunocompromised patients, but large cystic collections less typical. No mention of pulmonary findings or other systemic involvement.  

2) Infection by Mycobacterium tuberculosis:  
- Pros: Classic location for knee involvement, large effusion with synovitis, erosions, bone marrow edema, peripheral enhancement pattern typical of tuberculous arthritis. Extra-articular mass consistent with caseous necrosis and cold abscess formation. Immunosuppression and prior articular puncture are risk factors. Lack of blooming artifacts; typical for granulomatous infections like TB.  
- Cons: None significant; the findings fit well.  

3) Pyogenic arthritis:  
- Pros: Acute pyogenic infections can cause joint effusions, inflammation, and erosions.  
- Cons: Expected to see acute inflammation signs, internal vascularity on US (though may be absent if chronic), more intense solid enhancement than peripheral. The chronic, slowly enlarging mass and lack of fever or acute signs argue against pyogenic infection.  

4) Inflammatory arthritis:  
- Pros: Synovitis and erosions can be seen in inflammatory arthritides.  
- Cons: Large well-defined extra-articular mass with peripheral enhancement is atypical; inflammatory arthritis rarely presents with large cystic collections and no solid enhancement. Also, bone marrow edema is usually present, but mass formation with the described characteristics is unlikely.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Infection by Mycobacterium tuberculosis (best fit: classic imaging features of TB arthritis, host risk factors, lesion characteristics).  
2) Infection by Mycobacterium avium (less classic imaging; possible but less likely).  
3) Pyogenic arthritis (acute clinical presentation and imaging patterns not well matched).  
4) Inflammatory arthritis (imaging and clinical picture not consistent).  

<final> Infection by Mycobacterium tuberculosis </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old male presented with headache, giddiness, disorientation, and right lower limb weakness. He had a recent history of a fall.
 Imaging Findings: Initial MRI revealed a crescent-shaped extra-axial lesion along the right frontoparietotemporal convexity, hyperintense on T1 and FLAIR-weighted sequences (Figures 1a and 1b), with mild mass effect and midline shift, suggesting a subacute subdural haematoma. A similar extra-axial collection was seen on the left side. No diffusion restriction was observed on DWI (Figure 1c). A well-defined enhancing lesion was also seen in the right lateral ventricle (persistent in serial imaging).
 
 The patient was initially managed conservatively and scheduled for follow-up. Two months later, a non-contrast CT was performed due to recurrence of symptoms, including disorientation and right lower limb weakness. CT revealed a well-defined subdural extraxial crescent-shaped collection along the cerebral convexity in the left frontoparietal region. It was causing a mass effect on the underlying cerebral parenchyma. Midline shift was noted towards the right, with left uncal herniation and dilatation of the contralateral lateral ventricle. A heterogeneous predominantly iso to hypodense lesion was seen along the left frontoparietal convexity, causing mass effect on the underlying brain parenchyma and left lateral ventricle (Figure 2a). Mild periosteal reaction along the inner table of the left parietal bone was observed (Figure 2b).
 
 No update MRI with gadolinium contrast was performed between this CT and surgery. This may have limited diagnostic clarity and contributed to incomplete preoperative planning.
 
 Intraoperatively, no haematoma was identified. Instead, thickened dura, granular periosteum and hyperostosis of underlying bone were noted, raising concern for an underlying neoplastic process (Figure 3).
 
 Postoperative contrast-enhanced MRI revealed a well-defined extra-axial lesion in the left frontoparietotemporal region. It was hyperintense on T2/FLAIR, isointense on T1, with mild diffusion restriction and heterogeneous peripheral enhancement (Figures 4a, 4b, 4c and 4d). There was also a significant mass effect on the adjacent brain parenchyma and ventricles. T1 hyperintense and T2 hypointense areas likely represented postoperative changes. A thin underlying T2 hyperintense, T1 hypointense, and non-enhancing collection were also noted.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 20-year-old male presented with worsening lower back pain for 6 months. On physical examination there was no deformity or neurological deficits noted. There was no history of fever, night sweats or weight loss.
 Imaging Findings: An anteroposterior (AP) and lateral radiograph (Figures 1a and 1b) of the lumbar spine was done initially. It showed reduced intervertebral disc space at the L2–L3 level with adjacent end plate erosions and sclerosis, and soft tissue opacity in the paravertebral region at the L2–L3 lumbar vertebral level. Loss of the psoas muscle outline was also noted.
 
 On MRI, partial destruction of the posteroinferior aspect of L1 and posterosuperior aspect of L2 is seen with involvement of the intervening IV disc (Figures 2a and 2b). Altered signal intensity is seen in the vertebral bodies of L4 and L5, which appear hypointense on T1-weighted (T1WI) and hyperintense on T2-weighted (T2WI). There is an associated extensive, multiloculated prevertebral and bilateral paravertebral collection seen (Figures 3a, 3b, 4a, and 4b). The soft tissue components appear hypointense on T1WI and hyperintense on T2W images. These show peripheral restriction of diffusion (Figures 7a and 7b). On post-contrast imaging (Figures 5a, 5b, and 6), there is enhancement of the soft tissue components with central necrotic areas and the involved parts of L1 and L2 vertebral bodies.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 68-year-old female presented to the emergency department with acute lower abdominal pain, accompanied by chronic burning sensations and stabbing pain in the right lower leg. Based on the initial clinical presentation, diverticulitis was suspected, and a routine abdominal CT scan was performed for further evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: CT imaging demonstrated a lipomatous, highly vascularised mass located posterior to the proximal femoral diaphysis of the right hip, with extension into the ischiofemoral space (Figures 1a and 1b). The lesion was predominantly composed of fatty tissue and exhibited intense arterial enhancement, consistent with a hypervascular process.
 
 Subsequent MRI offered further characterisation, identifying a complex intramuscular mass within the right quadratus femoris muscle, composed of both lipomatous (hyperintense on T1-weighted images) and nodular components (Figure 2). The nodular areas appeared hypointense on T1-weighted images and hyperintense on T2 fat-suppressed sequences (Figure 3). These regions demonstrated moderate, heterogeneous contrast enhancement, indicating variable internal vascularity (Figure 4). There were no significant signal voids on T2* sequences, effectively excluding haemorrhage or calcification. The lesion did not invade surrounding structures. However, the adjacent sciatic nerve exhibited focal thickening, which was suggestive of possible irritation due to mass effect or local inflammation (Figure 5).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 15-year-old female patient with right lower limb shortening since childhood was evaluated in consultation for limb lengthening surgery. Her personal history was negative for trauma. On physical examination, she had a clinically measured leg-length discrepancy of 4 cm.
 Imaging Findings: Full-length standing anteroposterior view radiograph (Figure 1a) demonstrated right lower limb shortening secondary to femoral hypoplasia, with normal ossification and no proximal deformity, consistent with Paley type 1A congenital femoral deficiency (CFD). Mild proximal fibular shortening was also noted, compatible with fibular hemimelia (Achterman–Kalamchi type IA).
 
 On the magnified AP radiograph of the right knee (Figure 1b), the femoral intercondylar notch appeared shallow, and the tibial spines were markedly hypoplastic.
 
 Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) showed anterior translation of the tibia relative to the femur (anterior drawer sign) and uncovering of the lateral meniscus posterior horn (Figure 2b), findings suggestive of anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) insufficiency. On mid-sagittal fat-suppressed proton-density (PD) and oblique sagittal PD-weighted images (Figures 2c and 2d), the ACL was not visualised. The posterior cruciate ligament (PCL) was present and vertically oriented (positive PCL line sign). No bone marrow oedema was seen, and the menisci were unremarkable.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 57-year-old diabetic man, with 25 years of bilateral lower limb weakness, presented with a 1.5-month history of a mildly painful, firm, non-tender swelling over the left shoulder. Limited shoulder movement, reduced sensation, and a scar noted (Figures 1a and 1b). No trauma or aspiration records.
 Imaging Findings: X-ray of the left shoulder (Figure 2) showed joint destruction, including humeral head and glenoid resorption, widened joint space, periarticular calcifications, and soft tissue inflammation.
 
 MRI of the left shoulder (Figures 4, 5, 6, 7a, and 7b) revealed extensive bony damage, complete tears in the rotator cuff tendons and complete destruction of the glenoid labrum, glenohumeral ligaments, and biceps-labral anchor. A large, thick-walled fluid collection with peripheral enhancement extended into the intermuscular and intramuscular planes of the rotator cuff muscles.
 
 MRI of the spine (Figures 3a and 3b) showed a syrinx extending from the cervico-medullary junction to the thoracic vertebra at T11, consistent with syringomyelia, and a wedge collapse of the vertebral body of T12 thoracic vertebra, indicating chronic vertebral involvement.

A) Key findings:  
- 57-year-old diabetic man with long-standing (25 years) bilateral lower limb weakness.  
- Recent (1.5 months) swelling over left shoulder, mildly painful, firm, non-tender.  
- Limited shoulder movement, reduced sensation, scar.

- MRI spine shows syrinx from cervico-medullary junction to T11 (syringomyelia) and wedge collapse of T12 vertebral body (chronic vertebral involvement).  

B) Candidate pass/fail with 1–2 pros/cons:  
1) Gorham–Stout disease  
   Pros: Can cause massive osteolysis and bone resorption.  
   Cons: Typically occurs in younger patients, not usually associated with joint destruction, syrinx, or diabetic neuropathy; usually affects metaphyses of long bones and ribs, not commonly the shoulder; no mention of massive angiomatous tissue typical of Gorham-Stout; imaging does not mention vascular or lymphatic proliferation.  
   Pass/Fail: Fail (unlikely based on age, location, and other clinical features).  

2) Septic arthritis  
   Pros: Joint destruction, soft tissue inflammation, fluid collection with peripheral enhancement could indicate infection.  
   Cons: Chronicity of neurological deficit and syrinx not typical; diabetes can predispose to infection but long-standing neurological deficits and syringomyelia suggest neuropathic changes rather than infection etiology; lack of pain or systemic inflammatory signs makes septic arthritis less likely; syrinx is not explained.  
   Pass/Fail: Fail (inconsistent with chronic neurological disease and syrinx).  

3) Neuropathic arthropathy  
   Pros: Classic presentation in diabetics with neuropathy and syrinx causing loss of sensation and joint protection; shoulder involvement common with painless or mildly painful swelling, joint destruction, periarticular calcifications (Charcot joint), extensive bone and soft tissue destruction; involvement of rotator cuff and soft tissue inflammatory collections consistent with neuropathic joint changes; syringomyelia is a known cause of neuropathic joints.  
   Cons: None significant; findings align well.  
   Pass/Fail: Pass  

4) Milwaukee shoulder syndrome  
   Pros: Shoulder involvement in elderly women; massive rotator cuff rotator cuff calcifications and synovial proliferation.  
   Cons: Patient is male, not classical age group; usually involves hydroxyapatite deposition disease without syrinx or neuropathy; joint destruction pattern differs; syrinx not explained.  
   Pass/Fail: Fail  

5) Osteolytic tumour  
   Pros: Can cause bone destruction and soft tissue masses.  
   Cons: No mention of aggressive periosteal reaction or soft tissue masses typical of tumors; joint space widening and syrinx not explained; no focal mass seen; diabetic neuropathy and syrinx are out-of-context.  
   Pass/Fail: Fail  

C) Ranking:  
1) Neuropathic arthropathy  
2) Septic arthritis  
3) Gorham–Stout disease  
4) Milwaukee shoulder syndrome  
5) Osteolytic tumour  

The diagnosis of neuropathic arthropathy best explains the combination of chronic diabetic neuropathy, syringomyelia, severe joint destruction including resorption, widening of joint space, periarticular calcifications, fluid collections, and extensive tendon and ligamentous injury.  

</analysis>  
<final>Neuropathic arthropathy</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 3-year-old male presented with severe triventricular ventriculomegaly and cortical thinning at 35 weeks on prenatal ultrasound. At birth, he had an unusual phenotype, adducted thumbs and poor spontaneous mobility. Progressive ventriculomegaly required ventriculoperitoneal derivation (VPD). Currently, the patient has severe neurological involvement, flaccid paresis with paroxysms of rigidity and epilepsy.
 Imaging Findings: Transfontanelar US and postnatal MRI at 1 day and 1 week, respectively, show severe triventricular hydrocephaly and large massa intermedia (Figures 1a and 1b), aqueductal stenosis, enlarged quadrigeminal plate, corpus callosum agenesis, vermian hypoplasia, severe cortical thinning and diencephalic–mesencephalic junction dysplasia (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d).
 
 The latest MRI, at the age of 3 years, before VPD treatment and mild improvement of ventriculomegaly, demonstrates better visualisation of the previous findings (Figure 3a), in addition to diffuse hypoplasia of cerebral white matter, polymicrogyria (Figures 3b and 3c), diencephalosynapsis (Figure 3d) with diencephalic–mesencephalic junction dysplasia in a so-called “butterfly-like pattern” (Figures 3e and 3f), and hypoplastic and poorly differentiated hippocampi.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 3-year-old male presented with severe triventricular ventriculomegaly and cortical thinning at 35 weeks on prenatal ultrasound. At birth, he had an unusual phenotype, adducted thumbs and poor spontaneous mobility. Progressive ventriculomegaly required ventriculoperitoneal derivation (VPD). Currently, the patient has severe neurological involvement, flaccid paresis with paroxysms of rigidity and epilepsy.

The latest MRI, at the age of 3 years, before VPD treatment and mild improvement of ventriculomegaly, demonstrates better visualisation of the previous findings, in addition to diffuse hypoplasia of cerebral white matter, polymicrogyria, diencephalosynapsis with diencephalic–mesencephalic junction dysplasia in a so-called “butterfly-like pattern”, and hypoplastic and poorly differentiated hippocampi.

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key Findings:
- 3-year-old male with severe triventricular hydrocephalus, cortical thinning, and severe neurological deficits.
- Agenesis of corpus callosum, severe vermian hypoplasia and diencephalosynapsis.
- Enlarged quadrigeminal plate and aqueductal stenosis.
- Polymicrogyria, diffuse cerebral white matter hypoplasia, and poorly differentiated hippocampi.
- Diencephalic–mesencephalic junction dysplasia with a "butterfly-like pattern."
- Thickened and maloriented massa intermedia.
- Patient presents with flaccid paresis, rigidity, and epilepsy from birth.

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) Rhombencephalosynapsis
   - Pros: Vermian hypoplasia/dysgenesis and diencephalic-mesencephalic junction dysplasia align with rhombencephalon anomalies.
   - Cons: Extensive cortical and white matter abnormalities less typical; ventriculomegaly and complex dysgenesis not classic.
2) MASA syndrome
   - Pros: Agenesis of corpus callosum, severe neurological deficits.
   - Cons: Classic features missing (e.g., MASA's specific L1CAM gene defects, ocular anomalies, variable ventricle size).
3) Chiari type II malformation
   - Pros: Hydrocephalus and vermian abnormalities.
   - Cons: Typically associated with spinal dysraphisms (e.g., meningomyelocele) and distinct hindbrain findings (e.g., tectal beaking, elongated fourth ventricle) not described here.
4) Tubulinopathy
   - Pros: Extensive anomalies including aqueductal stenosis, polymicrogyria, severe cortical thinning, malformation of the white matter, diencephalic and mesencephalic brainstem dysgenesis; typical tubulinopathy spectrum.
   - Cons: None significant; fits the phenotype.
5) α-dystroglycanopathies and Walker-Warburg syndrome
   - Pros: Polymicrogyria, severe brainstem and cerebellar malformations, hydrocephalus.
   - Cons: Typically have eye anomalies and muscle disease, which were not reported; also less common to have diencephalic-mesencephalic junction dysplasia.
6) Acquired causes of congenital aqueductal stenosis
   - Pros: Aqueductal stenosis and hydrocephalus.
   - Cons: Not consistent with congenital widespread cortical and diencephalic anomalies; no history of infection or hemorrhage; many additional findings cannot be explained.

C) Ranking and justification:
1) Tubulinopathy – Best fit with diffuse cortical thinning, corpus callosum agenesis, vermian hypoplasia, aqueductal stenosis, polymicrogyria, and deep white matter hypoplasia, along with diencephalic-mesencephalic dysplasia and butterfly pattern.
2) Rhombencephalosynapsis – Some overlap with vermian hypoplasia but lacks the extensive supratentorial brain abnormalities.
3) α-dystroglycanopathies / Walker-Warburg syndrome – Overlap with polymicrogyria and brainstem abnormalities but lacks systemic features.
4) MASA syndrome – Inconsistent with aqueductal stenosis and profound cortical-thinning phenotype.
5) Chiari type II – Inconsistent with deep brain anomalies and lack of spinal dysraphism.
6) Acquired causes – Inconsistent with congenital malformations.

Result: Tubulinopathy best explains the entire constellation of findings.
</analysis>

<final>Tubulinopathy</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old male with epigastric pain primarily localised to the right upper quadrant, a positive Murphy’s sign, associated with vomiting and hypotension. The patient presented with a fever spike of 38.1ºC as the only infectious clinical sign observed. Among the analytical parameters, there is a marked elevation of C-reactive protein (CRP; 12.8 mg/dL), procalcitonin (29.8 ng/mL), lactate dehydrogenase (LDH; 2488 U/L), and leukocytosis (24×10^9/L) with neutrophilia (18.9×10^9/L). The family reports diarrhoea in the preceding days.
 Imaging Findings: In a portable chest x-ray, a small left pleural effusion and the presence of gas in the right upper quadrant are seen (Figure 1).
 
 An abdominopelvic CT scan was performed with intravenous contrast in the arterial and portal venous phases. The main findings include numerous subcentimeter gas bubbles arranged in two clusters, primarily affecting the right liver lobe, with maximum axial diameters of 80 mm and 69 mm. Free gas bubbles within the parenchyma and perfusion abnormalities in the affected segments are also observed, along with predominantly central periportal oedema (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 3).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 22-year-old female presented with hallux valgus deformity of both first toes since birth (Figure 1). At the age of 12, she began experiencing difficulty walking and progressive joint stiffness. On physical examination, there was an inability to fully open the temporomandibular joints bilaterally and restricted flexion and abduction of the extremities.
 Imaging Findings: On plain radiographs of the feet, there was absence of the distal phalanges of the first toes and hallux valgus with metatarsophalangeal angles >15°, supporting early skeletal deformities (Figure 2). CT imaging of the cervicothoracic spine demonstrated heterotopic ossification of the supraspinous and posterior nuchal ligaments (Figure 3). Three-dimensional reconstructions showed multiple bridging ossifications forming tubular bone between the iliac bones and the twelfth ribs, as well as ossified extensions from the right iliac bone to the right humerus (Figures 4a and 4b).
PostDescription:

 Imaging Findings: On plain radiographs of the feet, there was absence of the distal phalanges of the first toes and hallux valgus with metatarsophalangeal angles >15°, supporting early skeletal deformities. CT imaging of the cervicothoracic spine demonstrated heterotopic ossification of the supraspinous and posterior nuchal ligaments. Three-dimensional reconstructions showed multiple bridging ossifications forming tubular bone between the iliac bones and the twelfth ribs, as well as ossified extensions from the right iliac bone to the right humerus.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis (DISH), Progressive fibrodysplasia ossificans, Ankylosing spondylitis
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Clinical history of bilateral hallux valgus deformity since birth, missing distal phalanges of the first toes, early skeletal deformities.  
- Progressive joint stiffness and difficulty walking from age 12, with temporomandibular joint involvement, restricted flexion and abduction of extremities.  
- Imaging findings include extensive heterotopic ossification in supraspinatus and posterior nuchal ligaments, multiple bridging ossifications between iliac bones and ribs, and ossified extensions from iliac bone to humerus.  
B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis (DISH):  
   - Pros: Hyperostosis usually presents with flowing ossifications along anterolateral aspects of vertebrae; possible ligamentous ossifications.  
   - Cons: DISH typically does not involve heterotopic ossifications across joints or extra-skeletal tissue; does not explain missing distal phalanges or severe congenital deformities.  
2) Progressive fibrodysplasia ossificans (FOP):  
   - Pros: Classic with progressive heterotopic ossification in soft tissues, formation of bone bridging joints, especially in the back and pelvis; restricted joint movement due to ossifications; characteristic involvement of cervical spine and temporomandibular joints; hallux valgus deformities and missing distal phalanges are typical hallmarks; onset often in childhood.  
   - Cons: None significant given the findings.  
3) Ankylosing spondylitis:  
   - Pros: Can cause ossification of spinal ligaments and joints.  
   - Cons: Typically involves sacroiliac joints and spine with syndesmophyte formation; does not present with hallux valgus or heterotopic ossifications involving extremities or soft tissues; congenital skeletal anomalies rare.  
C) Ranking and final decision:  
- FOP is highly specific given the congenital hallux deformity, progressive heterotopic ossifications including across joints, restricted joint motion, and cervical spine ligament ossifications. This is pathognomonic and matches classic descriptions.  
- DISH and ankylosing spondylitis do not account for hallux deformities or heterotopic ossifications in soft tissues beyond typical spinal involvement.  
Result: "Progressive fibrodysplasia ossificans"  
</analysis><final>  
Progressive fibrodysplasia ossificans  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 19-year-old patient with no notable personal or family medical history. The patient has been complaining for the past 3 years of bilateral gonalgia, right shoulder pain, and rachialgia, with more significant functional discomfort in the lower limbs. Clinical examination reveals tenderness upon palpation of the knees and right shoulder.
 Imaging Findings: A radiological assessment was performed (x-rays and full-body scan) revealing multiple exostotic lesions of the dorsolumbar spine, sternoclavicular joints, costal arches, scapulas, as well as in bones of the upper limbs (bilaterally, more marked at the metaphyseal-epiphyseal region of the right proximal humerus), pelvic girdle, and bones of the lower limbs and feet (bilaterally, more marked at the knees).
